SALT LAKE CITY,	JUNE 15, 1885.


SOME AFFAIRS IN ENGLAND.

	Gladstone’s resignation of the position next to
the Queen herself must have been a surprise to
the English people, who look with such pride and
admiration upon all places of honor near the
throne, and especially upon any one who is in the
confidence of her Royal Highness. This move-
ment will be likely to bring about serious conse-
quences, although, perhaps the Marquis of Salis-
bury, whom the Queen immediately summoned to
her assistance to aid in arranging matters with a
view to creating a new cabinet, may manage the
present crisis of affairs so diplomatically that the
people will scarcely realize the radical change that
is being brought about, for England has an adap-
tation in matters of State, whereby very grave
questions are adjusted with such nicety and pre-
cision that the great world scarcely heeds it more
than a ripple on a summer sea. But the policy of
the incoming party is likely to be so widely differ-
ent from the tenor of Gladstone’s administration
that, though the cabinet may come in with the
passiveness of the lamb, ere long there is sure to
be the roaring of lions, and it will disturb the
peaceful equanimity of those who have been ac-
customed to submit gracefully to liberal rule.
	Things have gone on so smoothly in England
during the reign of Queen Victoria, that the pres-
ent generation know very little of what actual
political disturbances in the United Kingdom
really are. But from the premonitions of the
times one cannot help anticipating difficulties that
will cause disturbances in the very heart of this
powerful empire.
	It is said the Queen has not been informed on
all matters on which Gladstone has acted in repre-
senting the government, and she is delighted with
his resignation. To exult in the downfall of any
one is not becoming, even in a queen, and in this
instance, in consequence of the complication of
questions that are already arising from this change,
Her Majesty may have an experience more try-
ing than the indifference of Gladstone to Royal
etiquette.
	If the attention is given to foreign policy to the
neglect of matters in the domain of England and
Scotland, etc., it will prove more or less disas-
trous to the government, and this seems to be the
present status of the affair. The masses of the
people do not care so much for foreign interests as
they do for bettering their own condition, and
they are not likely, with the enlightenment of the
nineteenth century, to be satisfied without due
attention to the questions pending in regard to
home affairs.
	The course the Queen is pursuing in not invit-
ing any of the royal family of Germany to the
wedding of the Princess Beatrice  will certainly
cause an unpleasantness that may not be so easily
conciliated, for these unobserved points of royal
etiquette are sometimes greater offenses than
things vastly more important.
	Germany is a great nation, and England can

			[Column 2]
carcely afford to overlook her even in matters of
etiquette, and especially on the occasion of a
royal marriage such as that of the Princess Bea-
trice to Prince Henry of Battenberg. It is said
too, that instead of being a “quiet” wedding, such
as had been at first intended, there is to be a great
deal of pomp and ceremony, and an elaborate and
artistic bride’s cake, built up in terraces and
placed upon a pedestal of gold. The weight
of the cake will be two hundred and fifty
pounds.
	Queen Victoria has settled the difficulty which
existed between Her Majesty and her son-in-law 
the Grand Duke of Hesse Darmstadt, which orig-
inated on account of his morganatic marriage to
Madame Kalomine. Report says previous to the
discovery of this secret marriage the Prince was
a suitor for the hand of the Princess Beatrice,and
a marriage was anticipated between the two as
soon as Parliament passed the bill permitting the
marriage to a deceased wife’s sister. This was of
course broken off as soon as the Queen discovered
his relationship to Madame Kalomine, The an-
nouncement has recently been made of his mar-
riage with the Princess Hilda of Hanault-Dessau
wdiich is soon to take place.


A EEW REFLECTIONS.

	The condition of affairs in Utah is being com-
mented upon at the present time all over the civil-
ized world. Yet one finds there are many very
ignorant of what is believed in by this people, as
well as their practices and institutions. This cru-
sade is spreading the “glad tidings” at home and
abroad, and opening up fields for the Elders to
glean in, where they may find many sincere and
honest-hearted, who will  hear the sound of
the Gospel of the Son of God, and obey the
same.
	There are many things to be thankful for, and it
becomes the Latter-day Saints, as God’s peculiar
people, to acknowledge the hand of the Almighty
in all things, even in this persecution.  “God
moves in a mysterious way,”and He knows how to
deal with His people.
	Among the things which the Saints should re-
cognize as from God is the fact that Judge Zane,
in his rulings, places plural marriage on such a
high plane. He makes it entirely separate and
distinct from any sexual sin, and distinctly takes
the ground that plural marriages are religious in
their form. The commissioners have also affirmed,
in their latest report, that plural marriage is as
much a religious principle as baptism.
	Then why should any one intefere with it, while
those who are practicing it are themselves satis-
fied with its conditions and responsibilites. Why
not allow the experiment to be made, at least; as
there are so many failures in social life, as well as
in politics and religion, let the "Mormons" try
this social method, which they have introduced,
and wait a few years more for the result.
	The Savior said, "By their fruits ye shall know
them," and assuredly, so far, judging of the fruits
of these celestial marriages, there has been abun-
dant proof that the offspring is of good guality.
The beautiful and bright children, who fill these
mountain vales and congregate in the Sunday
Schools and district schools, speak volumes to
those in doubt as to the physical and mental de-
velopment of children born in plural marriage.
	But Satan is angry because the kingdom of
God is increasing in power and in numbers, and
he is determined to overthrow it, and he works
upon the minds and hearts of men to break down
and destroy this system of marriage, through
which so many souls may find tabernacles in the
flesh, and instead of passing laws to slay the
children, as in the days of Pharoah, he causes
men to pass laws to prevent their being born in
the "marriage relation," but God sought out a way


to bring to pass His own wise purposes then, and
He is just as able now.
	Men cannot compass the ways of God nor hin-
der the growth of His work on the earth, though
they may persecute and afflict His Saints, and
even shed the blood of prophets and men of God.


EDITORIAL NOTES.

	SISTER Eliza R. S. Smith has been absent for
a few days in Brigham City on business.
	WE are very grateful to Sister Mary Gray of
the 15th Ward for a basket of delicious, ripe, red
cherries, which were fully appreciated.
	SISTERS M. I. Horne and E. Howard went to
Grantsville on Friday, June 12th, to attend the
sisters’ conference of Tooele Stake, held there
Saturday and Sunday, and to visit some of the
settlements, before returning home. 
	BELVA A. LOCKWOOD is expected to lecture in
the theatre, in this city, sometime in the near fu-
ture. Due notice will be given of the date fixed
upon. The sisters will remember that she has
spoken in Washington several times in defense of
the rights of the women of Utah.
	FROM Mrs. Sarah Sears of the 11th Ward, a
beautiful and large bouquet of the most fragrant
and choicest flowers to grace our sanctum. Roses
of several varieties and colors, geraniums, honey-
suckles, carnation pinks, and a great many others
equally charming, arranged with artistic taste to
please the eye and shed around a sweet perfume.
Many sincere thanks to the giver.
	THE Presidents of Stakes of the Relief Society
who have not received the charts containing the
names of all the Presidents of the Relief Society
in all the branches in the several stakes, can get
the number to which they are entitled by applying,
either personally, or by letter, at the office of
WOMAN'S EXPONENT, Room No. 4, Hooper and
Eldredge Block, Main Street, a few doors south
of Z. C. M. I.
	WE acknowledge, with thanks, from the office
of the Juvenile Instructor, a book of 118 pages,
entitled "The Edmunds Law." Unlawful cohabita-
tion as defined by Chief Justice Chas. S. Zane, of
the Territory of Utah, in the trial of Angus M.
Cannon Esq., in the Third District Court, Salt
Lake City, April 27, 28 and 29, 1885. Reported
in shorthand by John Irvine. Price, 30 cts.
This book gives every detail of this remarkable
trial in full. It is upon a subject which the peo-
ple here ought certainly to understand in all its
bearings and effects, and to be familiar with the
manner in which the question is treated by the
lawyers and jndges who handle these matters in
the courts of this Territory. It will be a useful
book for reference and ought to find ready sale.
	A GREAT number of strangers and tourists are
visiting our beautiful city the present season, and
many of them make special inquiries into the doc-
trines of the Mormon people, and are desirous to
learn what effect is being produced by the enforce-
ment of the Edmunds law, and whether it is work-
ing a hardship for the people. Perhaps the most 
noted man who has visited Salt Lake City this
season was Monsignur Capel, who delivered two
lectures here and preached in the Catholic Church
on the 7th and 15th of June. He is one of the strong
pillars of that ancient church, and prides himself
in being one of its devoted representatives. In
appearance he is very distinguished; in manners,
polished in the extreme; in conversation, ex-
ceedingly pleasant and fascinating. His language
is the perfection of English, and his great knowl-
edge of books, of men and countries, make
him exceptionally brilliant in the art of convers-
ing. He has quite a collection of "Mormon books,
and his opinions on the subject may be different
after he has read them; at any rate he is a strong


opponent of its doctrines at the present time. He
" intends returning here after a'visit’of about,two
'-weeks in California, and deliver a course of lec-
tures.
	FRIDAY morning, May 15th, attended the an-
nual meeting of the Primary Association of the
Tenth Ward in this city, commencing at 10 a.m.,
Mrs. Jennie McLean presiding. The singing of
the children and their exercises were
excellent, and the best of order was strictly
observed. Sister McLean, who has presided
over the Society ever since its organization, has,
with her assitants, accomplished great good, more
especially in the spiritual training of the little ones
in the cultivation of faith and the fruits of the
Holy Spirit. The annual report, which was read,
showed that considerable had been done during
the year, even by these little ones. After the pro-
gramme1 had been carried out remarks, suitable to
- tbe occasion,were made by Sisters E. C. Clawson,
E. B. Wells, L. A. Wells, Coun. James Woods,
Sister McLean and her Coun., Miss Edwards.,
At the close of the meeting, the officers, visitors
and children repaired to Fuller’s Hill, wherea ll
partook of refreshments prepared for the occa-
sion, and enjoyed the afternoon in a pleasant
manner, the children indulging in sports and pas-
times, such as swinging, boating, examining the
views and other amusements.  It was an event in
the, lives of tlie little folks which they will look
back upon with a sweet remembrance.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


R. S. AND Y. L. MEETING.

	The ladies regular semi-monthly meeting was
held in the Fourteenth Ward Assembly Rooms,
May 30th, 1885, Mrs. E. S. Taylor presiding.
Minutes were read from the Y. L. M. I. A. of the
17th Ward.
	Remarks were made by Sister E. S. Taylor on
the spirit and peculiarity of the times, and testi-
monies were borne by Sisters Smith, Green, Dyer,
Watmough and other sisters in the congregation,
and remarks were made by Sisters M. E. Kimball
and Julia C. Howe.
	Counselor B. W. Smith made closing remarks,
in which she gave a sketch of her visit to the
penitentiary, and spoke upon other subjects of in-
terest.  Meeting adjourned.  Singing, Doxology.
Prayer by Sister E. S. Taylor.

	Meeting held at the same time and place, Satur-
day, June 13th, Counselor S. M. Kimball presid-
ing.  Mrs. M. A. Wilcox was elected Secretary
pro tem.  Minutes of previous meeting were read,
and also minutes of Y. L. from 20th and 2nd
Wards, and P. A. from 21st Ward.  The meeting,
which was numerously attended, was then ad-
dressed by Sisters M. A. Freeze, L. W. Kimball,
P. W. Woodruff, B. W. Smith, Dr. E. S. Barney,
Mary Gerber, H. Risley, S. M. Kimball, H, T.
King, E. B. Wells and M. A. E. Watmohgh.
The subjects spoken upon were the signs of the
times, the necessity of being humble, faithful and
prayerful, officiating for the dead in temples, the
present crusade, the Deseret Hospital, the train-
ing of children and some items of business in
relation to the Relief Society.
	A caution was given to the officers of the Re-
lief Society in regard to agents for books and
other things, not to place the Society in any
way under obligations to them, and in purchas-
ing books and other things, or accepting any
agency for these things to take every precaution
to secure themselves against being imposed upon.
	The appointment for the Relief Society Confer-
ence of this Stake of Zion was given out to be 
held on Friday, June 26th, and the Primary June
27th, in the forenoon, at 10 a.m., and Y. L. M. I.
A. at 2 p.m. Singing, "Lord dismiss us," etc.
Prayer by Sister M. W. Wilcox.


[Column 1]
﻿	SKETCH OF THE F. R. SOCIETY.
	The Female Relief Society was organized by
the Prophet Joseph Smith, in Nauvoo, Illi-
nois, March 17th, 1842. Willard Richards and
John Taylor were also present on the occasion, Wil-
lard Richards acting as secretary pro tem.  Here
is a minute from the Prophet’s own history.
	March 17th. “I attended, by request, the F. R.
S., whose object is the relief of the poor, the des-
titute, the widow and the orphan, and for the exer-
cise of all benevolent purposes. Its organization
was completed this day, Mrs. Emma Smith,
President; Mrs. Elizabeth Ann Whitney and Mrs.
Sarah M. Cleveland, Counselors; Miss E. R.
Snow, Secretary; Miss Sarah Wheeler, Assistant
Secretary; Miss Elvira Cole, Treasurer. The
brethren then laid their hands on them and or-
dained them to their several offices.”  The
Prophet said, "You are now organized after the
pattern of the Church, and that whenever the
Church of Jesus Christ was fully organized upon
the earth this organization of the sisters existed.”
On another occasion, April 28th, 1842, he says, “I
met the members of the F. R. S.,and after presid-
ing at the admission of many new members, gave
a lecture on the Priesthood, showing how the
sisters would come in possession of the privileges,
gifts and blessings of the Priesthood, and that the
signs should follow them, such as healing the sick,
casting out devils, etc., and that they might attain,
unto these blessings by a virtuous life and conver-
sation, and diligence in keeping all the command-
ments. This is a charitable institution, and ac-
cording to your nature. It is natural for females
to have feelings of charity and benevolence. You
are now placed in a situation where you can act
according to those sympathies God has placed in
your bosoms. If you live up to these privileges
how great and glorious will be your reward in the
celestial kingdom. If you live up to your privi-,
leges the angels cannot be restrained from being
your associates. Females, if they are pure and
innocent, can come into the presence of God, for
what is more pleasing to God than innocene. You
must be innocent or you cannot come up before
Him. If we would come before God we must
Weep ourselves pure as He is pure.”
	He spoke of some little foolish things being
circulated in the Society against some of the sis-
ters not doing right in laying on hands to heal the
sick; said that if the people had common sym-
pathies they would rejoice that the sick could be
healed.
	He continued his subject by quoting the com-
mission, given to the ancient apostles, in Mark
xvi, "Go ye into all the world and preach the Gos-
pel to every creature. He that believeth and is
baptised shall be saved; and he that believeth not
shall be damned; and these signs shall follow
them that believe: In my name they shall cast out
devils; they shall speak with new tongues; they
shall take up serpents, and if they drink any dead-
ly thing, it shall not hurt them; they shall lay
hands on the sick and they shall recover.”
	"No matter who believeth, these signs, such as
healing the sick, casting out devils etc., should
follow all that believe, whether male or female,
and if the sisters should have faith to heal the
sick, let all hold their tongues.” He further re-
marked that there could be no devil in it if God
gave His sanction by healing, and there could be
no more sin in any female laying hands on and
praying for the sick than in wetting their face with
water.
	This was about two years before his martyrdom,
which sad event took place June 27th, 1844.
	In 1846 the Church left Nauvoo, consequently
the F. R. S. was not in operation until the time
it was reorganized in Salt Lake City by President
Brigham Young. He gave Sister. E. R. Snow a
mission to assist the bishops to organize a F. R.
S. in every ward in the city. Commencing at the
[Coumn 2]
15 Ward, Sarah M Kimball was made President,
with two Counselors, Secretary and Treasurer,
also a visiting committee, who were to go from
house to house to search out the poor and look
after their wants.
	The Society has extended its branches from
ward to ward of the city and from settlement to
settlement in the country, throughout the Terri-
tory, United States,Great Britain, and throughout
the world where there is a branch of the Church.
The funds of the Society are mostly donations.
Many of the branches of the Society have started
industries, from which they realize a moderate in-
come; besides stated business meetings, each
branch has set days on which to work for the
benefit of the poor.
	When the Society commenced its labors in
Salt Lake City the donations consisted of mate-
rial for patchwork quilts,rags for carpets,uncarded
wool, for socks and stockings, etc.  In some in-
stances the hair of animals was cleansed and
carded by hand, of course, as there were no card-
ing machines in the city at that time, spun and
knit into socks and mittens.
	As fast as may be the various branches are
building for themselves places of meeting, work-
shops. etc.  The first of these buildings was
erected in the 15th Ward. They commenced
their labors as above, their first capital stock be-
ng donations of pieces for quilts, carpet rags, etc.
By energy and perseverance they helped to sus-
tain their poor, and in a few years purchased land
and built on it a commodious house.
	The laying of the corner stone was performed
by the sisters, and was followed by appropriate
speech making on the part of the presiding officers
of the Society—Miss S. M. Kimball, Mrs. E. R.
Snow and others, each in their turn mounting the
corner stone for a rostrum, and each winning de-
served applause from the assembled throng.
	After the building was completed it was dedi-
cated by the First Presidency. Sister E. R.
Snow composed a hymn for the occasion. Since
its reorganization here the Relief Society had
gathered and disbursed over $100,000 eight years
ago. Probably this sum has doubled since that
time.
	The Relief Society in the 13th Ward, in which
I live, was organized April 18th, 1868, at the resi-
dence of Bishop E. D. Wooley, who was present
with Counselors Wm. S. Godbe and F. A.
Mitchell. After a few interesting remarks from
the Bishop with regard to the organization and the
duties of its members, he proceeded to set apart
Mrs. Rachel R. Grant,as President of the society;
the Bishop himself ordained her to that office.
Mrs. Grant was then requested to choose her
Counselors, when she selected Mrs. Annie T.
Godbe for her First Counselor and Mrs. Margaret
Mitchell for her Second Counselor.  Mrs. Godbe
was ordained by her husband, Counselor Wm. S.
Godbe, and Mrs. Mitchell by her husband, Coun-
selor F.A. Mitchell.  It was then moved that Mrs.
E. H. Goddard act as Secretary, and Mrs. Anna
Musser was chosen as Treasurer. All were re-
ceived by unanimous vote. The following persons
were then appointed as teachers.  Mrs. Clarrissa
Scofield, Rosina Godbe, Martha Wells, Abbie
Rumel, Emily Young, Louisa Spencer, Agnes
Park, Mary Musser, Catherine Horrecks, Martha
Stringam, Bathsheba W. Smith, Phebe Maiben,
Elizabeth Maiben and Isabella Anderson.
	After a few encouraging and appropriate re-
marks from Sister E. R. Snow the meeting was
adjourned till April 30, 1868, to meet at Mrs.
Mary Godbe’s residence at 2 p.m. Counselor
Wm. S. Godbe acted as Secretary pro tem at the
organization.
	On May 3rd, 1871 Mrs. Mary-Musser was ap-
pointed Treasurer after, the decease of Mrs. Anna
L. Musser, and Mrs. E. B. Wells, Assistant Sec-
retary
April, 1869, Mrs. Bathsheba W. Smith was or-


[First column]
dained as First Counselor to Mrs. Rachel R.
Grant, Mrs. Annie T. Godbe having resigned her
position, and in January, 1873, Mrs. Lydia Ann
Wells was ordained as the Second Counselor in
the Relief Society in place of Mrs. Margaret
Mitchell, who had been called to accompany her
husband on a mission to the Sandwich Islands.
Mrs. Relief C. Atwood was appointed Treasurer
in 1876, Mrs. Mary Musser resigning her position
on account of removing out of the ward.
	In Sept., 1883, Mrs. B. W. Smith removed to
another ward, and soon after resigned her posi-
tion as First Counselor to Mrs. R. R. Grant, who
then chose Mrs. Lydia Ann Wells to occupy the
position of First Counselor, and Mrs. Louisa
Spencer to fill the office of the Second Counselor.
	Soon after our settlement in this valley I heard
President Heber C. Kimball say the time would
come when the sisters of this Church would be
organized as well as the brethren.  Before the
decease of President Brigham Young the work of
organizing the branches of the Relief Society in
a Stake capacity, with President, two Counselors,
Secretary and Treasurer, was commenced by him,
﻿and since organizations of the Relief Society have
been made in each Stake of Zion, and when new
Stakes of Zion have been built up this organiza-
tion follows the other as soon as practicable.
	On June 19th, 1880, at a conference of the Re-
lief Society, held in the Salt Lake Assembly Hall,
a central organization was formed, President
John Taylor officiating. He appointed Mrs.
Eliza R. Snow Smith to preside over all the or-
ganizations of the Relief Society in the Church
of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints in all the
world.  She chose for her Counselors Mrs. Zina
D. H. Young and Mrs. Elizabeth Ann Whitney,
and for Secretary Mrs. Sarah M. Kimball, and for
Treasurer Mrs. M. Isabella Horne.
	In a meeting held in the Fourteenth Ward As-
sembly Rooms, July 17th, 1880, President John
Taylor set apart and blessed the officers he had
appointed to preside over the Central Board of
Relief Society, thus effectually completing this
important organization.
	This Society in its several branches is auxil-
eary to the Priesthood in the various wards, and
works in harmony with the Bishops and acting
brethren of the ward.  If all are faithful and
true to their office and calling none of the poor
need suffer or want for the necessary comforts of
life, for the organization of the Church is so com-
plete and perfect in every part.
	We commenced our labors in the Society in the
Thirteenth Ward similar to that of other wards,
making quilts, rag carpets, buckskin gloves and
clothing of different kinds.  With these industries
and other sources, since April 18th, 1868, to
January 28th, 1885, we have gathered and dis-
bursed in our ward, in cash and sundries, $3,880.
30, and if we are all united we will soon have a
place to meet in of our own - a hall suitable for
the purpose.
	As a matter of record I would here state that in
1872, by the suggestion of our President, Eliza
R. Snow, and the approval of President Brigham
Young, the word Female prefixed to the name of
the Society, was dropped.  This was done by
vote in all the several branches of the organiza-
tion.
	In 1870 the Legislature of Utah gave to the
women of this Territory the right of suffrage.
	In 1872 the WOMAN'S EXPONENT was estab-
lished, the need of a woman's paper having been
agitated among the sisters for some time, by
means of which the women of Utah might repre-
sent themselves and tell their own story.  The
first number was issued June 1st, 1872, edited
by Miss Louisa L. Greene.  
	In establishing home industries of a public
character the sisters have labored earnestly and
diligently.  In 1874 the Deseret Silk Association
was organized, Mrs. Zina D. H. Young, Presi-
dent.

[Second column]
	Millinery stores, straw manufacture and co-op-
erative stores of this kind, and also for fancy arti-
cles, and home-manufactured goods have, from
time to time, been very successfully manipulated
by the sisters of the Relief Society.
	In 1876, at the time of the Centennial Exhibi-
tion in Philadelphia, a Woman's Fair was held in
the Old Constitution Building in this city, over
which Miss Eliza R. Snow presided, having
received an official appointment from the
Centennial Board in Philadelphia.  This fair
gave the women of Utah an excellent opportunity
of displaying their articles of handiwork of all
kinds and sorts imaginable, and it was visited by
thousands of people who called at Salt Lake in
traveling to and from the Centennial, as well as
many from foreign countries, whose route lay
across the continent.
	In October, 1876, President Young called upon
the sisters through the WOMAN'S EXPONENT to
lay up grain against a day of famine, and on the
11th of November following Mrs. E. B. Wells was
chosen President of the Grain Association.  At
the same meeting measures were taken to publish
a book called "The Women of Mormondom."
The Relief Society in the branches throughout
the Stakes of Zion have been engaged from year
to year ever since in the storing of wheat, and
many of them have built store-houses of their own,
in which to preserve it.
	Thousands of bushels have, since that time,
been stored away by the sisters, and they look up-
on it as one of the important missions given them
to perform.
	In May, 1882, the sisters formed an association
for the establishment of a hospital for the sick
and afflicted, and by unanimous vote Sister Eliza
R. Snow Smith was chosen President.  The Deseret
Hospital was dedicated July 17th, 1882, President
John Taylor and other leading brethren officiat-
ing.
	The labors of the Women of Zion in an organ-
ized capacity are very effective for good, and are
of great benefit to the people, and here it might
be well also to mention that Young Ladies, Asso-
ciations, for mutual improvement, have been or-
ganized in all the Stakes of Zion in each ward,
commencing in 1867, by the advice and authority
of Brigham Young.  There are also what are
called Primary Associations for the children, un-
der the management of the sisters; these are also
organized in each bishop's ward, and are of great
benefit in teaching and instructing the little ones
in the principles of the Gospel, as well as impart-
ing to them the Spirit of the Lord in their young
and tender years.
	From this brief sketch of the Relief Society
and public labors of the women of the Latter-day
Saints, it is easy to see, judging from the fact that
a wide field of usefulness is open for the next
generation, that great results may be anticipated,
if we are wise, diligent and faithful in the perform-
ance of every duty, and in keeping the command-
ments of God.
						R. C. ATWOOD
[short line break]
	In connection with the article above we pub-
lish the following from the American Phrenologi-
cal Journal, New York issued February 1869.

		WOMAN'S SPHERE IN UTAH.

	The women of Salt Lake City, as will be seen
by the following communication, are not disposed
to be left behind in the race of improvement by
their Eastern sisters.  They feel the importance
of extending their sphere of labor and of useful-
ness, and of making for themselves room in the
world for the more complete exercise and de-
velopment of all their faculties.  The object of the
"Female Relief Society" is certainly a good one,
and we have no doubt but that the women who
have the work in hand will make it a means of
good not only to those who may receive its help
in time of need, but to themselves also, in the

[Third column]
promption of their own intellectual, social, and
moral improvement.
	A valued lady correspondent of Salt Lake City,
under date of November 13th, 1868, writes as
follows:

		SALT LAKE CITY, 13th November, 1868.
	The laying of the corner-stone of a "Temple
of Commerce," by the Female Relief Society of
the Fifteenth Ward, Salt Lake City, took place
yesterday.  The novelty consists in its being a
female enterprise developed under the fostering
care of Bishop R. T. Burton.  A large audience,
composed in part of members of the Society, was
on the ground.  At 2 p.m., after the usual form
on such occasions, the following address was
read by the president, followed by an extempore
speech by E. R. Snow on Woman's Relations to
the Sterner Sex; a speech by Bishop Burton,
commendatory and encouraging, and one by Mrs.
Bathsheba Smith appropriate to the occasion.
						MRS. S. M. K. 
			ADDRESS.
GENTLEMEND AND LADIES:
	"I appear before you on this interesting occasion
in behalf of the Female Relief Society, to express
thanks to Almighty God that the wheels of pro-
gress have been permitted to run until they have
brought us to a more extended field of useful
labor for female minds and hands.
	It will be readily admitted that woman's allotted
sphere of labor is not sufficiently extensive and
varied to enable her to exercise all her God-given
powers and faculties in the manner best calculated
to strengthen, and develop, and perfect her; nor
are her labors made sufficiently remunerative to
insure her that independence essential to true
womanly dignity.
	We realize that unless wisely conducted, our
practical operations may subject us to criticism
and censure.  But the consciousness that our
theory is correct, and our efforts in the direction
of human improvement and universal good, will
strengthen and encourage us, bestowing that
boon--
		"Which nothing earthly gives or can destroy,
		The soul's calm sunshine and the heartfelt joy."
	With feelings of humility and gratitude I stand
upon this consecrated rock, and contemplate the
anticipated result of the completion of this un-
pretending edifice (which I will here call "Our
Store"), the upper story of which will be dedicat-
ed to art and to science; the lower story to com-
merce or trade.  I view this as a stepping-stone
to similar enterprises on a grander scale.
	The object of the building is to enable the mem-
bers of the Society more perfectly to combine their
labor, their means, their tastes, and their talents
for improvement, physically, socially, morally, intel-
ectually, and financially, and for more extended
usefulness.
	Many gentlemen kindly proffer their aid in
forwarding this enterprise.  To them, in behalf
of the cause for which we labor, we extend
heartfelt thanks.
	We feel that our friends who so graciously pat-
ronize us will expect much at our hands.  We
promise you our best endeavors to meet your
highest expectations.  But we ask you merci-
fully to remember that the seat at the merchant's
counting-room table is a new one for us
to occupy, hence, as pioneers for our sex in this
department of female labor in our Territory, we
beg you not to be too severe in your criticisms,
but show your magnanimity by giving us an ap-
proving look and an occasional encouraging word.
With such helps, and the continued blessing of
God, we have all confidence that we shall be en-
abled to extend variously needed relief, and make
our labors a blessing to the cause of humanity."
	[Now this is a bold - may we not say grand? -
movement for the Salt Lake sisterhood.  With
the inevitable franchise, for which women are


asking, nay demanding, these Mormon wives and
mothers will be in the majority, and when they
vote it will fix things just as they please. We are
in favor of the movement. Let the usurping
"lords of creation" make their peace while they
may. Clear the track, for the locomotive is on
its way to Salt Lake City.]


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


EXTRACTS FROM AN IRISH 
		LETTER.
	I have just returned from a trip into the country
of Antrim. The whole country, as far as I have
seen it, is surpassingly beautiful—new changes
and never ending green and brown, with hawthorn
hedges wherever one turns, and the long, winding
roads, and wild flowers—violets, daisies, oceans
of fragrant primroses and dandelions—growing in
reckless, profusion along the banks of streams,
hiding under the hedges, and bursting into view
through every gap in the green and thorny hedge.
Then there is the buttercup and a yellow blossom
about the size of a $20 gold peice, perhaps a
trifle, smaller, shaped much like a buttercup,
which grows in dank and boggy places, and is
called the mayflower. It is the brightest of gold-
en yellow, rich in color beyond belief almost.
	We are poor indeed, in wild songsters,compared
with this country. Our robbin is nearly twice the
size of that here, and the yellow and red-breasted
little fellow here sings twice as sweet. The wren
is a little midget;the pugnacious sparrow, the lark,
the thrush, the starling, the yellow yarling, the
blue top and many others, besides the magpie and
the noisy, cawing crow. Wherever trees grow to
a considerable height, there you will find a con-
gregation of crows building their huge and clumsy
nests in the topmost boughs, and keeping up a
never ending and almost deafening cawing.
	I heard the cuckoo, the other day, for the first
time in my left. I was wandering in the evening
along a deep, shady hedge, and I thought some
one in a distant field was calling his cows, when
the bird flew swiftly above me and disappeared in
a wooded hill in the neighborhood. I have heard
it several time since,but have not been able to see
it plainly as yet.
	The town Antrim, which I visited, is a rare old-
fashioned place, and is well worth a visit, and I
have regretted deeply that I did not reach it until
evening and that I left it in the darkness of the
same night.
	It has a quaint, quiet, old fashioned look about
it that was captivating; and its old time market,
and chapel, and the high wall and castle of Lord
Masserine make it a place worth cultivating. But
more than all this, than the splendid castle and
grounds of Lord Masserine, which I could see
very plainly, though only for a moment, was a
splendid trout stream, that sweeps down the side
of the town, past the castle, and empties into a
beautiful sheet of water called the Lough Neagh.
It is a fresh water body, said to be the largest
in the United Kingdom, and is about seven miles
wide, by fourteen miles long. Though the hour
was late and the sun had already sunk, I could not
resist a walk in the twilight, which lingers so long
in this clime, and I shall never regret it.
	Leaving the town, a winding, well kept road
leads through a wild wood, with green grass and
flowers springing everywhere, to a little bay, in
which were anchored yachts and fishermen’s
yawls, while all about were nets and the appliances
of fishermen. Away out upon the water, in the
clear and lingering twilight, the little boats could
be seen resting, seemingly without motion, on the
placid, unruffled bosom of the lake. The stars be-
gan to peep, the warm tints were gradually dying
out of the heavens, the vesper song of the birds
grew fainter and fainter, as the eye reached far-
ther and farther into the darkening west.
	If tears filled those eyes,because the vision was
limited, and because, though the heart so longed
to see dear ones away, away, away in the path of


the golden sun[-] none knew it but God and the
heavens and him who stood and gazed till his
heart was full and he could not speak. W.


MRS. WELDON.

	In presenting our readers with a pen portrait of
this lady, who has been so much before the pub-
lic of late, we may be allowed to express the
opinion that she has shown herself to be possessed
of unquestionable ability, indomitable persevere-
ance, and tireless energy.
	Without entering into questions of a personal
nature, it will, perhaps, be a matter of interest to
our readers to learn that at the outset of her life,
young, beautiful, well-born, accomplished, a
vocalist of unusual excellence, Mrs Weldon was
the object of admiration and adulation of the
society in which she moved “the observed of all
observers.”
	She was, as Miss Treherne, and, subsequently
as Mrs. Weldon, the idol of a circle, including
among its numbers members of the highest rank.
	Wealthy, and without care, she pursued the
career to which ladies of her class are accustomed,
when, in the ninth year of her married life, she
was unexpectedly consulted respecting the musi-
cal education of the daughter of a Welsh coun-
try parson.
	About this time Mrs. Weldon had been fram-
ing a plan for the founding of a school for music,
and although she had never given a singing-lesson
in her life, she commenced to teach Miss Gwen-
doline Jones vocalism. Out of that act sprang
the school established by her at Tavistock House,
Russell-square, London, which for many years
was the residence of the late Charles Dickens,
and carried on for many years.
	We have not space to record the trials, rebuffs,
and we may say afflictions, Mrs. Weldon has
passed through in her attempts to fulfil what she
has conceived to be her mission, nor to recount
the various vicissitudes of her career, but we
may allude to the wonderfnl success with which
she has conducted her various cases, and to the
consummate skill she has displayed throughout,
especially in the attack she has so triumphantly
made upon the present condition of the Lunacy
Laws.
	That the state of our legislation in this respect
cries aloud for improvement, is no new story; but
perhaps, it was never better exemplified than in
the trial in which Mrs. Weldon showed how easy
 it might be, under the existing laws, for a per-
fectly sane person to be put away in a lunatic
asylum.
	The learned Judge complimented the lady on
having brought the matter forward, and it is to be
hoped the outcome may be something really bene-
ficial to the community at large, and which may
put it out of the power of one or two interested
persons to devote a perfectly sane person to a life
which one shudders to contemplate.
	If Mrs. Weldon had done nothing but
this, she would have commanded our admiration,
but she has been very active in many other ways,
and among others, has done her best to advance
the cause of vocal music, as far as lies in her
power, and for this she deserves the thanks of
every amateur, though every one may not agree
with her methods of tuition.
	At present she maybe heard “discoursing sweet
sounds” at the London Pavilion, in Piccadilly,
and it is, perhaps, a good sign of the times when
the surroundings are such that a lady of Mrs.
Weldon’s culture and intelligence does not think
it beneath her to amuse the audience of a Music
Hall.	SELECTED.


[Column 1]
THOUGHTS ONE QUIET EVENING

	Husband away and baby in dreamland, I
sit here at home musing and pondering by

[Column 2]
myself on the condition of our people, as it has
been in the past, of what it is at present, and
trying to penetrate far into the future, 
as far as my mind's eye will allow me. But let my
mind wander where it will it most invariably
will turn back to this, the great momentous
Zion. As it has been said, it is truly a great
age, and words would be inadequate to express
my great joy that I am allowed to live in this
great day, and to be of the chosen Saints of
God.
	I ask myself, as I sit here in solitude, if we 
could look far ahead into the annals of history,
see there the world's history recorded as it is
now progressing, would there not be looks of
wonder,astonishment and, oh! of horror? Horror
at seeing recorded the wicked deeds being done
now upon the earth. Noble men and women
suffering through injustice done them, because
they believe in worshiping God according to
the dictates of their own consciences; laws
twisted and turned to suit their own vile pur-
poses, that is to work against the poor degraded
Mormon people (as they are usually called)
and to bring them nearer to civilization.
	Methinks, if they could look ahead into the
records of history, the mantle of shame might
cover their cheek, that is if they were capable
of such feelings. I am thankful to my Hea-
venly Father that I do not belong to this
honorable class. 
	I wonder how these oppressors of mankind 
will feel when they stand at that great and
terrible day at the bar of judgment, and hear
their sentence pronounced, by the judge of all
the world, "Depart ye, ye cursed, into everlast-
ing fire, prepared for the devil and his angels,"
and their terrible feelings at their doom,
andthen to hear the sentence of those now
persecuted, "Come, ye blessed of my Father,"
etc.
	We can well afford to be brave, for He who
has ordained all things in His own wise way,
has ordered these trials and tribulations to
come upon us, that we may grow more perfect,
and come nearer to God; and then, if we have
these trials to contend with. we will not have 
so much time to spend on fashion and other
frivolous pastimes, that help to lower and de-
grade the mind.
	An again, when we look around us it is
really surprising, to an intelligent, thinking
mind, how the petty vice, prejudice, is ruling
the world to-day. To think, in as an enlightend
age as this, that prejudice is allowed to rule
the people to such an extent as it does in re-
gard to the Latter-day Saints. To me it carries
an odor of ignorance with it wherever it is to
be found. Some people visit us and have the
opportunity of seeing and knowing for them-
selves, and yet they are so steeped in egotism
that they will not accept of these opportuni-
ties, seemingly in fear of finding out that we,
as a people, are greatly misrepresented, or
sometimes they are so egotistical that they
know it all any way, while some that have
never been here, and know nothing but from
hearsay, help to encourage and circulate all
kinds of bad reports against us.
	One incident that happened to an aunt of
mine I will here relate. While getting ready
to come to the far west, she stepped into a
milliner store, where she was well acquainted.
to make some purchases. After the usual
greetings were over, she said, "We are all
sorry to lose such a good citizen as you are; I
am afraid, that in coming in contact with those
people, you will become as degraded as they
are." She also said, "Why, the women work
so hard, they have to do all the work out of 
doors there!" My aunt replied in her usual
calm, dignified, straight forward manner, "In-
deed! is that really so?" and the lady again re-
plied, "Yes, that is really so." Then said my
aunt, "We read of beautiful cities and villages
being laid out there, beautiful buildings erected


and in fact I have seen them. Don’t you feel
proud of our sex, to know that they have such
great capabilities?” She seemed dumbfounded;
she knew not what to say; she stuttered, stam-
mered, and finally changed the subject.
	To me this little incident goes a great way
to show that prejudice, pride and arrogance
should be put out of reach in some far away
corner,that none could handle them only those
that are doomed to be destroyed. 	IDA.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


BIRTHDAY PARTY.

	The first day of May dawned calm, bright and beauti-
ful, as many of the children of the First Ward of Richfield
hoped it would.
	There was a surprise party on hand for Grandma Hay-
ward, First Counselor in the Primary Association of said
ward. It was the seventy-first birthday of this venerable
lady.
	At two o’clock parents and children were seen wending
their way toward the Social Hall, which was nicely decora-
ted with pictures, evergreens, etc., with baskets filled with
the very nicest of picnic. A half an hour later Sister Hay-
ward was cordially escorted to the hall by her husband and
the President of the Association, Sister Nancy Nebeker.
Here she found the members of the Association, Bishop,
of the ward, her only daughter, Mrs. Wm. Baker, her
grandchildren and great-grandchildren and other friends,
anxiously waiting her arrival. On entering she was
greeted by a song of welcome, sung by a number of the
larger girls of the Association. Prayer was offered by
Bishop Paul Poulson. A short programme was gone
through with, consisting of speeches, songs recitations, etc.
Miss Lizzie Baker, Sister Hayward's granddaughter, re-
cited the following piece, which was compos[-]ed for the oc
casion. 

	Dear Grandma Hayward, “I’m much surprised,"
		We think we hear you say,
	But now we greet you one and all
		On your seventy-first birthday.

	Tis not an empty show of love
		That we now offer you,
	But a love that’s grown within ourselves,
		From hearts, most firm and true.

	We call ourselves your children,
		We listen to your voice,
	We heed your good instructions,
		And in this we do rejoice.

	We rejoice that you are with us,
		That you have lived to see
	The blessings that are realized
		In the children’s Primary.

	Accept the feeble tokens
		That are offered to you here,
	'Tis but a small endeavor,
		Which we’ve made, your heart to cheer.
	
	And let us now assure you
		Our wishes we renew,
	That many, many birthdays
		May be in store for you.

	That for many, many years to come
		You may live to guide and bless
	The members of the Primary	 
		To lives of usefulness.

	After the poem Sister Hayward was presented with a
beautiful quilt, made by the President and older members
of the Association.	
	She was almost overcome with this token of love and
respect, but expressed her desire to aid in building up, and
in the advancement of the young people of Zion.
	Bishop Poulson then encouraged the children and all
present never to tire in well doing.	
	Our company then partook of a bounteous picnic, after
which the children joined in a dance until dusk; then all
went home, thinking over what an enjoyable time they
had spent.	Respectfully,
				NANCY NEBEKER.


RESOLUTIONS OF RESPECT.

	At a meeting of the South Primary Association of
Spanish Fork, a vote was taken and carried unanimously,
that we draft some resolutions of condolence to the


memory of Simeon James, son of Simeon O. Powell and
Edith Cornaby, who departed this life on the 19th of,
May, 1885, at the early age of nine years, eleven months
and twenty-three days.
	The following is a copy of the Resolutions:

	Whereas, Providence has seen fit to call from our midst
our much beloved brother and co-laborer in our Associa-
tion,
	We, The members of the same, offer our heart-felt sym.
pathies to the bereaved parents, grandparents and friends
of the deceased.
	Although he has been taken from our midst so young,
we feel that he has been taken for a wise purpose, and that
our loss is his gain.
		AGNES R. LEWIS.
In behalf of the Association.

	Whereas, In the divine providence and wisdom of our
Heavenly Father, we have been called to mourn the loss
of our beloved sister, Hannah Jones, who departed this
life May 9th, 1885, after a lingering illness, and
	Whereas, She has been an active teacher in the Relief
Society ever since its organization, and a willing worker at
all times, and devoted to the cause of truth and righteous-
ness, and earnest in helping to move it forward, we feel it
our bounden duty to express our appreciation of her labors,
and to ever hold her memory blessed. Therefore be it
	Resolved, That we sincerely sympathize with the be-
reaved relatives and friends of the deceased, and pray
that the Heavenly Comforter may enable them to bear this
trial uncomplainingly.

	She has gone from this world of trial and care,
		To dwell with the noble and good;
	Her salvation is certain, for noble her aim,
		And true to her covenant she stood.

	Then grieve not for her, she has gone to her rest,
		The anthems of angels to swell;
	We will think of her kindly, her memory [-]s blest,
		And we know that for her—all is well.
		SUSAN GRANT, President,
		L. D. MUIR, Secretary.
Woods Cross, May 29th, 1885.


IN MEMORIAM. 

	We, the officers and members of the Fourth Ward
Y. L. M. I. A., do tender these Resolutions of Respect to
the memory of Sister Clara Erickson, who departed this
life April 30th, 1885, aged fourteen years, six months and
twenty-one days.
	Whereas, She was loved and respected by all her asso-
ciates, and,
	Whereas, She was a faithful member of the association,
therefore, be it	
	Resolved, That we esteem her faithful devotion to duty
as worthy the emulation of us all.
	Resolved, That we condole with the bereaved parents
and relatives in the loss of their dear one, not, however,
without the consoling reflection that she will come forth in
the morning of the first resurrection.
	Resolved, That a copy of these Resolutions be sent to
the bereaved fam[-]ly, that another be retained in the Asso-
cition, and that a third be sent to the EXPONENT for
publication.
						ANNA WILCOX,
	Committee,			ANNIE THACKERAY,
						LOUIE SMITH.

	LINES TO THE MEMORY OF CLARA.

Dear, faithful Clara’s left us,
	She’s mingling with the blest—
Gone from this world of sorrow
	To a happy home of rest.

She will no longer suffer pain,
	As while on earth she stayed,
And she'll gain eternal glory,
	For in God her hopes were laid.

Yes, her earthly toils are over,
	She’s free from care and pain;
She's sleeping with the blest
	'Til the Savior comes again.

Then should we mourn for her, dear friends,
	Or to despair be driven?—
For she has been a faithful child,
	And we know she is in heaven.

Though 'tis hard to part with those we love,
	And loved she's always been,


True to her parents and her friends,
	And free from every sin.

Then let us all live faithful,
	That when we sleep ‘neath the sod,
Our names may all be written	
	In the autograph of God.
		M. A. THACKERAY.
