WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

The Rights of the Women of Zion, and the Rights of the Women of all Nations.

SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, JULY 1, 1885.	No. 3.


ODE TO JUNE.
O gentle, tender, rosy, blushing June,
	How fair thou art, how beautiful and bright,
And sweet the music of that wondrous tune
	That nature sings for thee both day and night— 
Sings ere the morning dawn lights up the day,
	When the lone-star its faithful vigil keeps:
And on, and on, through early morning grey
	While the tired world in peaceful silence sleeps.
Then, when the sun in splendor doth appear,.
	And golden light with blue and purple blends,
Myriads of birds in chorus, sweet and clear,
	Join nature’s anthem, which to heaven ascends;
And o’er the valleys fair, and on the heights;
	Where foot of man, perchance, has never trod,
There rests a glory; that the sunshine lights,
	Bearing the impress of the works of God.

Flow’rs of the garden, and of wood and field,
	Burst into beauty in their bright array,
A silent power o’er human hearts they wield,
	And light with joy the dark and lonely way.
Roses of June, a charm within them dwells,
	To shed their fragrance over all the land,
And this alone a subtle story tells,
	If we the mystery could understand.

O halcyon time, when June aud roses sweet
	Gladden the world for those who nature love,
For then her beauty seemeth most complete;
	The earth is fair, and heaven smiles above.
And all day long the zephyrs waft perfume
	Grateful as echoes of an old-time song;
While flowers of every kind spring into bloom,
	And birds and brooks sweet melody prolong.
E’en lovers voices; soft and low, are heard
	With all the tenderness and grace of youth,
Pouring forth rapture in each whisper’d word.
	Pledging their vows of constancy and truth.
These are the tributes of thy sunny hours,
	Yea, hearts made glad with all thy loveliness,
Birds, brooks and sunshine, tender words and flow’rs,
	Add much of joy to human happiness.
Not these alone, for in our hearts of hearts
	The June of love and faith and trust we keep,
Which confidence and hope in God imparts-
	A living fountain, pure and clear and deep.

As June's sweet harmonies our pulses thrill,
	And all our souls respond with freshness new,
So shall we to diviner music still,
	Pour forth those symphonies to nature true,
And though dark shadows cross  onward way,
	Or winter winds blow fierce and wild and strong,
That heav'nly music nature sings to-day
	Will lift our souls to God in rapturous song,

O joyful June! twin-sister thou of Jove,
	From realms enchanted comest thou each year,
O'er all the earth in sportive grace to rove,
	Waving thy fairy wand both far and near,
With which thon turnest one more golden page
	In the great volume of increasing years,
Wherein the history of every age,
	Since time began, till time shall cease, appears.

O June of life, and tenderness of you,
	What guerdon leavest thou of pleasant hours,
What precious treasures, or what gems of truth
	Are garner'd in the hearts of those rare flow'rs
Of love and friendship, that once bloomed for thee-
	Say, didst thou cherish for the coming time,
In hope and faith and loving constancy,
	These sacred flow'rs so heav'nly and sublime?
	June 25, 1885.						EMILE.


THE POLISH EXILE.

		[A CHARACTER.]

   Manners polished, easy, graceful, obliging,
delicate and unobstrusive, denoting superior
birth and education. The upper part of the
face handsome; hair black; eyes blue--a con-
trast not often seen--benevolence, the ruling
expression of the face, that of the eyes, pen-
sive and meditative, but when in conversation
they seem to ignite from within, and become
capable of a complex expression. Touch but
the chords on which, hung grievous sorrows
and injuries, such as few are called upon to
pass through, and they darted forth fierce and
riery rays, and the passion vein of the fore-
head would swell well nigh to bursting; pas-
sionate words would escape, the lip curled with
a bitterness unnatural to it; but soon would
come the crushing sense of the impotence of
such feelings; then would be felt, in all its
agony, the entire prostration of all power in
SELF, the eyes would swim in boiling tears,
that seemed to scald instead of soothe, and
memory, that curse and blessing of our exist-
ence, would, for the moment, well nigh extin-
guish the kindly feeling of that kind heart;
but 'twould pass--the swollen vein would
gradually  disperse, that tried heart would
cease its mighty pulsation, its bitter throbbings,
and the countenance wear again the index of
peace and serenity.
   Few have witnessed such sad extremes of
fortune.  Not only were house and lands, home
and hearth broken up, but every tie to which
the heart fondly links itself--all were reckless-
ly severed, and left that mighty heart seared
on the Pyre of Patriotism! But though all
these were rent asunder friends sprang up
around the exile to speak the consoling word,
and administer both to the mental and to the
corporeal. Friendship, warm and faithful, none
could drive away, no change of fortune could
extinguish. It hovered like a guardian angel
over the doomed, and poured the "oil and
wine" of sympathy into the lacerated bosom
of the exile and the solitary.
   Fond of solitude--society recalling too
vividly sad reverses and bereavements; but
within the circle that friendship drew around,
cheerful, polite, and even for a few moments,
sometimes gay; but soon an air of sadness and
indifference would be visible, for retrospective
thoughts would crowd upon that mind, and
the laugh would become hysterical and the
countenance indicate that "when the heart is
better than the fortune there is something that
makes even affection bitter!” Yet over all
there was thrown a dignified resignation, that
ever exerted its supremacy--and gratitude for
the sympathy and loving kindness of friends,
so liberally tendered, was never for a moment
extinguished by one who was singularly sensi-
tive and appreciative.
   How I longed at that moment to be rich
and powerful, that I might bless myself in
blessing one who stood in such impressive
need.
   Who can see a "countenance sicklied o'er
with the pale cast of thought", which will in
future shine but through memory, and not
yearn to cheer, to lighten, to brush off--if only
with a feather from the downy wing of Chris-
tian love, a portion of that grief which "passeth
show?" to inflate the shrinking heart with
hope and joy, and raise the drooping head.
Thank God! this can be done without money
and without price! Yes, yes, this has been
done without the MAMMON of this world.

	Hail! heaven-born sympathy! magnetic power,
	The sweetest cordial in th' afflictive hour.
	Tho' poor we be, and impotent to save,
	This precious balm the loving always have.
	Oh, why is sorrow, why corroding care
	To some apportioned in so large a share?
	Their lives one scene of desolating woe,
	And joy and happiness they rarely know--
	Their sun of peace but gilds life's chequered scene,
	Like angel visits, "few and far between;
	'Tis not for sinners to arraign the rod--
	"Be still," ye mortals, know that I am God!
	Father of Love, we bow before Thy throne,
	And trembling faith would lisp, "Thy will be done."
							HANNAH T. KING.


AUTOBIOGRAPHY
			OF EMILY D. P. YOUNG.
			Continued.
	On the fourth of July, 1838, the Saints as-
sembled in Far West to celebrate the day, and
I think the spot for the temple was that day
dedicated.  Our national flag, the stars and a
stripes, attached to the liberty-pole, floated 
gaily in the breeze.  All were happy and joy- 
ful, as none but Saints know how to be.
	Shortly after the Fourth a terrible storm
arose; the thunder and lightning were terrific;
the liberty pole was struck and shattered by a
bolt, foreshadowing coming events, as the se-
quel proved.
	Not long after this rumors came to Far
West, from different settlements of the Saints,
of threats, and depredations being committed
by small parties of Missourians.  There was
trouble in Davis County—a battle was fought
on Crooked River, and Brother David Patten,
one of the Twelve, and some others of the
brethren were killed.  Then came the news of
the terrible massacre at Haun’s Mill, and be-
fore we were hardly aware of it a large army
of the mob were marching towards Far West,
with an exterminating order from the Gove-
nor.  The brethren hastily got together wag-
ons, logs, boards, and whatever they could find
that would do, and threw up a breastwork to
protect themselves, in a measure, from the bul-
lets of the murderous mob.  The mob halted
when within about half a mile of Far West.
A white flag was sent out by the mob, and
were met by a party of our brethren, also
carrying a white flag.  The mob demanded
three persons to be brought out of the city,
then their design was to massacre the rest.
	The day following another flag was sent by,
the mob, and some of our brethren met them
and learned that they were commissioned by
the chief executive, and were authorized to
exterminate the Mormons en masse, and they
had three thousand troops under command to
carry these orders into effect.  Col. Hinkle
went out to meet the flag of truce, and secret-
ly made arrangements to deliver up the lead-
ers of the Church to be tried and punished; to
have the property of the Saints delivered over
to the mob to pay their expenses and all dam-
ages done them, and also arranged that the
Saints should leave the state, and their arms
be delivered up to the enemy.
	In the evening of the same day the first step
in this base treachery was taken.  Hinkle re-
presented to the Prophet that the officers of
the militia desired an interview with Him, in
the hope that a settlement might be brought
about without carrying out the Governor’s ex-,
terminating orders.


Brother Joseph and others complied with
the request, and were delivered into the hands
of the mob, as prisoners of war, by the treacher-
ous and cowardly George. M. Hinkle. The
brethren were put into a hollow square and
strongly guarded. The mob then set up a
most horrid, unearthly yell, and one might
well imagine that it came from the throats of
demons of the lower regions. It was a sound
long to be remembered, and one that no person
could desire to hear but once in their lives, es-
pecially under like circumstances.
	On the morning of the 1st of November the
ougle sounded for the brethren to assemble.
Every man went well armed, and was paraded
and delivered over to the mob.
	The brethren were surrounded and required
to surrender their arms, and were guarded all
day, while the soldiers went from house to
house, plundering, pillaging, destroying and
driving, in some instances, women and children
from their homes.
	Before the mob disbanded, after securing
the arms of the brethren, they rode through
the city, and passed so close to our house that
we could hear their remarks.
	A short time after this I was out doors, when
a party of the mob came up with their guns on
their shoulder, almost to our door, and shot a
two year old heifer. I felt no fear; for I had
got pretty well used to seeing mobocrats by
this time, but stood and see them skin and cut
it up and carry  it away.
	A court martial was held by the officers of
the mob and Joseph, and the brethren that
were with him, without having the privilege of
saying a word in their own defense, were sen-
tenced to be shot on Friday morning on the
public square in Far West, in the presence of
their families and friends. At this General
Doniphan objected, saying, he would have
nothing to do with such cold-blooded actions,
and he would withdraw his company from the
army. This probably saved the lives of the
prisoners, at that time, as the sentence was
changed, and they were taken to Independence,
Jackson Co.  Fifty-six more of the brethren
were taken prisoners by General Clark, among
whom was my father. They were collected
within a small circle on the public square,
surrounded by a strong guard, and there they
were compelled to sign a deed of trust, which
deed was designed to put their property into
the hands of a committee, to be disposed of to
pay all the debts which had been contracted
by any, and all that belonged to the Church.
Also to pay all damages the mob might have
sustained from any person whatever. And all
those who denied the faith were exonerated
from signing this deed of trust. They were
then marched and confined in Burk's tavern,
in Far West, Nov., 1838.
	General Clark came in and said to the
brethren there, that they were guilty of all
manner of crimes, and although they might
not be more guilty than others who were not
taken prisoners, yet he intended to make an
example of them. The nature and enormity
of their crimes were such that they were not
fit to live among a moral people, in moral so-
ciety. Therefore they should not be allowed
to live in the state; that it was a part of the
treaty made by General Lucas, that the Mor-
mons should leave the state and that was the
Governor's orders also. He said he would
permit them to stay until the weather became
warm, and if they were not off then he would
pledge himself that he would drive them out
of the state; and if he had to come again he
would show them no quarter.
	They were then driven to Richmond, Ray
Co., Missouri, like so many dumb animals, and
confined in Richmond jail. The weather was
cold, the ground was covered with mud and
slush, and when they camped for the night
they lay upon the ground, and some were
without a blanket to wrap around them to
protect them from the cold and frost. But I
will let my father tell his treatment at that
time, while a prisoner, in his own words, as he
wrote them in the form of a prayer.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


SCENES AND INCIDENTS AT
	WINTER QUARTERS.

BY HELEN MAR WHITNEY.

(Continued.)

     "Bro. Kimball called upon father to speak.
He arose and said:  'I suppose, as I am the old-
est person in the room, you may look upon me,
in some degree, as a father. I don't know
that I can say any more, in essence, than what
your Father Kimball has said. He has spoken
of several things, especially of faith, which, if
acted upon, will redound to your glory.
Brother Heber has spoken of the parable of
the watch--it is a good one, and if we keep
the watch wound up we cannot sin, for if a
man has the spirit of truth and righteousness
he cannot sin, and when he is possessed by the
opposite spirit he cannot do anything correct.
When we have a bad spirit, and call upon the
Lord, we will find that He is a great deal farther
off than we would imagine. Brother Heber
touched upon doing right--I believe that there
is no man in the whole circle of my acquaint-
ance, that intends to do better and work right-
eousness more than Bro. Kimball, nor one that
comes nearer to the point in so doing; there-
fore it behooves you to hold up his hands, and
as he advances in knowledge, so will you the
same; as his vessel is filled, he will pour it on
to your heads. God is no respecter of persons,
but He sets men on the earth to teach His peo-
ple, as masters are set to instruct little child-
ren. God will instruct His people from time to
time as they can bear knowledge, and when we
have fulfilled the things our messenger has
given us, another will come with fresh intelli-
gence. I have no doubt but what many of
you here will live to see the Ancient of Days
come--even old Father Adam.
     We are now in the dispensation of the ful-
ness of times, and every ordinance or office
that has been practised in the church of form-
er days, will be revived in this dispensation,
even to the offering of sacrifice. Malachi
says, 'before the great and terrible day of the
Lord, I will send Elijah, the prophet, lest
there be not faith enough on the earth, and I
smite it with a curse.'
	'And for that reason was Joseph Smith sent
to turn the hearts of the fathers to the child-
ren, etc., to prepare them for the coming of
the Lord--and so He will continue to send us
intelligence by messengers, till at last, old
Father Adam shall at last appear to see his
children.
     We are too apt to judge our brethren unjust-
ly, and we should be very careful how we do
so, when we know that they are governed by
the Spirit of God. We should mind and keep
the helm of the ship in our hands, lest the
helm slip a little, a storm come on, and we lose
our latitude and longitude--but if we have
the spirit of the Lord we never shall get out
of our latitude and longitude. We should be
careful and keep the Spirit of God on our side,
for the path will grow brighter and brighter
until the perfect day.
     'Now, brethern, do you not think that it
would be a heaven on earth if there was per-
fect confidence among you--and this is heaven,
and all the heaven that we have will be that
which we make ourselves. I have sometimes
thought of the words of the Psalmist--'How
pleasant it is for brethren to dwell together in
unity,' etc. A two fold cord is stronger than
one: and take it in a family and see the neces-
sity, as well as the beauty, of union. What

[column 2]
shall I say more, brethern? I could sit down
and chat with you, I suppose, and answer al-
most any question on principle--I always rejoice
when I am able to throw light upon any mind
that is seeking intelligence.  Bro. Kimball
was observing that he had done the best he
could, and I verily believe it, under the cir-
cumstances. It is our duty to mind the ad-
monitions of the Spirit, lest we be shoaled on
the quicksands of evil. Now, I say, hearken
to the admonitions of your father, for his
teachings are good--and as for this dancing--
when we dance we should do it as much by
the spirit as we would pray. The Almighty
does not intend that a man will stay on his
knees twenty-four hours praying, but he is
willing that we should have our recreations,
but we must not get giddy, nor transcend the
bounds of reason and right, or we will get out
of the way, and have to fall back and hunt up
a position of right again. Peter sums up the
case in a few words--'add to your faith vir-
tue, and to your virtue knowledge, temperance,
and withal be patient, and last of all godliness
and brotherly kindness; and if ye observe
these things ye will not be barren nor unfruit-
ful in the knowledge of our Lord and Savior
Jesus Christ.'
     'I know I have my faults as well as your-
selves, and my principal fault is impatience,
and when I preach I always hew to the line, if
the chips fly in my face. And now I feel to
bless you all in the name of Jesus Christ.'
     "Bro. Kimball arose and said: 'I have been
much edified by the conversation of Bro.
Whitney. We always had the same feelings
in common, and our views always met, and
our thoughts always flow in the same channel
--I don't know why it is so.
     'Don't you see that there should be temper-
ance in everything--dancing, eating and
drinking--now, I love to dance myself, but let
us learn to be subject to government in all
things. I am your head, your law giver and
king, and will be to all eternity, and I am re-
sponsible to my head and President, and you
are responsible to me your file leader.  I be-
lieve that some of my old progenitors, of
whom I have no knowledge, will appear and
tell me when the time shall come fore me to
rise up and administer in the ordinances for
them, and I shall receive a great deal of
knowledge from them, which I could not re-
ceive directly by revelation. Now, this idea
you never thought of, did you, boys? and I
want you to keep it secret within the walls of
your own breasts. I want to have power, when
I see my brother or sister afflicted, to tell death
to depart, and it shall depart. Every one has
the greatest obstacles to govern himself. I have
greater trouble to govern Bro. Heber, and put
self down than anything else. I always want
a clerk to attend my meetings, so that like to-
day we can have a record to look back upon
for our edification.
     'I never felt better in my mind, but my
body is ailing. I do delight in intelligence,
and delight to see an intelligent mind. I do
not know, what I know, unless by the utter-
ance of the Spirit; and if you wish to know
anything of me, put a paper in your pocket
and minute things down, that you may ask
me questions, and it will not impoverish me.
     Now, I feel to bless you in the name of the
Lord. I feel as if we have had a good time;
my feelings are good towards you all. Bishop
Whitney is as honest a man as there is this
side of heaven, and I want you to receive his
counsel as one of the Judges in Israel.'

     "Miss Helen Taylor, the step-daughter of
John Stuart Mill, and efficient member of the
London school board, has accepted a nomina-
tion to Parliament, and will make a canvass
for the seat."


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


LOVE AND HOPE.

O lost but loved one!  tell me why
Thy mystic charm I cannot fly.
And though to love thee less I try,
All, all is in vain?
If from that siren glance I flee,
'Tis but ot sigh and dream of thee.
And till again thy face I see,
'Tis lingering pain.
In social throng, at play or ball,
The magnet thou to me of all,
The queen that holds my heart in thrall
	With constant sway.
One glance from out those love-lit eyes
One word from lips whose sweetness vies
The fabled nectar of the skies,
And I obey.
The heedless winds may hear thee speak
The insect, sporting, kiss thy cheek.
And other souls thy smiles may seek
	And haply find;
But I, thy lover, from afar
Must gaze as on some distant star,
And wage with faith unequal war,
	All unresigned.

Must we like streams run on forever,
Though nearing oft, uniting never,
Shall cruel doom our lives dissever
	In heaven as here?
Or, in the light of happier spheres,
Where live our hopes without our fears,
Will mutual love smile through our tears
	And blend us there?


WEST POINT IN NOVEMBER. 

	A dull and wintry looking morning it was
when we left New York to visit West Point.
I shall never forget the day. As we rode
along the west shore of the river—named after
Heindrich Hudson, and immortalized by that
prince of imaginative writers, Washington
Irving—the clouds, which had gathered threat-
eningly, paled, began to disperse, and the sun
broke out rich and warm. 	
	The train rolled over an undulating country
of a rather unpromising character for a brief
distance, and dashed through several tunnels.
Emerging from one of these, however, a
magnificent sweep of the Hudson River and 
the opposite shore rose full upon the view, He
must have been a stoic of the olden times and
most approved school, who, gazing on this fair
sight for the first time, could resist an exclama-
tion of delight and surprise, or whose pulses
would not give an exultant bound at the en-
chanting prospect spread out before his vi[-]ion.
The stream resembles a lake more than a river.
No current is preceptible. The yachts, the
river steamers and fishing yawls sped up and
down and danced upon its placid waters as
they sailed. Along the banks and up the side of the mountains—or deeply wooded hills,
which hem in the stream on either side—are
pleasant little villages, charming and elegant
summer residences, and here and there a 
manufacturing structure.
	It is a little less than fifty miles from New
York to West Point, and the distance is won-
derfully short, to one to whom the scene has
not grown familiar. The train discharges the
West Point visitor at a point below the reserva-
tion, and speeds on its way through a tunnel
right under the hill on which the reservation is
located.
	To realize the beauty of West Point one
must stroll fully over the reservation. Situa-
ted on a table land, which swells abruptly to
the east, the river makes a majestic curve
around it, and thus a magnificent view of the
river is obtained on the north, the east and
the south, while fringing both sides are the low,
slowly rising hills, deeply bedded with woods.
The mountains gird the reservation on the

[Column 2]
west, while to the east is a preciptious bluff
looking down upon the smooth waters be-
neath.
	The educational buildings and cadet quar-
ters are on the south side of the plateu. The
 residences of officers are also on the south and 
some on the west. Those to the west are 
skirted by a drive on the east, and are not un-
like the quarters of the officers at Fort Doug-
las, only the drive there is on the west with
the mountains on the east—at West Point it
is just the reverse: the drive is on the east of
the quarters, while the mountains form a back-
ground on the west.  The broad parade or
drill ground is on the north and east of the
barracks and officers' residences, while from
the north end, or the hotel, a superb view may
be had of the river and the town of Newburgh,
at which place Washington established his
head-quarters.  (The building is said to be
standing yet, and is preserved by the State.)
at the north side, also, are many of the guns,
or cannon captured during the Mexican war,
and on the east are trenches and earth works
in all stages of construction, used doubtless in
the education of cadets.
	The riding school is down in the east, be-
neath the brow of the hill and nearer the
river.
	 All these details are unimportant, save that
they add to the reputation of the place in a
mental sense, nor are they entirely without
picturesqueness, while they give an air of
strength and majesty which would otherwise
be wanting.
	The view, looking from any point, and in
any direction, is incomparable, and leaves an
impression upon the mind that cannot be ef-
faced.  But the winding river, broad, placid
and bright in the sunlight, with the white sails
dotting its surface, the pleasant villages and
charming villas, half hidden amid the thick
growth of evergreen trees bordering the lan-
guid stream on either side, the scattered tokens
of success of American armies, the structures
for the education of the American youth in the
art of war, with their pleasant and delightful
surroundings, all, all are as nothing compared
with the rare, the romantic beauty of the walk
that winds, along the river bank, and nestles so
closely under the brow of the hill, on which
the grounds and buildings are situated as to
hide all evidence of their existence from the
view “Flirtation Walk”—name well suited to
those whose weightiest thoughts are as the
bubble that floats measuredly by on the grand
stream (the waters leaving the shore at their
feet) and which the lightest breath of heaven
will destroy, only to give place to another as
short of life and as evanescent.
	Straying along the winding way one can
but wonder how nature creates souls so lacking
in depth as to withstand its spell, its pensive
witch[-]ry, or fail to be moved by that sweet,
that touching melancholy which alone kindles
in the trusting heart purified and hallowed
fellings, which alone can fill the mind with
bright, ennobling, glowing aspirations. Art
has done much to enchance the beauty of a
scene naturally enchanting; and the art was
true, for its presence can hardly be detected.
Save that the walk underneath the precipitons
hill, and just out of the water's reach, is kept
clean and safe, save for the occasional rustic
seat, placed in out of the way corners for the
rest of travelers and the gratification of lovers,
save for the steps provided for safety in pre-
cipitous places, or where leading down to
springs of water, all is natural, all rustic, all
enchanting beyond the power of words to de-
scribe.
	There are two or three paths leading down
to the walks, from the plateau above. Once in
this winding path, all evidence of military
academy and its immediate surroundings are
lost; nothing but the broad stream; with boats

[Column 3]
gliding over its unmurmuring bosom, the
wooded hills on the other side, with the lazy,
curling smoke rising from houses buried in the
evergreen foliage, nothing but the steep and
overhanging bluff to be seen. This walk leads
into the most delightfully squestered nooks;
through small groves, over rough and brokən
places; here a huge, rough stone, seamed
through and through, each seam filled with
fresh, green moss; yonder is a tiny dell. There,
below that overhaning cliff, is a most delight-
ful grotto, filled with damp moss, and trailing
vines everywhere, while the odor is fresh and
sweet; here the denuded trees overarch, and
around that turn you will find a recess with
the most convenient seat imaginable in it for
those listening to the shy, soft tones of one, the
pleading words of the otehr, and living, for
the sweet moment at least, in the tender glance
and the radiant smile from loving eyes and
warm and tempting lips—oblivious to all that
surrounds—to all the world. And foreer, to be
viewed from every point, the grand old
river glides serenely on, the unmoved, the un-
heeding confident of the enraptured poet; of
the uttered rhapsodies of the deep and abiding
admirer of nature; of the pensive sighs of
modest maidens; of the warm and passionate
pleadings of the ardent lover; of the flippant
gibes of the self-satisfied cadet, whose whole,
whose ever-recurring, ever-uttered, persistently
out-spoken idea is that to imagine anything
above or equal to a West Point cadet is the 
measure of absurdity.
	There's not a rustle even of a tiny leaf, as
I move along the path (my soul drinking deep
of the calm, the serene, the holy enchantment
of the scene) that does not awaken tender
thoughts and sweet and touching recollec-
tions, that does not fill me with better
impulses, with truer, manlier aspirations. I 
have long been confirmed in the belief that
surroundings are potent features in the develop-
ment of character. Believing this I cannot
understand why it is that the influence of the
magnificent scenery, everywhere to be seen
along the Hudson River, and especially at
West Point, should not produce, even among
those striving to be skilled in war, deep lovers
of art, and those imbued with such an ardent
spirit of poetry, such as can find satisfaction
and relief only in individual expression, in the
utterance of the thoughts that forever well up
in the hearts of those who are deeply moved by
nature in her endless, her manifold and varied
forms, all forever beautiful.
	The day is sombre and cold. Ever and
anon the clouds gather and a storm threatens,
but each time the darkening clouds disperse
and the sun again comes out bright. They
tell me the scene is cold and cheerless now;
that compared with the appearance of the
place in June, or during the spring and sum-
mer the present sight is as nothing.  I can
hardly believe it, and yet realize it must be so.
But should I be denied the happiness of a
visit when the recurring seasons attire this
dreamy and romantic spot in her lovliest robes
of green and flowers, still the picture it pre-
sents on this fitful November day will ever re-
main bright, glowing and pensively beauti-
ful.
	It was night when we left to return to New
York. We took the Grand Central down the
east side, passing Tarrytown, Sing Sing,
Harlem and other points. The moon shone
full and clear, and as we swept along the edge
of the river, now filled with steamers, yachts
and tiny boats, all lit and dancing on the great
river, the weird beauty, the romantic halo
which the rich moonlight sheds over all it
floods upon, served only to make deeper and
more lasting the impressions of this day, be-
causes of that which the eye had beheld, and
which had already sunk deep into the soul.
						WANDERING BOY.

SALT LAKE CITY,	*	*	JULY 1, 1885.


OLD FOLKS’ EXCURSION.

	It is now ten years since the first excursion of
the old people, on May 14th, 1875.  Each time it
has been a success, giving much happiness to
those whose life is necessarily more or less mo-
notonous.  It was a happy idea--the suggestion
of Brother C. R. Savage--to give the aged this
annual treat, and must afford considerable satis-
faction to those who first engaged in the affair, to
know how popular these excursions have become,
and how much the old people have enjoyed these
delelightful “outs.”
	Those who have contributed of their means to
help the committee make the excursions in every
way successful, will certainly feel doubly blest, and
more than repaid in the genuine enjoyment of
those who are decending the down hill of life.
	On Tuesday, June 23rd, by invitation of the
committee, we had the pleasure of joining the
party of veterans, who went by the Utah and Ne-
vada Railway to Garfield to spend the day.  There
were about 600 persons over seventy years of age,
and quite a number of others, making in all about
900.  The Sixth Ward silver band played some
good music at the depot as the train started.
The morning was delightfully cool after the re-
freshing shower on Monday, and the train arrived,
after about an hour's ride, at Garfield Station,
where tables and seats had been prepared under
the bowery to accomodate the party.  The Old
Folks' choir, led by W. H. Foster, sang some
pleasing songs, and Brother Dunbar played sever-
al tunes upon the bagpipes, which gave variety to
the enjoyment.
	There was much social conversation, and the
meeting of old friends and shaking hands was in
itself a great pleasure.  The picnic seemed very
plentiful; indeed all seemed not only to have an
abundance, but a variety, and cold and hot water,
tea and coffee were furnished by the committee, 
who spared no pains to make all the party feel at
home, and enjoy themselves.
	Some indulged in the luxury of a bath in the
salt water, but everybody seemed to participate in
the general good feeling that prevailed through-
out the entire day.
	About 11 o’clock the train from Tooele County
arrived with two hundred more, and carriages
and wagons brought about 400, mostly from
Tooele and Grantsville.  They came supplied
with everything good to entertain their party, and
plenty to spare; also some very beautiful flowers,
strawberries and cherries, which gave their tables
a handsome appearance, and were very inviting.
	At .2 p.m. the committee had all arranged to
open the meeting.  A few very old people were
seated on the stand; the oldest person was Mary
Bishop, from Manchester, England, who will be
ninety-nine on the 4th of September next.. The
next oldest was James Berghon, English, who will
be ninety-three on the 18th of next September.
The choir sang "Sweet By and Bye," and prayer
was offered by Elder George Goddard, after
which "Fleeting Years" was very sweetly ren-
dered by the choir.
	By this time the special train, which brought
the Presiding Bishop and his Counselors, had ar-
rived, and when the Bishops were seated upon
the stand, Brother Goddard, of the Old Folks
Committee, moved that Bishop W. B. Preston
succeed the late Bishop E. Hunter as chairman of
said committee.  The motion was seconded and
carried unanimously.
	Then came the distribution of prizes, which is
always  exciting time, and immensely interest-
ing.  Those over ninety, who wore white ribbon
bows, were served first, and of course received
the best; after that, the two oldest people from
Tooele and Grantsville; and we much not forget
to state that the brass band from Grantsville came
in their handsome uniform and furnished excellent
music for the day.  Mr. Radcliffe, the leader of
the band, was given a fine centennial watch, for
the musical services rendered the old folks.
Next, prizes were given to all those present who
had pulled handcarts across the plains; then to
the man who had the greatest number of living
children, and to the lady who had been longest a
widow; to Bishop Alexander McRae, who had
been in prison with the Prophet Joseph Smith in
Liberty Jail, Mo.; to Gretha Anderson, who had
walked the entire distance across the plains; to
the oldest man and woman from the Sandwich
Islands, Sweden, Norway, Denmark, France,
Germany, Switzerland, Australia and Canada; to
Brother and Sister Ringwood of this city, who
have lived together fifty-nine years; to the hand-
somest man over eighty, the prize being awarded
to Brother Lewis of this city, the old ladies ren-
dering the decision.  Prizes to all the blind; to
several women over seventy, who go out washing;
to the oldest bachelor and old maid present; to
Sister Booth of India, who resides in Beaver, and
who was not present. 
	The prizes consisted of rocking chairs, a trunk, 
hats, caps, bonnets, shoes, stockings, socks, dress
patterns, parasols, canes, tea coffee, wine, china
and glass sets, copper tea kettle, metal tea pots,
etc., etc.  Most of them were handsome and dur-
able articles.
	After the prizes were distributed, the old peo-
ple were treated to cakes, candy, oranges and
lemonade.
	At seven o'clock the party were all on board the
train, homeward bound, and all feeling happy and
joyful for the good time they had enjoyed together
during the day.  It was an interesting sight to see
so many of the aged, and to think that the Gospel
had brought most of them together, here in this
far off western land, and to contemplate the varie-
ty of experiences through which each one of these
had passed.
	God bless the old folks, and all those who
honor them and contribute to their welfare and
happiness.


[Column 1]
		EDITORIAL NOTES.

	THE Relief Society of this Stake of Zion held
a quarterly conference in the Fourteenth Ward
Assembly rooms on Friday, June 26th, commence-
ing at 10 a. m., and 2 p. m., Mrs. M. I. Horne
presiding. A synopsis of proceedings will be
given in our next issue. Saturday June 27, the
Primary Conference of this stake was held in the
Assembly Hall, commencing at 10 a. m., Mrs.
Ellen C. Clawson presiding. Reports were re-
ceived from nearly all the wards in the stake, in-
terspersed with songs and recitations from the
several Associations. In the afternoon at 2 p. m.
the Y. L. M. I. A. of this stake, held conference
in the Assembly Hall, Miss Nellie Colebrook,
presiding.
	THE annual meeting of the Relief Society of the
16th Ward of this city, was held in the ward
meeting house in Monday, June 22nd, commence-

[Column 2]
ing at 2 p. m., Mrs. Diana Reid presiding. The
Bishop and his Counselors were present. After
the usual opening exercises, the minutes and re-
port were read, which were very satisfactory,
and the meeting was addressed by Sisters E.
Howard, E. B. Wells, B. W. Smith and M. I.
Horne. President Reid made a few closing re-
marks, and the meeting was dismissed with prayer
by the Bishop. A bounteous repast had been
prepared by the members of the Society, and
tables were spread in the hall, very handsomely
ornamented with a variety of flowers and
most delicious fruits of the season, at
which the guests were seated and waited
upon by the officers of the Society,and some of the
young ladies of the ward.
	THE first annual meeting of the Relief Society of
Granger, was held on Monday the 15th of June,
1885, at the school house, at 2 p. m.,Mrs Mary E.
Porter presiding. After singing, prayer was
offered by Bishop Dan McRae, and after singing
again, minutes of the organization were read and
a yearly report, which showed that the sisters had
been diligent and faithful in the discharge of their
several duties. The meeting was addressed by
Sisters B. W. Smith, E. B. Wells and E. H.
Woodmansee of Salt Lake City, and by Elder
Geo. B. Wallace, Bishop D. McRae and Presi-
dent Porter. A very good spirit prevailed in the
meeting, and the Bishop expressed his approval
of the sisters’ labors, and stated it was his inten-
tions, sometime in the near future, to have a
Young Ladies Association and a Primary organ-
ized. The benediction was pronounced by Coun-
selor A. Sorenson. The Sisters from the city
the Bishop and wife, Elder Geo. B. Walace
and wife, Elder Park and wife, dined at the
residence of M. B. Porter, and feasted upon
strawberries, and after the close of the meeting
partook of delicious ice cream, prepared by Sis-
ter Mary Gerber.
	BY courtesy of Mr. James Dwyer, we have re-
ceived the new work just recently issued, entitled
“The Fall of the Great Republic.” It is by Sir
Henry Standish Coverdale, and is indeed quite a
remarkable book. It is written in a very novel way,
and the most dreadful calamities are portrayed in
a literary style, calculated to hold the attention,
though one likes not the subject. It is very unlike
any other book we have ever seen. The author
evidently believes what he writes, and is thorough-
ly in earnest, though he tells things in his own pe-
culiar style. He begins with 1895, as though the
world had gone through all the important changes
which are afterwards graphically described. Se-
cret organizations, from dissatisfied conditions,
brought about through the conflict between capital
and labor, the hatred of the rich by the working
people, these and similar causes, growing and as-
summing an alarming phase, culminate in outbreak
and lawlessness, until there is a general outbreak
in the United States, commencing, according to
his prediction, in Chicago, on the 19th of April,
1887, when he describes a horrible carnival of
blood, rapine and incendiarism, which he further
depicts in detail. The book, in many points, is
similar to the prophecies of Joseph Smith the
Prophet; at any rate there is no doubt in the mind
of every true Latter-day Saint, that the troubles
are already beginning to develop that will bring
about alarming and terrifying consequences, that
will end in a most serious struggle, previous to the 
time of Christ’s coming to reign a thousand years
in the millennium of peace. The book is for sale
at James Dwyer’s, Main Street, price, 35 cents,
and will be mailed to any address, postage paid,
on receipt of price, either in cash or two cent
stamps. The book is worth a thorough perusal.
	ON Tuesday, June 30th, a social party convened,
by invitation of Sister Marinda Hyde, at the resi-
dence of her son, Alonzo, to celebrate the seven-
tieth anniversary of the birth of this most esti-


mable and venerable lady. Her birthday, how-
ever, occurred on Sunday, the 28th of June.
There was a delicious repast set for the ladies, at
six p.m., with delicacies to satisfy an Epicurean,
and the tables were handsomely ornamented with
flowers. After supper the ladies indulged in a
little speech making in honor of the happy occas-
ion, and to express the sentiments of love and
admiration they felt for this honored mother in
Israel. Sister Phebe Woodruff made the open-
ing speech, and was followed by Sisters H. T.
King, C. Horrocks, M. I. Horne and S. M. Kim-
ball. Sisters Hyde then made a few remarks in
response to the good feelings of the sisters, fol-
lowed by Sister Riter, B W. Smith, Julia Pack,
Sisters E. B. Wells, E. Howard and Julia C.
Howe. It was a pleasant and happy gathering of
congenial friends and acquaintances. Among the
guests assembled were Sisters Laura Miner and
Emily Chase, married daughters of Sister Hyde,
Sisters E. S. Taylor, R. M. Carrington, S.
A. Reese, Helen Mar Whitney, Lizzie R. Young,
Rachel R. Grant, Annie Ivins, Sister Adams
and several other dear friends and relatives. Sis-
ter Hyde was baptized into the Church in April,
1831, in Kirtland, where her father resided. She
is one of the Johnson family,notable in the early
history of the Church, and is also the widow of
the late Apostle Orson Hyde. Sister Hyde has
ever been known for good works, her labors of
love, her sweet charity and wise counsel to the
daughters of Zion. May she live to see as many
happy returns of the day as her heart desires. Her
posterity already number over forty, among them
eight great grandchildren.
	THE Lecture on Education given before the
Teacher’s Institute in the Fourteenth Ward As-
sembly Rooms, by Bishop O. F. Whitney, on
Friday evening, June 9th, was a finished and pol-
ished work of its kind. It is decidedly the best lec-
ture on that subject given by any one of the young
people born and reared in Utah. It contains
much food for thought, as well as excellent mate-
rial to put in practise in our every day life. The
speaker’s enunciation was clear and distinct,
his voice well modulated, which adds much to the
interest of the listener, and his manner was with-
al well adapted to his subject, and pleasing to the
audience. He summed up a perfect education as
a full and uniform development of the mental,
the physical, the moral and the spiritual capabili-
ties of the faculties and powers given to man
and woman. He pointed out, and illustrated
clearly, the mistake made, the educating of the
mind to the neglect of the body, and made a
quotation from Byron, which is beautifully adapt-
ed to the illustration. He reasoned well and
eloquently upon the strong point the infidels
claim to make that religion and science are in
conflict, and brought powerful testimony to prove
that science and true religion could not be an-
tagonistic. It is a very great pity that the lecture
was not given in the theatre, or some large build-
ing, and well advertised beforehand, so that very
many more might have had the opportunity of
hearing him. It has, however, been published
in full in the Deseret News, and it should be read
by all those interested in the education and culti-
vation of the young. Although hearing and
reading are very different in effect, upon the mind
and character. To hear a lecture delivered with
the power, force and earnestness of the speaker
makes a far deeper and more lasting impression
upon most minds than to read it. Mr. Whitney’s
lecture would certainly bear reading carefully
after hearing it delivered.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


MRS. LOCKWOOD IS COMING.

	Mrs. Belva A. Lockwood is announced to lec-
ture in the Salt Lake Theatre, on Monday evening,
July 6th. Her subject will be "Political and So-
cial Life in Washington," a most prolific subject


and one in which the people of Utah are much
interested. She is well posted in regard to Wash-
ington society indoors and out, and her lecture
cannot fail to be of deep interest, for she knows
how to manage her subjects in an effective and in-
teresting manner. Mrs. Lockwood is as well
known, perhaps, as any lady of the present day in
the United States. She has been for some years
before the public as a lawyer and a leading woman
suffragist, and was the first woman admitted to
practice before the bar of the Supreme Court of
the United States. She is the leading editor of
the National Equal Rights, a paper published in
Washington and San Francisco (the organ of the
Equal Rights Party) the party that nominated
Mrs. Lockwood as their candidate for President
of the United States, during the last canvasing
for Presidential election.
	Mrs. Lockwood has several times spoken before
committees in Congress, and also before the con-
ventions of the National Woman Suffrage Associa-
tion in the City of Washington, against the bills
framed from time to time to deprive this people of
the rights and liberties to which all free-born men
and women are entitled. Mrs. Lockwood is brave,
frank and generous, and deserves all the praise
bestowed upon her. She is a very pleasing, agree-
able and interesting speaker. She is exceedingly
sharp and clear-sighted, and abounds with interest-
ing facts and pithy paragraphs. Her logic is
plain and conclusive, and she cares very little
about popular ideas or opinions, but is quite origi-
nal and independent in expressing her views.
	Those who attend her lecture will have a rare
treat, for there are few, if any, women in the
world who are better calculated to attract and in-
terest an audience than Mrs. Lockwood. The
lady's home is in Washington, and she has an ex-
tensive practice in her profession. She has been a
firm and consistent advocate for the rights of
the women of Utah, and is fairly well acquainted
with our question, having been associated con-
siderably with several of the people from Utah,
when they have been in the City of Washington.
We trust the people here will show their apprecia-
tion of Mrs. Lockwood's efforts in their behalf by
coming out en masse to hear her speak at
the theatre in this city, on Monday, July 6th, at
8 in the evening. Tickets, 75, 50 and 25 cts,
Mrs. Lockwood will speak in Ogden, Logan and
Provo, on Tuesday, Wednesday and Thursday,
July 7th, 8th and 9th.


ITEMS FROM AN INTERESTING
	LETTER.

	The extracts published below are from a letter
written by a maiden lady, who resides upon the
Isle of Wight, to her cousin, an English gentle-
woman, one well known and respected among this
people, and very highly respected by all who have
the pleasure of her acquaintance, in this city.
The lady is one of high social standing, and es-
pecially interested in educational interests.—ED.]
	“I have not yet acknowledged
three enclosures of papers—one containing two
of the WOMAN'S EXPONENT, and a magazine
containing the little book—all so beautifully and
firmly done up, that it seems quite an art to show
such expertness.
	“As to Miss Faithful,I have already heard of
her in a way that somewhat surprised me, though
of small import in comparison with matters of
real moment; but in the absence of any explana
tion of her conduct, as represented in the Novem-
ber number of the EXPONENT, one is at a loss to
understand the ungracious and ungrateful part of
receiving hospitality, kindnesses and courtesies
from strangers, all the more worthy of considera-
tion on that very account, and then making so 
base a return! Bearing fully in mind that she
might conscientiously differ on some points of


great importance, she might well have justified
the expectation, suggested in the article on the
subject, in the said number, that she would at
leest deal honestly and fairly in the matter, and I
should have thought, also, have warmly acknowl-
edged the kindness shown to herself.
	“Of course the passage, in which she speaks or
'the keen eye of the Prophet' as discerning his op-
portunities in reference to the 'fertile spot,'
showed great ignorance of what she is writing
about, as those who read the leading papers and
journals know that it is an accepted fact that the
change from a mere wilderness into a fertile spot
—indeed I think I have seen it called an earthly
paradise—was due tothe talent, energy, persever-
ance and industry of Brigham Young and his fol-
lowers, and as I have read the credit of it was
fully accorded to them, and that without any pre-
tence of sympathy with their religious views. I
am a dear  lover of fairness and straight-forward
dealing.”


VARIOUS OPINIONS REGARDING OUR
	FUTURE STATE.

	Many diverse opinions have been entertained
by different religious sects and nations as to if we
had an existence after we ceased to breathe, and
if so, what was the condition of the spirit after it
had left the body?
	All professors of religion, at the present time,
believe in an after life, but with the exception of a
few, often ridiculous notions, are at a loss to de-
termine how the spirit is employed in its new
home.
	The Spiritualists do not claim to be a religious
sect—that is, they do not recognize any priesthood,
but all of their teachings are unreasonable, when
compared with what we know of mind, and the
conclusions we would naturally arrive at concern-
ing the next world from the experience and knowl-
edge we have of this.
	The Essenees, the most strict of the Jewish sects,
believed that souls were immortal, and when re-
leased from their bodies rejoiced, and mounted
upwards, and that good souls had their home be-
yond the ocean, from which blew a gentle west
wind, where there was neither storms of rain or
snow, nor intense heat; "while they allotted to the
bad souls a dark and tempestuous den, full of never
ceasing punishments."
	The Greeks allotted the islands of the blessed
to their brave men, whom they called heroes, or
demi-gods, and to the souls of the wicked the re-
gion of the ungodly in Hades.
	Josephus also informs us that the Pharisees be-
lieved that "under the earth there would be re-
wards or punishments, accordingly as they had
lived virtuously or viciously in this life," and that
the souls of good only were removed into other
bodies, or would have power to revive and to live
again in this world, and that the souls of bad men
are subject to eternal punishment.
	The Sadducees did not believe in the immortality
of t he soul.
	The Romans judge that man, happy, who died
in battle, and believed, his spirit "was received by
the ether and joined to that company which are
placed among the stars, and that they became
good demons, and showed themselves to their
posterity afterwards."  While to those who died
a natural death, though they lived good lives,
there came a subterranean night to dissolve them
to nothing, and a deep oblivion to take away all
remembrance of them.
	Socrates, an Athenian philosopher, who died
399 years B.C., believed in the immortality of the
soul, and that the dead were conducted by demons
to a place where they were all judged; that the
very wicked were hurled into Tartarus, from where
they never departed; that others suffered accord-
ing to their sins, and that the innocent inhabited


a pure region, without their bodies, enjoying every
pleasure.
	Pythagoras advanced the doctrine of the me-
tempsychosis, or transmigration of souls:
	The Egyptians, to whom he was indebted for it,
thought that at death souls transmigrated into
other human bodies; that if they had been wicked
they were imprisoned for some centuries in the
bodies of unclean beasts until they had atoned for
their sins, when they were allotted a human form.
Another of their beliefs was, that the body would
rise after some years, while the soul, though
bound to it, was at liberty to leave the grave and
ruturn to it during the day time, in any form it
choose.
	The belief of Socrates on this point corresponds
very nearly with our own, and we can say with
him, "If what I say is true I gain everything, 
whilst I hazard very little, and if false, I lose
nothing; on the contrary, I am still a great 
gainer."
	ANNIE FELLOWS.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]			
	CORRESPONDENCE.

	GORHAM, N. H., June 22nd, 1885.
DEAR MRS. WELLS:
	 We had a pleasant trip from Salt
Lake to New York—stopped at various points of
interest along the way, not forgeting one lovely
day at Niagara Falls.  From New York we took
coast steamer to Portland, stopping over one
day in Boston.
	Since our arrival we have been visiting most of
the time in  vicinity of Bethel, and other small
villages, situated on the Androscoggin, Sunday
and Bear Rivers in Maine.  The principal occu-
pation of the people in that vicinity is farming in
the summer and lumbering in the winter; but the
seasons are so short and the soil so unproductive,
that they have, to “go west” for bread.  They ex-
port lumber and import wheat, corn, etc.
	Maine is noted for her pine forests,and they
are lovely.  The sugar maple,from which the best
maple sugar and honey are made,grows abundant-
ly.  Spruce trees grow here in great numbers, and
some people make a business of collecting the
gum from these trees and sending it all over the
country. Poplar trees are ground into pulp, and
from this some of the finest paper is made. The
poplar is also used largely to make the spools up-
on which our cotton thread is wound, and large
quantities of the 'spoolsticks," as they are called,
are sent to Europe.  A large per cent of the
woodmen are Canadian French, that come across
the border, great numbers from Prince Edward’s
Island, and Nova Scotia.
	They cut down the trees in the winter, and then,
during the high water in spring and early summer,
they float them down the river to the mills and the
sea coast. It is quite a picturesque sight to see
these  "river drivers,” as they are called, especial-
ly at night when they have their bright camp fires
burning, and their white tents pitched by the
river.
	In campany with a party of friends I visited
one of the French camps in the woods, where
they had been choping.  It was Sunday, so they
were not at work, and the swarthy, lounging men,
the blackeyed, vivacious women, and the bare-
footed children reminded me of a gypsy camp.
Their houses were made in the most economical
manner, of a few boards and limbs, covered with
birch bark.  They say that in the winter the heat
from the inside melts the snow on the outside,
forming a coating of ice, something after the man-
ner of Esquimaux houses, and making them very
comfortable. 
	To get to the camp we had to cross the river on
logs floating in the water.  They assured us there
was no danger in crossing, but the logs danced up
and down in a delightfully promiscuous manner;

[Column 2]
but with the assistance of one of the "hands" we
got across with no more results than wet feet. A
number of children were paddling around on
floating logs with sticks, and quite frequently
some one would give the log a quick turn and
hal a dozen of them would go splashing into the
water. They were good swimmers; so it did no
harm, and they were greeted by a shout of laugh-
ter from their friends on the shore.  They treated
us kindly, and were quite sociable, as much so as
they could be under the circumstances.  The little
French I remembered they understood readily,
and some of them spoke pretty good English.
	I like this part of the country very much for
summer. I never get tired of walking through
the woods, where so many pretty wild flowers,
ferns and mosses can be found, and the grandest
trees, rocks and waterfalls.  The rivers and lakes
are a paradise for fishermen, and the trout caught
here makes as dainty a breakfast dish as one
could ask for.
	Maine is noted for her strict prohibition laws,
and although the law is sometimes evaded, still
drunkenness is not so prevalent here as in many
other places; but the profanity is something dread-
ful.  The most shockingly profane language is
used in common conversation, and even by little,
children. The people, as a rule, are thrifty, in-
dustrious and intelligent.  I have heard a great
deal said about the women of Utah having to per-
form manual labor, but never in my life have I
seen women do so much out door work as here.
It is a common sight to see them driving a horse
in the field, planting corn, etc., and I was told
that one young lady managed a mowing machine,
and cut all the hay on a farm, last year. I don’t
think there is  anything wrong about this, if they
choose to do it, and I do not consider that it de-
tracts anything from, their natural refinement,
but it does indicate that they possess a stronger
degree of muscular power than most young ladies
can boast of.  I read a letter, written bythe same
young lady I have mentioned; the penmanship
was very nice, and, so far as I was able to judge,
there were no mistakes in spelling, punctuation, or
grammar. 
	Though my letter is written in New Hamshire,
it is principally descriptive of Maine, but there is
not so very much difference, as Gorham is just
over the border.  It is a charming place though,
at the foot of the White Mountains, and I like it
as well as any place I have seen.
	We are enjoying our visit very much;
both mother's health and mine is very good, and we
have found many kind friends here. With kind
wish,	Yours,
	AUGUSTA WINTERS.


[Column 1]

R. S., Y. L. M. I. A. & P. A. REPORTS

SOUTH COTTONWOOD PRIMARY.

 The Annual meeting of the Primary Asso-
ciation of South Cottonwood wsa held May
23rd, in the ward meeting house, President
Elizabeth Davis presiding.
	A full attendance of children with many of
their parents, also Sister E. C. Clawson from
Salt Lake City, were present, on the occasion.
After singing prayer was offered by Brother
Wheeler, followed by singing "In our lovely
Deseret." Minutes and report of last annual
meeting read and approved, which gave credit
to the association. The programme was
promptly responded to, and encouraging and
interesting remarks were made by Sister E.C.
Clawson, Sister M. Rawlins, Brother Wheeler,
Brother Howe and President Elizabeth Davis.
All seemed to enjoy the meeting. The closing
prayer was offered by Bro. R. Howe.

SOUTH COTTONWOOD.

	The eighteenth annual meeting held at
South Cottonwood, May 18th, 1885, President

[Column 2]
Janette Y. Cahoon presiding. Singing. Pray-
er by Bishop Rawlius. Singing. Minutes of
last annual meeting and yearly report read
and approved.
	The President, on account of having moved
away from the ward and not being able to at-
tend her meetings, wished to resign her office.
Sisters M. I. Horne. E. S. Taylor and the
Bishop being present, it was motioned and
unanimously sustained that we accept her
resignation. A vote of thanks was rendered
for her long service and faithfulness in the dis-
charge of her duties, and her many good ex-
amples set forth for us to appreciate and follow
after. Therefore the Society was necessarily
reorganized. Counselor Mary Rawlins was
nominated Presient; Ann Wheeler and Phebe
Boyce; Couselors; Harriet A. Walker, Secre-
tary; Ann Boyce, Assistant Secretary; Ellen
Wheeler, Treasurer, in the place of Francis
Litson, deceased, who faithfully performed that
office from 1871 until her demise, Feb. 17th,
1885. All the officers were unanimously sus-
tained by the assembly.
	Our meeting was well attended, and we re-
ceived excellent instructions from those who
addressed us, the principal speakers being Sis-
ters Horne and Taylor. Our meeting was
adjourned until the 17th of next May. Dis-
missed with singing and prayer.
		HARRIET A. WALKER, SECRETARY. 

			JUAN STATE.

	The Relief Society held its quarterly confer-
ence at Levan in the new R. S. Hall, on the
13th inst. The reports, both written and ver-
bal, were very encouraging and spoke in high
praise of the vast amount of labor that had
been accomplished during the past quarter.
	The sisters seem to be awake to the sense of
their duty, and feel a deep interest in the wel-
fare of each other, and desire to obey the
principles of their holy religion, and serve
their God with one accord.
	Words of instruction and counsel were given
by the brethren and sisters who presided, which
was listened to with marked attention, and I
trust we will long remember the timely ad-
vice and counsel given.
	The sisters of Levan, with the help of the
brethren, have recently completed a very com-
fortable hall, which will be used for a meeting
house, for the present, as well as for the Relief
Society of that branch.
	The branches of the Society throughout the
Stake have been active, both in donating and
administering to the wants of the needy, and
comforting the sick. Their efforts to do good
to their fellow creatures have been crowned
with success, and the blessing of the Lord
has attended their labor of love.
	The Relief Society of Nephi celebrated the
anniversary of its organization on Tuesday,
the 23rd inst., and had a very enjoyable time,
listeing to addresses, songs and recitations,
given by the brethren and sisters.
		M. HAYTE, Stake Secretary.

		ST. GEORGE STAKE

The Relief Society and Y. L. M. I. A. of St.
George Stake held their quarterly conference
in the Lyceum, St. George, on Friday, the
12th of June ult. There was a good attend-
ance. The reports from the various settle-
ments were very encouraging. There is evi-
dently a great awakening among the Saints, a
closer union, and firmer faith in God our
Heavenly Father. The sisters are on hand
for the fulfillment of any duty, and desire to
promote and uphold the interests of the King-
dom of God; they have no fears as to the final
outcome of the persecutions by which the
faithful are at present being tried; nor do they
feel in the least disposed to retreat in their obe-
dience to any of the principles which have been
revealed from heaven.


[Column 1]

﻿	The young ladies are improving, and some
are gaining for themselves testimony of the
truth of the Gospel as revealed to the Prophet
Joseph in these last days.
	The Primary Conference was held on the
13th. Quite a number of the parents, as well
as the children, were present, and were ad-
dressed by President John D. T. McAllister,as
well as the Stake President of the Primary As-
sociation, Sister Julia H. Ivins, and others.
The children seem to appreciate the instruct-
tion given to them in their Primary meetings,
and take a lively interest in preparing their
exercises and rendering them in a creditable
manner.
	May the Lord bless the youth of Zion, and
give those to whose care their training is en-
trusted the faithfulness and perseverance neces-
sary for their important duties.
		M. A. G. BAKER.

UTAH STAKE.
	The Relief Society of Utah County met in
conference May 29th, 1885, President Mary
John presiding, with Counselor Emily G. Cluff.
Present, of the Priesthood, Apostles John
Henry Smith and F. M. Lyman; President A.
O. Smoot, Counselor . D. John and others.
Visitors from Salt Lake City, Sisters E. B.
Wells and Lucy W. Kimball. 
	Conference opened by singing and prayer.
Minutes of former conference read and ap-
proved. The resignation of Sister Rhoda
Lyman, Second Counselor to President Mary
John, was read and accepted; a vote of thanks
was also given Sister Lyman for her services.
The presidents of the various branches of the
Relief Society throughout the county were
called upon to report the respective organiza-
tions over which they presided. All that were
present responded in a very satisfactory man-
ner, reporting, as a general thing,faith and good
works, and a desire to live humble and prayer-
ful, and remain faithful to the end, that they
might gain salvation in the world to come.
	Sister E. B. Wells made many comforting
and encouraging remarks to the sisters, ex-
horting them to faithfulness in every duty, es-
pecially in the training of their children, etc.
thought we need not be cast down, we have
plenty of bright days to look forward to, etc.
	Apostle John H. Smith spoke to the sisters
for a short time, when, after singing and
prayer, conference adjourned till two p.m.
	Afternoon session. Conference met per ad-
journment. Opened with singing and prayer.
President A. O. Smoot said he would now pro-
ceed to fill the vacancy caused by the resigna-
tion of Sister Rhoda Lyman, and as Sister
John had chosen Sister Marilla M. Daniels to be
her Second Counselor, he would call a vote to
that effect. The vote was then called and car-
ried unanimously. President Smoot spoke in
terms of approbation of the sisters of the Re-
lief Society, of their willingness at all times
to respond to all calls made upon them by the
Priesthood, etc.
	Counselors Cluff and Daniels spoke a few
words; also Sisters E. B. Wells and L. W.
Kimball, followed by Apostle F. M. Lyman,
with very instructive remarks. Altogether
our conference was a feast to all, and the Spirit
of God was with us.
	President John made the closing remarks,
endorsing all that had been said, and praying
the Lord to bless each and every one.
	Conference adjourned for three months.
Singing. Benediction by F. M. Lyman.
		C. DANIELS, Secretary.

RICHMOND, CACHE CO.
	The sixth annual meeting of the Richmond
Primary Association was held in the new
meeting house, June 1st, 1885, at two p.m.
There were present Sisters Presendia L. Kim-

[Column 2]

ball and Margaret P. Young of Salt Lake
City; Adaline Barber and Martha Williams of
Smithfield, besides several other visitors; the
ward authorities, Sister Myra Merrill, Prest. of
the Relief Society, her Counselors and a great
many of the parents of the children.
	After the opening exercises the minutes of
the fifth annual meeting were read and ap-
proved; also the statistical and financial reports
of the past year.
	The officers for the ensuing year were pre-
sented by Bishop Wm. L. Skidmore as follows:
Mary Allsop, President; Mary Monson and
Maggie W. Thompson, Counselors; Satie J.
Petty, Secretary; Jane Hyer, Assistant Secre-
tary; Byrdie Harrison, Treasurer; Libbie A.
Merrill, Organist. All were unanimously sus-
tained. The programme, consisting of songs,
recitations, addresses, etc., was very well ren-
dered.
	Jane Hyer, in behalf of the members of the
Association, presented Sister Mary Allsop with
a beautiful dress pattern, with materials ready
for making up, accompanied by the following
address:
	To our beloved President, Sister Mary Allsop.
	We, the members of the Primary Associa-
tion, take pleasure in expressing our love and
good will toward you on this occasion, for the
great interest you have taken in us, and request
you to accept this small present as a token of
our love and esteem. May the blessings of
peace, comfort and health attend you, are the
wishes of the members of this Association.
	Sister Allsop was taken entirely by sur-
prise, and responded with a brief address of
thanks.
	Sisters Barber, Kimball, Young and Wil
liams, Bishop Wm. L. Skidmore and Counsel-
or Hyer made short addresses, encouraging
the children to be diligent in their labors in
the Association.
	After closing exercises meeting adjourned
for one year.
	Financial and Statistical report for the
year beginning June first, 1884, and ending
June 1st, 1885. Officers, 7; members, 205;
total, 212; average attendance, 110; meetings
held, 49; cash in treasury from previous year,
$1.25; cash received from members during the
year, $8.15; cash received from Primary enter-
tainment, $64; total, $73.40. Disbursements.
To new meeting house, $53.25; expended for
Primary entertainment, 50; emigration fund,
$3; martyr's fund, $2; paid for cards, $4.75; to
Stake Presidency, 50; Primary hymn books
and writing materials, $1.15; total expended,
$65.15; cash on hand, $8.25; property on hand,
$5.		MAGGIE W. THOMPSON,
			Secretary pro. tem.

NOTES AND NEWS.
	“Elizabeth Fry is to have a monument'.”
