column in a newspaper of Richmond, Ky.

	“Sixteen women graduated this year from the
medical department of' the Iowa State Univer-
sity."

	"Miss Blanche Blanchard has excuted and
exhibited a fine crayon picture of Hon C. A.
Burke."

"Mrs. Warner, a sister of the late W. Brandt
Storer, has on exhibition at Doll & Richards'
a finely executed plaster basrelief of George
Eliot."

	“A California lady has discovered a process
for preserving the juices of citrous fruits with
their natural flavor and without fermenta-
tion”


"The Empress Eugenie is to witness the
marriage of Beatrice and Prince Henry of
Battenberg, by special invitation of the
Queen."
	"It was the son and namesake of Mr. Lin-
coln's old antagonist, Stephen A. Douglas, who
said in his speech on Memorial Day: 'God
never made a better man than Abraham Lin-
coln.' "
	"Silk culture will be a feature of summer
work at the Woman's Prison at Sherborn,
Mass., where the superintendent, Mrs. Ellen
Johnson, will devote particular attention to
it."
	"The bill requiring temperance, physiology,
and hygiene to be taught in the public schools
was passed in the Massachusetts House of Re-
presentatives last Thursday by a vote of 181
to 1. The women who have worked so faith-
fully for that excellent measure deserve great
credit for this unexpected triumph."
	"The Woman's National Press Association
was organized at New Orleans, May 13th, 1885.
Mrs. E. I. Nicholson, editor and proprieter of
the New Orleans Picayune, was elected presi-
dent; Mrs. Marian McBride, of the Boston
Post, secretary. Vice-presidents recently
elected are Mrs. Florence Adkinson, of the
Indianapolis Sentinel, Mrs. L. M. Pavy. of the
St. Louis Republican, Miss Mary McMullen, of
London, of the Anglo-American Times.

	Patents have been issued to women during
the week ending May 19. as follows:
	Henrietta H. Dangerfield, Harrisburg, Va.,
Adjustable Reversible Foot-rest.
	Mary J. Holt, San Fancisco, Cal., Meat
and Provision Safe.
	Carrie F. Marsh, Detroit, Mich., Adjustable
Clothing Stiffener.
	Annie E. Sawyer, Charlestown, Mass., Skirt
Protector.


VERIFICATION OF AN ANCIENT
		PROVERB

	The following prophecy is said to have been delivered by
a British bard, in the time of William the Norman, and
preserved by some monkish annalist, viz[-]
	That no more than three monarchs in direct succession
should ever again reign over these kingdoms without some
violent interruption.
	Following is the succession of sovereigns:
	I. William the Norman, 2 William Rufus, 3 Henry the
First: interrupted by the usurpation of Stephen.
	I Henry the Second, 2 Edward the First, 3 Edward the
Second; interrupted by the abdication and murder of
Edward the Second.
	I. Edward the Third, 2 Richard the Second; interrupted
by the deposition of that monarch.
 	I. Henry the Fourth, 2 Henry the Fifth, 3 Henry the
Sixth; interrupted by the restoration of the House of
York.
I Edward the Fourth, 2 Edward the Fifth, 3 Richard the
Third; interrupted by the usurpation of Henry Richmond.
	I Henry the Seventh, 2 Henry the Eight, 3 Edward the
Sixth; interrupted by the election of Lady Jane Gray.
	I Mary, 2 Elizabeth; a foreign king (James of Scotland)
called in to assume the crown. 
	I James the First, 2 Charles the First; interrupted by the
deposition of that monarch, and the establishment of an-
other form of government, in the person of Oliver Crom-
well.
	I Charles the Second; 2 James the Second, interrupted
by the abdication of that king, and the election of a 
foreigner.
	I William the Third, 2 Anne; interrupted by the parlia-
mentary appointment of a foreigner.
	I George the First, 2 Goorge the Second, 3 George the
Third, interrupted by the unfortunate incapacity of that
sovereign, and a parliamentary appointment for exercising
the sovereign, in the person of the Prince Regent.
	I George the Fourth, 2 William the Fourth, 3 Victoria
the First, whom may God bless; but what is to be the next
interruption?—Selected
					AUGUSTA JOYCE CROCHERON


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


IN MEMORIAM.

	Our Nellie, twin daughter of Andrew S. and M. E.
Gray died May 3rd, 1885, aged fourteen years, two months
and nineteen days.

			OUR NELLIE

	Spirit immortal, sweet spirit blest,
	Gone from all pain to the bright land of rest;
	Leaving a void that no other can fill,
	And a grief in our hearts that no power can still.

	Save He who did give and hath taken away
	Our Nellie to mansions of eternal day;
	And oh, may His Spirit heal up each sore heart,
	'Til we meet her again never, never to part.

	No purer e'er came from heaven to earth,
	To gladden the home and the family hearth;
	Silently weaving a mystical chain
	Of the chords of affection, for aye to remain.

	The Father hath gathered her back to His fold,
	And her dear face no more shall our sad eyes behold,
	'Til life's journey is ended, and we reach the shore
	Where sorrow and sadness and partings are o'er.

	Oh, choice the home circle gathering there,
	Unclouded, unstricken by anguish and care,
	And sweet will the meeting be there bye and bye,
	In a blessed reunion, where love cannot die,

	Of father and mother, Buddie and all,
	Who have gone at the voice of Jehovah's call,
	And now, oh! our darling, the call was for thee -
	Oh! help us, our Father, Thy purpose to see,

	And oh, speed the day when Thy kingdom shall come,
	When throughout our sad earth Thy will shall be done;
	When the grave shall give back our loved ones again,
	And Thine own great victory Christ shall proclaim.

										HOPE.


[Column 1]		
		OBITUARIES

	DIED, at Kanosh, Millard Co, Laura Moody Kimball,
daughter of William C. and Eliza Moody, and the wife of
Bishop A. A. Kimball, born in Salt Lake City, May 23rd
1859. Died April 6th, 1885.
	Her faith and works, reflected by a mild and tender 
disposition, endeared her to all who knew her, and her
memory warms the soul with celestial love, the order of
her birth and life.
			GEORGE CRANE.

DIED, at Mill Creek, June 7th, 1885, David, son of 
John F. and Elizabeth Snedaker, aged twelve years, eight
months and twenty-three days.
	The following resolutions were adopted at the Third 
District Primary of Mill Creek Ward.
	Whereas, Our Heavenly Father has seen fit to call from
our midst our dearly beloved young brother and associate,
be it
	Resolved, That we, the members of the Association, do
sympathize with Brother Snedaker and family in the loss of
their loved son and brother. David has the promise of a 
life beyond the grave, where sickness and death has no
power, where all is bright and beautiful.
	He was a faithful member of the Association, always
ready to respond to every call. We will miss him from 
our society, though we feel to say, "Thy will be done, O
Lord."
	Resolved, That we place this on our records, and pre-
sent a copy to the bereaved family, and one be sent to the
EXPONENT for publication.
		FRANCIS HANSON,
		ISABELL CARLISLE,
		KINDNESS WRIGHT,
			HADIE CARLISLE, Secretary

EDITOR EXPONENT:	
	We, the members of the Annabella Ward, Sevier Co.,
Utah are with much regret, compelled to inform you of
the death of our beloved sister and friend, Ann Sophia
Rollins Beckstead, wife of Edward K. Roberts, who de-
parted this life May 13th, 1885. She was born March 5th,
1831, in the State of Maine. Was baptized in November, 
1842, at the early age of eleven years.
	She passed through many of the persecutions and heart-
rending scenes of the Church; was in the drivings from 
Nauvoo, and was compelled to cross the Mississippi River
in a canoe. She afterwards returned to Nauvoo, living

[Column 2]
with the mother of the Prophet, and witnessed the burning
of the Nauvoo Temple.
	Sister Rollins came to Council Bluffs in 1849, and was 
married to Sidney Beckstead in 1850. She crossed the 
plains in 1852, and passed through many hardships and
privations incident to the early settlement of Utah.
	In 1864 she lost her husband, by his accidently shooting
himself, when assisting to bring out the emigration. She 
was married to her present husband in March, 1869.
	During her last days all he children were present—five
daughters and the youngest child, a son, nearly fourteen
years of age. Her disease was pulmonary consumption.
The fondest attention was tendered her, during her dis-
tressing and fatal illness of five months duration.
	She was Second Counselor to the President of the
Annabella Ward Relief Society, and First Counseler to
the President of the Primary Association, and was dearly
beloved by all the members of both associations, for the
faithful discharge and performaece of every duty, by
which she won many tokens of their love and esteem.
	The people of the ward, and for miles around, attended
her funeral services, in token of esteem for the worthy and 
faithful worker in the places to which she had been called
so well. 
	Her last days were those of great suffering, and she
died firm to the cause of truth, and in the hope of a glori-
ous resurrection. Her last work was mostly with the
Primary, as her mind was so much upon their develop-
ment and success.
	The following Resolutions of Respect were unanimously
adopted by the Society:
	That, As in the Providence of God our most worthy
sister has been called to a higher and happier sphere, to 
perform a labor of love in heaven, as she had done here
upon earth, be it
	Resolved, That we, the sisters of Annabella Ward, do
greatly mourn the loss of our beloved sister, and sincerely
tender our heartfelt sympathy to the many relatives and
friends, who are called upon to lose mother, wife, sister
and friend, and with them we weep and acknowledge the
hand of an Almighty God, but are greatly consoled in
knowing that the Saintly sleeper enjoys the rest of the 
faithful, and the reunion with those she loved on Earth. We
also look forth to the time when the grave will give up its 
dead, and the earth again will be brtened with the
presence of the redeemed. And, be it
	Resolved, That a copy of this notice and resolutions be
forwarded to the EXPONENT for publication.
		EMMA STAFFORD, President.
		ELLEN GARDNER, Counselor.
		ANN THURSTON, Secretary.


MISCELLANEOUS.

	He who can suppress a moment’s anger may
prevent a day of sorrow.—Ex.

	My thoughts are my own possession; my
acts may be limited to my country’s laws.— G.
Forster.

	God not only reigns, but rules. The uni-
verse is His kingdom; eternity is His term of
office.—H.H. Tucker.

	The fortunate circumstances of our lives
are generally found at last to be of our own
producing.—Goldsmith.

	Beyond all doing of good is the being good
for he that is good not only does good things,
but all that he does, is good.—Ex.

	God has so arranged the chronometry of our
spirits that there shall be thousands of silent
moments between the striking hours.—Ex.

							The Rights of the Women of Zion, and the Rights of the Women of  all Nations.
VOL. 14.								SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, JULY 15, 1885.


MY LUNAR LETTER.

Something I've lost, that threw o'er life a charm,
	Something that fed me with "convenient food,"
That kept the springs of life both fresh and warm,
	Something that helped to keep me rich and good.

A lunar letter - only just a letter!
	But such a one as few sit down to write,
That summon'd a response that made me better,
By teaching me with wisdom to indite

A long, sweet poem e'er was traced
	Upon those lunar sheets for me;
That time nor distance e'er defaced,
	But only made our souls more free.

We spoke too meaningly to speak amiss,
	We meant too well to dream we could offend;
Each heart was laden with its own sweet bliss.
	And as the thought arose was quickly penn'd.

And thus, though distance severed, we were near,
	Though sight, or touch, or hearing all were mute;
And thus we traveled on from year to year,
	Striking sweet chords upon the heart's own lute!

Oh, can it be? my heart cannot
	Receive as truth the bitter word -
Three decades saw me ne'er forgot -
	'Tis fit thro' silence it is heard,

Silence that speaks! alas! alas!
	'Tis vain to doubt the bitter truth
Life's sweet elixir ne'er can pass
	Again my lip, preserving youth.

Yes, all is silent now, the lute unstrung.
	The hand has dropt that struck the chords so long,
The song has ceased that was so sweetly sung,
	My lunar letters to the past belong.
							HANNAH T. KING.
Salt Lake City.


CHILDREN OF ZION.

Out of the cloud draped heights above
A watching spirit leaned,
Out from the throng of human souls
A heavy sheaf he gleaned,
For the harvest field of life was large,
And tares in the wheat were screened.

The angel saw where'er he looked,
On faces young or old.
A look that his faultless spirit knew,
And ever the same it told -
Love of pleasure and love of self
And love of the shining gold.

Over the lonely cry and prayer,
In homes made desolate,
Over the reckless song and oath
Behind the prison gate,
The song and laugh of the thoughtless world,
Heartless and deaf as fate.

For in the eastern skies arose
Dark clouds that nearer drew,
Afar the muffled thunder rolled,
Or lightnings flashed to view;
Yet still no warning ear they gave,
And folly faster flew.

While those who'd fed them "bread of life"
Tasted the prison fare,
These feasted on earth's choicest food,
Flaunting in garments rare;
And e'en fraternal greetings change
With such as placed them there.

O heedless children of the Saints!
How long for you must wait
The patience of deserted love


That guardeth long and late?
Return, the dark hour cometh on
And swings the Shepherd's gate.

O, hasten home, ye heedless lambs,
Listen the Shepherd's voice;
While thunders roll and foes may rave,
Safe in the fold rejoice;
And answer, guile, or threat, or wound,
"The kingdom is our choice."
	AUGUSTA JOYCE CROCHERON.
South Bountiful, July 2nd, 1885.


[For the EXPONENT-]

EXPECTANCY.

[ORIGINAL.]

Autumn past, bold winter deepens,
	Still I watch and wait in vain,
Yearning in my human weakness
	For his coming once again.
Spring came tardy, drear and gloomy,
	Bringing scarce one ray to cheer,
Now 'tis past in all its beauty,
	Yet I wait his voice to hear.

June, the first glad month of summer, 
	Waneth toward the last fair day—
Still I'm waiting for his coming.
	Though he wanders far away.
Messengers of earth have failed me,
	Still hope whispers, "Do not fear!"
As thine heart for love is yearning,
	So thy dear one's draweth near.

Came the meeting and the parting,
	Darknes lifted, light passed on,
Like unto the	swift appearing.
	Of a meteor at dawn.

As from fairest fields of Eden,	
	Floateth echoes of a song,
Like the rainbow arch at even,
	Bringing gladness after storm.

So we clasp love's priceless token,
	Mark the summer passing by;
Flowers bloom, and fruitage golden,
	Singing o'er "sweet bye and bye."

		L. M. HEWLINGS.
July, 1885.


[Column 1]

SILENT COMMUNINGS.

	My other self and I have had a long collo-
quy this evening upon many deep and impor-
tant subjects,upon which it is good to ruminate.
I believe the meditations were caused by
attending, a few days before, Doctor Ellis
Shipp's lecture.
	After we ceased our cogitation, for the
time being, I tried to recall some of the ideas,
and make them palpable for my own especial
benefit, and, perhaps, for some others who may
read them, so I promptly summoned my pri-
vate secretary (my pen), who is always within
reach of my hand, and I, dictating, we set to
work to make palpable some of these reflec-
tions. We entered upon them by observing
what a marvelous piece of mechanism the hu-
man organism presents to the eyes of the
astonished beholder! When we began to study
it, whether in the mental or corporeal, we
found both equally startling in their complex
machinery; we decided that here was visible the
very hand of God! the grand architect and
builder of this wonderful and mysterious struc-
ture; we felt that anatomy ought to be certain-

[Column 2]

ly one of the studies of the human mind, as it
is one of the essentials of self-knowledge.
	When attending a lecture of this kind, the
intelligent mind is shocked at its own igno-
rance, and at once determines to enter upon
an anatomical study, by attending lectures and
by reading and reflection, that we may, at any
rate, gain an insight of how "fearfully and
wonderfully we are made." Not only the
grand structure is to be studied, but we mnst
pass into the secret chambers of that mysteri-
ous organism, and there behold how intense
has been the almighty fashioner, that nothing
should be wanting for the happiness and com-
fort of this being, "only a little lower than the
angels"—there we shall behold the minute,but
most important germs of immortal life, which
we need to behold through a microscope in
their minuteness, yet all in a grand perfection,
complete, essential and practical. This is a
temple that all should enter, taking "their
shoes from off their feet," and the finger on the
lip, for it is "holy ground," for the observing,
thinking and devout mind realizes that God
himself is there! Blind indeed must be the
eye, and dark as Erebus the mind that cannot
discern Him in this mysterious form and struc-
ture. On entering into the presence of these
sublime mysteries, the ermine falls to the
ground, the purple robe is forgotten, the jewels
become dim, feeling we stand in nudity in
that awful presence; not humbled, in the com-
mon acceptation of the term, but simply
silenced by a grand and stupendous mystery!
Thought prompts the conviction that the Al-
mighty Maker has fashioned and wrought out
that sublime, that beautiful structure for an
immortal existence. Yes, immortality is im-
printed upon every part of it, because every
part is perfection!
	And what are the meditations that follow
this inspection? Do I not behold myself in
beholding this? Does it not hold up to the re-
viewer a mirror on which is written thyself? and
have we been ignorant of the perfection God
has given us? and do we treasure it as the
temple of the Holy One? do we never abuse it,
and wilfully or thoughtlessly injure, or perhaps
destroy it as a thing of naught? We look
abroad and see deformity in this once perfect
structure, caused, perhaps, by accident, perhaps
by wickedness in persons, or their progenitors.
Oh! how sad, how mournful, after beholding
perfection, to turn our eyes upon disease and
distortion! God, forgive all Thy pure eyes see
amiss in Thine erring creatures; enable them
to see wherein they err, and to increase in
knowledge, wisdom and understanding, that
above all things they may never desecrate the
perfect temple which Thou hast fashioned for
their immortal spirit to dwell in, while it so-
journs on Thine earth in this probation. And
well may we believe, and know that this per-
fect form, with its immaculate, its sublime, its
holy and mysterious apparatus cannot perish,
but within its most secret chambers lies the
hidden spark of the eternal fire, from off the
eternal altar of our God, which cannot be ex-
tinguished; though the corruptible must put
on incorruption, that eternal spark shall never
be dimmed, and shall again be ignited by the
breath of the Eternal and Omnipotent Archi-
tect, and shall have power to attract the parti-
cles pertaining to it, and will again stand forth
in its own grand identity and immortal being.
	The apostle of old said, "Know ye not that
your bodies are the temple of the Holy


Ghost?" Let us often recall these words, and
meditate fondly on their solemn meaning, es-
pecially when we look around and behold so
much to make the righteous mourn in the reck-
lessness, the thoughtlessness, the apathy—not
to speak of the willfully, the daringly wicked,
we feel to exclaim like one of old, "Who, then,
can be saved?" but the ever loving voice of the
Savior is heard in answer, "With men it is im-
possible, but with God all things are possible."
Truly may it be said, "God is Love." By
this blessed knowledge is our faith and hope
sustained and kept alive, and we arise renewed
and refreshed, and go on our way rejoicing.
		HANNAH T. KING.
	Salt Lake City, July, 1885.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.﻿


[Column 1]

AUTOBIOGRAPHY

OF EMILY D. P. YOUNG.

Continued.

	Oh, Lord look down in mercy upon Thy
people who are afflicted and oppressed.
	How long, O Lord, wilt Thou suffer the
enemy to oppress Thy Saints. Destruction
hath come upon us like a whirlwind, in the
which Thou hast verified thy words, for Thou
didst forewarn us that it should come, and be-
hold, Thy word is fulfilled. The enemy came
upon us to drive us from the State of Missouri,
or exterminate us. But Thou, O Lord, did
stay their hands from killing us, though num-
bers were massacred; and Thou didst send forth
uncommon severe frost and snow, and by that
means save us, as a people, from being driven
out at the time appointed. But Thou didst
suffer the enemy, unlawfully, to take Thy ser-
vant, together with scores of others, who drove
us like dumb asses from our homes, in a cold,
dreary and melancholy time. We were con-
fined in a large, open room, where the cold
northern blast penetrated freely. Our fires
were small, and our allowance for wood and
food scanty. They gave us not even a
blanket to lie upon. Our beds were the cold
floor.
	There, Thou didst suffer the wicked to tyran-
ize over us; yea, the vilest of the vile did
guard us and treat us like dogs; yet we bore
our oppressions without murmuring; but our
souls were vexed both night and day with
their filthy conversation, for they constantly
blasphemed Thy holy name.
	How long. O Lord, wilt Thou suffer them to
blaspheme Thy name? Wilt Thou not soon
cut them off and consign them their portion
among hypocrites and unbelievers? In the
midst of our oppressions we did call upon Thy
name, O Lord, and Thou didst hear us, and
deliver us in some degree from the hand of
oppression, yet the enemy doth still threaten
us and would fain destroy us from the face of
the earth, but we are in Thy hands, O Lord,
and we know that the enemy can go no farther
in oppressing us than Thou dost permit. O
Lord, deliver Thou us from our oppressors.
Send Thy judgments and destroy those who are
not willing to let Thy Saints have a resting
place upon Thy footstool. Save Thy people,
O Lord, save Thy people from oppression and
bondage, yea, redeem Thy Zion; in Thine own
time redeem it.
	How long, O Lord, shall the enemy be per-
mitted to wear out Thy Saints? Hasten, O
hasten the day when the Ancient of Days shall
sit, and power be given Thy Saints to take and
possess the kingdom, even forever and ever.
Amen.	[Signed]
		E. PARTRIDGE.
	Far West, Caldwell Co., Mo., Jan., 1839.

	The brethren were prohibited, by the mob,
from going out, or coming into the city, but
many of the families living out had gathered

[Column 2]

in for protection, which caused provisions to be
very scarce, and much suffering ensued. The
mob had taken cattle, pigs, poultry, and what-
ever they wanted for their army to eat while
they were camped outside of the city, for all
know that even a mob must eat to live, if they
have to rob women and children of their last
morsel to do it. I know how it was in our
family—mother had to boil wheat to eke out
the little corn meal that we had; flour we had
none, and I remember seeing her make some
pumpkin pies, using corn meal for the crust; I
suppose she thought the corn dodger and
pumpkin sauce would taste better to the child-
ren if it was made in the shape of, and called
a pie. Some were worse off than we were, for
they had nothing but parched corn to eat.
Well, these were some of the mobocratic days
of my childhood.
	Father was released from prison and returned
to Far West, but in consequence of trouble
being brought upon him by apostate brethren,
he was compelled to again flee from his family
and home. Mother, soon after, put what she
could of her effects into a wagon and, with
her family, started for—well, anywhere out of
the State of Missouri.
	We were set down on the banks of the
Mississippi River, opposite Quincy, and were
again houseless and homeless, wandering in the
cold and bleak winter weather, with scanty
food and clothing. We pitched our tents and
waited for an opportunity to cross the river.
There were several families of Saints there
when we arrived, and they were continually
coming, so the bank of the river was dotted
with tents, now the only home of the again
exiled Saints. The wagons bringing families
were unloaded and taken back for more of the
Saints. When we crossed the river it was
partly on the ice and partly in the ferry boat.
The shore on the Quincy side of the river was
lined with the inhabitants of that place, to
witness the crossing over of the Mormon out-
casts—even the exiled Saints in midwinter.
Perhaps many thought they were a strange
people, or some kind of animals; not human
beings like themselves, subject to sorrow and
pain, cold and hunger and distress.
	In all our wanderings and being driven, we
have had to go out in the cold winter months,
and the suffering of the people must have been
very great. Children could not sense the awful
reality of the situation as older ones did, on
whose shoulders the burden rested. I some-
times look back upon those scenes with horror,
and wonder how the Saints did continue to en-
dure, time after time, such heartless cruelties.
But many could not endure, and so found an
early grave.
	And now, in 1885, nearly all of the Saints
that were living then have passed away, and
the few that are living now are those that were
children then, and they are becoming ad-
vanced in years, and it will not be very long
and there will be none left living upon the
earth to bear witness against the horrid deeds
of the Missouri mob. But the record of their
wicked deeds will remain and condemn them;
they will yet have to foot the bill with inter-
est.
	After crossing the river, mother rented a
room in Quincy, and here father met us. We
remained but a short time in Quincy. Father
moved his family to Pittsfield, Pike County,
Illinois, where we remained until a place for
the gathering of the Saints was appointed, when
we moved to Commerce, afterwards Nauvoo.
Father secured a city lot, and pitched his tent
under a large elm tree.
	The place was very unhealthy, and nearly
everybody there was sick; so much so that it
was hard to find well ones enough to care for
the sick, or bury the dead. As we had not
been there long enough to be affected by the
climate, father sent his girls, that were old

[Column 3]

enough, among the sick to help take care of
them. I was at Bro. Ebenezer Robinson's; he
and his wife were both sick. I staid as long
as I could keep up. I went home sick, took
my bed, from which I was unable to rise for
several weeks. My sister Harriet preceeded
me home, and was occupying one side of the
bed, very sick. We lay in this condition until
one day Brothers Young and Kimball called
at our tent; they were just starting on a mission,
and they administered to us, when the fever
broke, and we were much better, but we did
not get our strength.
	After a few weeks, being neither sick nor
yet well, we were taken down with the shaking
ague, which continued with us, off and on, for
one year or more. As the tent was an uncom-
fortable place for sick folks, father rented a
room, in what was called the "upper store
house," built at the steam-boat landing, before
the Saints began to settle there. Several fami-
lies occupied other portions of the house. Bro.
Hyrum Smith's family had a room adjoining
ours.
	Father had the chills and fever, but he felt
so anxious to build a house for his family,
which had to be done mostly by his own labor,
that he felt he could hardly spare time to be
sick, so he would take quinine and break up
the chills for a week or two, so that he could
labor on his house; and when the chills re-
turned he would take more quinine and go to
work again; but he saw that it would take a
long time at this rate to get into his house, so
he concluded to build a stable for his cows and
move his family into that; but moving was the
last thing he ever did in this life. He died on
the 27th of May, 1840, in his forty-seventh
year. My sister Harriet died a few days be-
fore, in the store house, on the 16th of May,
1840, in her nineteenth year.
	I will here mention the kindness of Brother
and Sister William Law to our family in our
distressed condition. While my father lay
sick, my sister Eliza and I, and some of the
other children were sick also, and it was very
unpleasant for so many sick to be in one small
room. Brother and Sister Law took Eliza
and I home with them and showed us every
kindness. I felt as though I had almost got to
heaven, after all the years of suffering that we
had endured, and now to be in such a good
house, and to have a comfortable bed to lay
upon, with nourishing and palatable food, I
almost thought that it was too pleasant to be
true.
	After father's death Brother Law took our
whole family home and administered to our
wants, and with such good and kind care we
began to improve in health, and when we had
sufficiently regained our health we went back
into our little hut once more.
	When I think of the Laws, and what good
men they seemed to be, and realize the course
they have taken, my soul sorrows and mourns
over their fate. Can it be possible that such
kindness as they extended to my father's family
will be all lost?

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


AFFECTION.

Affection is like a flower,
	Sweet and precious,
Cultivated, rears its head.
	Gay and gracious.

Uncultivated, still it thrives
	With modest mien,
Happy, sun-kissed, and wet
	With heaven's rain.

The showy, pampered blossom
	Flings wide its power,
Lives, in passionate delight,
	It's sensuous hour.

But pluck the common weed,
	Unfold its story;
Inhale its perfumed breath—
	Behold its glory!

		HOMESPUN.


HOW THE GOSPEL CAME	TO ME.

When first the elders to us came,
	I did not want them here,
But very soon I learned from them
	Some precious truths most dear.
They left some little tracts with me,
	I read them with much care,
They taught to me salvation free,
	And banished all my fear.

I also read some other books--
	They all appeared so strange—
I could not find in them one fault.
	But much instruction gained.
I thought that I would ask a place
	Among the Saints of God,
Unworthiness would then arise
	And say, "You'd better not."

If ever I on earth did pray
	It surely was that time,
That God would lead me in the way.
	My duty then to find;
I  laid myself upon the bed,
	Poured out my soul in prayer,
And asked that God would bless me now
	With understanding clear.

I fell asleep, and dreamed a dream--
	I cannot tell it all--
But some one spoke, and said, "Turn thou
To the writings of St. Paul."
I was directed to the Hebrews,
	Epistle number two:
I read and trembled like a leaf,
	And knew not what to do.

It said to "give more earnest heed
	To things which we have heard;"
So now I thought I'd try to do
	Just what the Lord had said;
And surely now I must obey
	The Gospel of the Lord:
I knew that I had never been
"A doer of His word."

Soon after this I was baptized,
	By Elder O. A. Bates,
And ever more I hope to be
	A meek and humble Saint.
I thank the Lord for sending men
	To teach the truth to me.
They preach, "Repent of every sin,"
	And gain sweet liberty.

God sent His angels to the earth 
	With the Gospel's glorious plan,
Salvation to proclaim, once more,
	To lost, rebellious man.
The Lord has power on the earth
	To care for "Abra'm's seed,"
And every blessing give to them
	That in this life they need.

And now, dear friends, will you reject 
	The Gospel Jesus taught?
I hope you will obey His word--
	'Tis certain that you ought.

Come, then, arise and be baptized,
	And wash away your sins:
All honest souls I would advise
To obey their Lord and King.

But oh! I know I am too weak
	To give advice to you;
I know I need myself to ask
	More of His love to know.
Then pray for me, my brethren dear,
	And help meon the way;
Yea, ask the Lord to strengthen me,
	And guide me day by day.

And sisters, I would ask of you
	Remember me each day,
And ask the Lord to ever lead
	And teach me how to pray.
My Father who art in heaven,
	My every sin forgive,
Oh! hid me cease to wander,
	And let me with Thee live.
				M.J. MCNIECE
Mississippi.


[Column 1]

THE EDMUNDS BILL.

	To say that the Edmunds Bill has become a
law does not signify that it is a constitutional
law. He may just as well draft a bill making
it a penalty of fine and imprisonment to be-
lieve in the Bible, for every sane man knows
that the people of God practised polygamy,
and they were commanded so to do according
to the history of Abraham, and Jacob, who
served seven years for Rachel, the mother of
Joseph, who was sold into Egypt, which is
proof positive that God-sanctioned and blessed
this union of polygamous marriage. Has God
changed? Who has the authority to say He
has? Surely Mr. Edmunds has never read his
Bible, for it says He changeth not; and Jesus
says, "Judge not; with that judgment you
judge, you shall be judged." History repeats
itself; the same cause produces the same effect.
The imprisonment of such men as President
Cannon, and other righteous men with whom
we are well acquainted, and know them to be
the highest type of morality, walking in the
fear of God, with the greatest reverence for
the Constitution of our once free and happy
country, holding it as a sacred instrument,
given by inspiration, purchased with the noblest
blood of our veteran fathers. Can those who
signed the Declaration of Independence look
down and see that sacred, heaven-born boon
of freedom violated, trampled under foot, with-
out exerting all their influence, yes, all the
energies of their soul to save it, when it was
purchased at such a price? No; they may tear
it into shreds, they may burn it into ashes,
but they cannot destroy it. The same power
that organized and brought it forth can restore
the elements, bring them together again, raise
up mighty men filled with the spirit of the
patriots, whose prayers will ascend up to God
—give us liberty or death.
	Has this ceased to be a Republican Govern-
ment? Is this a land of liberty? Look with-
in the prison walls of Detroit, Salt Lake City
and Yuma, and you will find those there who
have never violated one single constitutional
law, without a shadow of evidence to convict
them. Shall we publish it to the world that
women, too, have been imprisoned for refusing
to answer illegal and insulting questions, such
as never were heard of before in any respect-
able court? They followed the example of our
Savior, when He was arraigned before the
judges, He opened not His mouth.
	If Mary, the mother of Christ, was here
they would imprison her, for 'tis nowhere re-
corded that she told who was the father of her
child, but there were many witnesses that said
He was the Son of God, although the angel of
the Lord appeared unto Joseph, unto whom
she was espoused, to justify her, and they fled
into the hilly country of Judea, yet we have
no account of her imprisonment.

[Column 2]

	In the days of our Savior, when the Apostle
Peter was imprisoned, we read that the disciples
prayed continually, and there came an angel
by night and delivered him out of prison; as
also in the case of Paul and Silas, the inter-
position of the Almighty delivered them. Up-
on reading the biographical sketch of Presi-
dent Cannon, and the defence of A. Milton
Musser, how forcibly we were reminded of the
noble and sublime defence made by that an-
cient and mighty apostle, when the governor
was led to exclaim, "Paul, thou almost per-
suadest me to be a Christian." What was the
answer? "Would to God thou wast, most
noble Festus, even such as I, excepting these
bonds." This great and mighty apostle, who
had persecuted the Saints, even unto death, had
seen a vision, heard a voice, and testified that
Jesus was the Christ. For this cause they
were persecuted, even unto death, in those days.
The same cause produces the same effect. Now,
to be a traitor to our country is considered
worthy of death; how much more so to be a
traitor to our God, who gives testimonies and
manifestations to make us witnesses of His
power and the plan of salvation.
	Now, we say unto our brethren who are in
prison, "Be of good cheer; the same spirit and
power that persecuted those ancient worthies, is
the power that is arraigned against you, and is
proof positive that you are engaged in the
same cause. There are ten thousand daily
prayers ascending up before the throne of God
for your deliverance, and well do we know
that God can deliver you, but suffers these
persecutions to fill up the cup of their ini-
quity."
	Some of the ladies are talking of getting up
a petition to the chief executive of the nation
to have the prisons built large enough for all
the religious world, as they all express a desire
to go to Abraham's bosom. We hope our
brethren who are in prison will appeal their
case to the courts on high, that the recording
angels may bear record of the proceedings and
sentence passed upon those innocent men that
God, angels and patriots may hear and know
and take action upon their case, that the cries
of the fatherless and the widow may come up
into the ears of the Lord against those that
have caused this sorrow ond oppression to come
upon the innocent. The religion of the Lord
Jesus is founded upon facts; it is no fable, and
this Government will have to account for the
persecution of the Latter-day Saints.
	The President of this Church is a living
witness to-day of the martyrdom of our Prophet
and Patriarch. He can testify that on the
night of the martyrdom the Prophet asked
him to sing these words:

	In prison I saw him next,—condemned
	To meet a traitor's doom at morn;
	The tide of lying tongues I stemmed,
	And honored him mid shame and scorn.

	My friendship's utmost zeal to try,
	He asked if I for him would die,
	The flesh was weaky, my blood ran chill,
	But the free spirit cried, "I will!"

	Then in a moment to my view,
	The stranger started from disguise,
	The tokens in his hands I knew,
	The Savior stood before mime eyes.

	He spake, and my poor name he named—
	"Of me thou hast hot been ashamed;
	These deeds shall thy memorial be;
	Fear not thou didst them unto me.

	President Taylor offered to die for the
Prophet. He stood between him and death in
fulfillment of the words he had sung but a few
hours before the massacre.
		W. EAST.


"It is the fashion to name children after
flowers in Virginia. That is probably because
they all start out as Virginia creepers."
