SALT LAKE CITY,					JULY 15 1855.


[Column 1]

A FEW FACTS.

	Every few days some one or two or more of the
brethren, men of position in the Church, are ar-
rested and brought before the United States Com-
missioner and bound over to appear at the next 
term of court, and in many instances whole fami-
lies are called up and questioned, and also placed
under bonds to appear as witnesses when the case
shall come up for hearing. This has been going
on for several months, and, during that time, some
have been tried by the courts, and sentenced, and
are suffering the penalty of imprisonment for
keeping a law which Congress has made a crime,
but neither the motive or the spirit which actuated
the person, can by any means be interpreted as 
evil, nor can the actual facts be considered sinful,
except under the proscriptive law made specially
for this people. Angus M. Cannon, President of this
Stake of Zion,Elders R. Clawson, A. M. Musser, J.
Watson, P. P. Pratt and Evans have all been tried
and sent to the Penitentiary from Salt Lake, and
from Ogden, F. A. Brown, Moroni Brown and
Job Pingree; each of these for living with and ac-
knowledging the wives given them in the holy
covenant of celestial marriage, solomnized by
men holding the authority to perform the cere-
mony.
	These men have all been exemplary in their
lives, and naught can be brought,against them as
honest and true men, only this, that believing im-
plicitly that God had commanded them, they have
obeyed this higher law, a law too high for the
comprehension of men, who are seeking only the
pleasures and riches of this world, or that which is
temporal. Indeed it cannot be expected that they
will understand in their present condition.
	We were very forcibly struck by the ruling of 
Judge Powers in the case of Job Pingree. These
are the men who talk so much of free agency and
liberty, and it would seem he tried to coerce Bro.
Pingree into saying he would not do certain things,
or actually force him to say he would give up a 
wife, or wives, with whom he had made the most
solemn covenants before God and angels that a
man can make. One cannot help thinking it were
better to die in prison than deny a principle he
knows to be from God. And all good men and
women, everywhere, will respect a man more who
will stand by his colors in the hour of peril, even
though it cost him his life, than one who could be
pursuaded to renounce a principle because he was
brought to a severe test. Surely the religion of
the Latter-day Saints is as good as that for which
the Saints of God in other ages have suffered and
died, and let it not be said of any Latter-day Saint
that he has not the courage of his convictions.
	The statements made in the court in the First
District by F. A. Brown and Moroni Brown of
Ogden must make an impression upon every
houest man and woman who will read them. Such
powerful testimonies cannot fail to have a potent
effect upon the young and lead them to reflect up-
on the principle and its practice, and upon the sa-
cred obligations of those who have embraced it,

[Column 2]

the purity of their motives, and the high and holy
aspirations that have influenced them to practi-
cally incorporate it in their lives. This persecu-
tion will arouse earnest thoughts and feelings in
regard to Mormonism at home and abroad, and
will create investigation and research into facts
and figures, that will increase the faith and
strength and courage of those who have been
born "Mormons," but thought little about it, and
among the honest everywhere, will produce a feel-
ing of sympathy and interest for those who are
persecuted on account of religious belief and
practice, but who are honest, upright, thrifty, in-
dustrious, temperate and God-fearing.
	Not only have men been immured in prisons,but
women also have had to suffer penalties for not
answering questions that were inconsistent and
improper to say the least. Our readers will re-
member the cases of Annie Gallifant,Belle Harris
and baby and of Nellie White. The more recent
cases were Lydia Spencer, Elizabeth Ann Starkey
and Lucy Devereux and baby. These women have
put themselves on record as true Latter-day Saints,
who knew for themselves what was right, and
were determined to remain steadfast to principle,
even though sent up for contempt. In view of
these facts we cannot but repeat what we have
often said before that women are as willing to
suffer for a principle as men are, and that true
women will maintain their integrity to the Gospel
and its principles,in spite of persuasion, opposition
and threats.


THE FOURTH OF JULY. 
	In childhood’s days the very mention of "Inde-
pendence day" sent the blood tingling through
our veins, and no exertion was too much to cele-
brate the memorable occasion. Have we forgotten
these early impressions, and the patriotism we
drank in with the  very air we breathed? No, we
have not; they are with us still, and the same
love of liberty and consciousness of freedom to
worship God, that we inherited as our birthright,
still remains strong and dominant in our nature.
Do we love our country now as ever? Yes, we
do,but we mourn for the blots upon her escutcheon,
for the  laws enacted, and now being rigidly en-
forced against religious liberty. Alas for freedom
of conscience and free speech, when men are
mobbed and even sent to prison and placed under
bonds for practicing the pure principles of the
Gospel of  salvation, and when for the same re-
ligious practices taught and inculcated in the
holy Scriptures, and obeyed by holy men of old—
Abraham, Jacob and others of the ancients, who
were chosen men of God, and blest above others
of their time, in keeping this law of God; when
men for this, and this only, are arrested and com-
mitted to prison with the most wicked and vile,
their wives, honorably married, called before the
courts, held up to ridicule, and questioned as
though they were, the scum of the earth, this is
liberty indeed.
	In America, the land of the free and the home
of the brave, a land blest above all other lands,
with a Constitution greater and grander than any
upon the face of the earth, the Lord raised up in
this age of the world a prophet, and gave him a
message of life and salvation and a warning unto
all people. In the very beginning of his work
presecution begun; religious persecution; and his
life was sought for, his disciples and his followers
were persecuted and driven from state to state,and
from city to city. These people were American
citizens, sons and daughters of the soil, native
born, and many of them descended from illustri-
ous patriots, whose fathers and grandfathers had
fought for freedom and religious liberty, and
whose mothers and grandmothers, had suffered the
hardships incident to the perils of war, and sent
forth their husbands and sons to fight for freedom,
laying them upon the altar of liberty, though their
hearts were as tender as the heart of Abraham
when he went to offer up his son Isaac as a sacri-
fice. Such were the forefathers and foremothers
of the Prophets Joseph and  Brigham, Heber and
the leading men and women of this Church, who
have declared their mission to be from God, and
who have sought to do good to all people. What
were they accused of in those early days of perse-
cution, when they were driven from Ohio to Mis-
souri, and from county to county and city to city?
There was no such thing as plural marriage,
against which so much is said now; they were ac-
cused of having new revelation, of fanaticism, of
strange doctrine, of this and of that,of being abo-
litionists and of various things, and men were im-
prisoned and robbed, families were driven out
from their own rightful possessions, in the dead of
winter, and compelled to seek refuge and protec-
tion wherever they could, and finally, after having
established themselves once more peacefully, and
built up a beautiful city and magnificent temple in
the State of Illinois, and having added to their
numbers many from other lands, behold this hand-
ful of people were again molested, their Prophet
and Patriarch foully murdered and Apostles bare-
ly escaped with their lives. One of them, John
Taylor, now President of the Church of Jesus
Christ of Latter-day Saints, wounded, the mob
thought, unto death, and saved as by a miracle,
bears the scars and the bullets  in his body to this
day, while under the guarantee of protection from
the state, and afterwards the whole body of the
people driven into the wilderness, without prepara-
tion, at the peril of their lives. 
	To recount the story of the wrongs and hard-
ships inflicted upon them would fill volumes of
history, but through it all this people maintained
their loyalty to the flag of their country, and
sought, by petitioning, and in various legitimate
ways, from time to time, to obtain some redress
for the wrongs inflicted upon them, but in vain.
The rulers of the nation acknowledged themselves
powerless to make any restitution to the "Mor-
mons." And so they were, so far as giving them
back their dead to life, slain for the Gospel’s sake,
and those who died by the wayside for want and
privation, brought about through this unhallowed
religious persecution, but in restoring rights of
property destroyed by mob violence, etc., they
need not have been powerless; but popular clamor
restrained those who were disposed to mete out
justice in any sense to the "Mormons."
	As proof positive of loyally to the dear old flag,
the “Mormons,” in their pilgrimage from Nauvoo
to the Rocky Mountain region, gave 500 of their
best picked men to the service of their country
and made no complaint. Women drove their own
teams, yoking and unyoking their cattle, taking
care of their children, and  performing the duties
and labor of both father and mother, sacrificing
themselves that the husband might show his allegi-
ance to his country in time of need, and honor the
laws of the land that had driven him and his dear
ones forth as an exile from his own inheritance.
	Arriving in the Valley of the Great Salt Lake,
Brigham Young and his noble and brave pioneers
planted the United States’ flag proudly and gladly
on this then desolate land, and dedicated it to God
and liberty in the name of the United States. Does
this savor of disrespect to the flag, or of disloyal-
ty to the government of "our common country?”
What have the rulers of this nation to say to our
oft-repeated wrongs? Only this: “We are tired
of hearing of the wrongs of Missouri and of
Nauvoo." No wonder, for there is a terrible re-
cord laid up against a future day of reckoning.
But why add to the calendar already well filled
with wrongs and outrages? Why seek to plunder
and persecute those who are only pursuing the
even tenor of their way and intruding upon the
rights of no man or woman?  Is there not room
and place for all, even for the "Mormons?” One
would think so, but this people have prospered in


the land, they have subdued the desert and made
it desirable, and unprincipled men, office seekers
and hangers on look with jealous eyes upon this fair
land, and desire it for an inheritance,and its pub-
lic offices to dispose of among themselves, and
they have resolved to take possession by whatever
means they can make available.
	Who can say that the flag has ever been dis-
honored by floating at half mast? Do not all
Americans know that it is an expression of public
grief? Did the Confederates float the flag at half
mast when they defied the United States and
sought to make war upon their country? No;
they unfurled a flag to the breeze that showed at
once the antagonism that ripened into a bloody
conflict. But those who are so anxiously seeking
for an excuse to justify the abuse of the "Mor-
mons" will wait long before they see the dear old
flag, of which all true American patriots are so
proud, and love with such intensity, dishonored.
Too many of the forefethers of this people bled
and died for that independence which the stars and
stripes are a significant of, and they know what
the feeling of genuine love of country is, and feel
it too deeply to make light of any of its symbols.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]		MRS. LOCKWOOD'S VISIT.

	On Monday, the 6th inst., Mrs. Belva A. Lock-
wood arrived in Salt Lake City, and an informal
reception, hurriedly arranged, was given her at 4
p.m., at the residence of F. S. Richards Esq., in
this city. Mrs. Richards’ parlor was well filled,
and after the formal presentation of the ladies to
Mrs. Lockwood, by Mrs. Richards, Mrs. H. T.
King spoke a few words of welcome, and read the
following.
		IMPROMPTU.

	On meeting Mrs. Belva A. Lockwood.

Hail lady! friend of human kind.
We greet you, heart and soul and mind!
We lay bright tributes at your feet—
Your advent here we warmly greet.

Lady, accept our hand and heart,
Your name to us doth love impart;
Friend of the oppress'd—we deem you one—
Then welcome to our mountain home.

Mrs. Lula Greene Richards read,
		A WELCOME.
To Mrs. Belva A. Lockwood, on her visiting Salt Lake
City. Written in behalf of the
Ladies of Utah.

Not to a princess born, yet stranger guest,
	Could we a welcome so sincere extend,
As unto thee, thou champion of the oppress'd—
	We warmly greet thee as a kind, brave friend.

We've given hospitality to some
	Who smiled upon us, and they turned away,
And, when they might speak well of us, are dumb,
	But evil things they find enough to say.

Yet we've no fear of this in greeting you,
	And wish your stay with us might be prolonged;
For you have said good things, when you but knew
	We were a people vilified and wronged.

We're grateful that you come to us and pause,
	That we may hear your voice and see your face,
And tell you, that when you have plead our cause,
	We've blest your name before the throne of grace.

This fact may seem to you but little weight,
	Your inner soul it may, or may not touch;
But to th' true "Mormon" woman it is great,
	No worldy compensation counts so mnch.

And though, to-day, it seems that “Mormon.” prayer
	And faith holds naught in this world's large events,
They gain us grace to bear and to forebear,
	And help us to proceed with calm, cool sense.

Our God is with us; and, dear lady, know
	That in our heart-felt welcoming to you,
Whatever honor we can hope to show, 
	Whatever good we may delight to do,

[Column 2]
This prayer is best—God bless and make you wise,
	The truth, the right, the worthy to defend;
Your great reward be that which never dies,
	Of all things good; thrice welcome, brave, kind friend.

	Mrs. Lockwood replied briefly to the cordial
welcome extended to her, and said she had simply
done her duty in speaking in defense of the women
of Utah. Brief speeches of welcome were made
by Mrs. Phebe W. Woodruff, Mrs. Emily H.
Woodmansee and Mrs. M. I. Horne; also by A.
Miner Esq., after which the conversation was
general, and an hour or two was spent very pleas-
antly.
	In the evening Mrs. Lockwood appeared on the
platform of the theatre, and was introduced to the
audience by the Hon. John T. Caine with a few
appropriate words, announcing, among other
notable events in her life, that she was the only
woman who had ever been nominated for Presi-
dent of the United States. The subject of her
lecture was, “Social and Political Life in Washing-
ton,” and it was given to an appreciative audience,
almost entirely “Mormon.” Without exception it
was the finest lecture we have ever heard given by
a woman. She seemed to have touched, in writing
it, every point pertaining to her subject, and made
each one effective.
	On Tuesday she went to Provo, where she
lectured in the evening. The following day to
Ogden, speaking there to a full house, and on
Thursday to Logan, returning to this city on Fri-
day, and spoke again in the theatre. Her lecture
was entitled, “Women of To-day,” which she
handled in a very able and interesting manner.
After the lecture was over, Hon. John T. Caine
announced that all who wished to be introduced to
Mrs. Lockwood might have that pleasure by com-
ing forward to the platform; quite a number of
ladies and gentlemen availed themselves of the
opportunity, and were presented to her by Mr.
Caine, who did the honors of the occasion very
handsomely. On Saturday morning Mrs. Lock-
wood left Salt Lake City for Evanston, where she
was expected to speak the same evening.
	We sincerely regret that Mrs. Lockwood had
not the time to remain here long enough to see and
hear a little more of the social and political life of
Utah and the “Mormons,” but she may come
again. As it is she has received favorable impres-
sions, and has been entertained by those who
would spare no pains to make her visit a pleasant
and agreeable one. In this city she was the
honored guest of F. S. Richards Esq., and of
Hon. John T. Caine. At Provo she was the guest
of Hon. W. N. Dusenberry, and in Ogden of
Apostle F. D. Richards, and in Logan she was
entertained at the home of Apostle Moses
Thatcher. 
	Of Mrs. Lockwood it may well be said she is a
woman among women; she is remarkable in many
respects. She is an indefatigable and untiring
worker for the elevation of her sex, and the good
of humanity.
	Mrs. Lockwood is famous enough to have drawn
a much larger audience, and certainly the young
ladies should have come out to see and hear her.
It is not only a mistake on their part, but they
have missed hearing a very distinguished woman
speak. Should Mrs. Lockwood ever visit Salt
Lake again, we hope she may have the house well
filled with young people. They are the ones who
ought certainly to have heard her.


A SIMILITUDE.

[THE PEBBLE AND THE ACORN.]

	In looking at the circumstances by which we
are surrounded to-day, I often think of the fable
of the pebble and the acorn, believing there is
quite a similarity in our case at the present time.
	You will remember the eloquent speech of the
pebble, saying, "I am a pebble, but whom art


thou, who comes rattling along from the rustling
bough?" None could tell of his birth, for he was
as old as the big round earth. The acorn, abashed
and mute at this rude rebuff, sank in the dust, and
in quietude lay thinking, of course, what her des-
tiny would be.
	In space of time a tiny sprout shoots forth, and
then a blade appeared, which formed a stem; it
grows to be a sturdy oak, which gives shelter to
the honorable pebble, whose age is so great, and a
kindred also of mother earth.
	After a space of time the tree blooms and bears
fruit; this also drops from its branches, and the
tree is again left bare. Still it thrives and grows
after meeting with such a loss, and shades the
honorable pebble. Time advances, and acorns
appear again; although the bloom is scattered to
the four winds, still the calyx holds the germ, so
that nothing is lost which is of worth to the fruit.
Viewing the subject in this light we see the hand
of an allwise Providence governing all His works,
and we readily understand the meaning of the
revelation given to the Prophet Joseph Smith,
saying, "The Lord is angry with none, excepting
those who will not acknowledge His hand in all
things."
	Thus we contemplate the designs and purposes
of an allwise Parent, whose decrees are all for the
good of His erring children, and we learn by ex-
perience how just and true He requires us to be.
	When in possession of His Holy Spirit we can
understand how the Lord blesses those who, like
the acorn, are humble and reflect and wait; they
will become acquainted with self and learn their
own abilities, and also learn that they have a des-
tiny to fill, after they learn this lesson perfectly,
which the pebble could never fill, although so hard
and inflexible; and again, the rock can fill a place
while the oak cannot. Why be proud? but remem-
ber that all are to fill their own sphere, as the
Bible teaches. There are vessels of honor and
vessels of dishonor. Let everything have its own
place and all will be useful in our Father's King-
dom.
	One thing is certain, great blessings await the
human family if they will listen and obey the voice
of the good Shepherd, who said to the Jews,
"How oft would I have gathered you together
as a hen gathereth her chickens under her wings,
but ye would not. Behold, your house is left unto
you desolate."		M. E. K.
	July 1st, 1885.


SOME FACTS PLAINLY SET FORTH.

	Occasionally I meet with those who claim to be
Latter-day Saints that are in doubt about the
principle of plural marriage being the revealed
will of God to His Saints in this dispensation, yet
hey verily believe in celestial marriage, and ex-
pect to be exalted in the new and everlasting
covenant. How they can believe in the one and
not in the other is a mystery to me, when they are
both contained in the same revelation.
	Those who doubt the authenticity of plural
marriage, use for one of their strongest argnments
against it, a portion of the second chapter of
Jacob, in the Book of Mormon, claiming that he
spoke against it, and that if it was not right then
it s not right now. I have frequently and care-
fully read the passage referred to, and invariably
fail to find one word of condemnation of plural
marriage in it, when it is entered into according to
the law of the Lord.
	He was not reproving the Nephites for having
more wives than one, but because they "excused
themselves in committing whoredoms, because of
the things which were written concerning David,
and Solomon his son." Whoredom is a sin
which has been condemned from the beginning,
but nowhere do we find in holy writ anything
condemnatory of lawful plural marriage, but much
to show that it was in many instances justified by
the Lord.


Jacob also said, "Behold, David and Solomon
<truly. had="" many="" wives="" and="" concubines,="" which="" thing,="" was="" abominable="" before.me,saith="" the="" lord”="" this1="" -="" b="" is="" a-point="" held="" to="" mostlenaciously="" by="" opponent="" "="" (of="" plural="" marriageas="" an,argument="" that="" -cannot="" be-="" refuted,="" claiming="" having="" ,of="" more="" wiy.es="" that!="" one="" abomination="" lord.="" hp="" .does="" ’not="" say="" so,="" or="" carry="" thp="" idea="" jn="" lea$t.,tha="" -david="" splomon="" done="" anything="" wrong="" in="" than,="" .at="" same="" time,="" but="" it="" and.="" .concubine?="" they="" jo..thfr.lord="" .thing="" (so-many="" .concubines)="" would="" .also="" be="" abominable'="" any="" chaste="" virtuous="" person,="" --is="" recorded="" ,="" bible,="" .lord="" gaye="" -tadavid="" his="" wives,="" have="" given="" .him="" oif="" he-had="" asked="" him="" for="" them..="" this="" i-under-="" standthat="" lord="" justified="" david.in="" haying="" (wives="" than="" at="" of="" concubines="" .abominable="" him..="" i-="" jacob="" again="" says,="" ,“for="" there="" hall="" not="" .man="" among="" yon="" save="" :be="" one,="" wife,="" concul="" nbines="" shall="" nope,"="" ..the="" question="" .is-fre?="" quently="" asked,="" ‘‘why.,?did="" give,="" command="" nephites,="" if="" marriage="" right?"="" are="" reasons="" why,="" th?="" --following="" i="" think="" sufficient-="">truly had many wives and concubines, which thing
was abominable before me, saith the Lord." This
is a point held to most tenaciously by the opponent
of plural marriage as an argument that cannot be
refuted, claiming that the having of more wives
than one is the abomination to the Lord. He does
not say so, or carry the idea in the least tha
David and Solomon had done anything wrong in
having more wives than one at the same time,
but it was the many wives and concubines they
had that was abominable to the Lord, which thing
(so many wives and concubines) would also be
abominable to any chaste and virtuous person. It
is recorded in the Bible that the Lord gave to
David his wives, and would have given him more
if He had asked Him for them. By this I under-
stand that the Lord justified David in having more
wives than one at the same time, but it was the
having of many wives and concubines that was
abominable to Him.
	Jacob again says, "For there shall not any man
among you have save it be one wife, and concu-
bines he shall have none." The question is fre-
quently asked, "Why did the Lord give this
command to the Nephites, if plural marriage is
right?" There are many reasons why, but the
following one I think is sufficient:
	In the revelation on celestial marriage, given
through the Prophet Joseph Smith, it is recorded
that "there is never but one on the earth at a time
on whom this power and the keys of the Priest-
hood are conferred." We have no record of
Lehi, or any one of the Nephites ever holding the
keys of this power, or ever having the right or the
authority to seal wives to any man. If they had
not this right then it was wrong for any man among
them to have more wives than one at the same
time.
	Jacob still further says, "For if I will, saith the
Lord of Hosts, raise up seed to me, I will com-
mand my people." This presupposes that the
Lord would, at some future time, command His
people to enter into plural marriage; virtually fore-
telling that the Lord would yet command His peo-
ple to enter into it for the purpose of increasing
the posterity of the Saints upon the earth.
	We are now living in the time when the Lord
has restored that order of marriage held sacred
among His ancient people, and the Saints have
been commanded to enter into the order of patri-
archal marriage, or "be damned, sayeth the
Lord."
	Paul says, "The Gospel is the power of God
unto salvation to all those who believe," and also
that "man is not without the woman, nor the
woman without the man in the Lord." If it is so
that man and woman must necessarily be together
in the Lord, then the Gospel plan of salvation
would be incomplete without the principle of plural
marriage being practiced in the Church, whereby
every woman might be joined with man in holy
matrimony for time and all eternity, that they may
eternally increase in an exaltation in the celestial
kingdom of God. There has been, and is now, a
numerical preponderance of women over the men
in the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints,
and probably will be, with an increase, until, as
Isaiah has said, seven women shall take hold of
one man to be called by his name to take away
their reproach.
	Much might be written on this subject, but a
"word to the wise is sufficient," and all will under-
stand, when they fully comprehend the plan of
salvation, that plural marriage is necessary to
make that plan complete, so that all who will may
attain to that glory and exaltation promised to the
faithful.		S. F. A.
	July 9, 1885.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]

BEWARE OF PRIDE!

	"Blessed are the poor in spirit, for theirs is the
kingdom of heaven. Blessed are the meek, for

[Column 2]

they shall inherit the earth. Blessed are the pure
in heart, for they shall see god."—MATTHEW, 5th
chapter.
	Good evening, my dear EXPONENT! how glad I
am to see you, for I was wishing I had some one
to talk with, for I am feeling quite sad. I have
been reading about the terrible earthquake in
Madrid, the cholera in Asia, that awful water-
sprout in Texas, that destroyed so much life and
property, and about the yellow fever making its
appearance again at New Orleans, and of the In-
dians slaughtering the inhabitants of New Mexico
and, oh, dear! some few more of our brethren
have been arrested. Yet it is not all this alone
that brings the sigh, but some of the things we
saw at conference, which was over a few days ago.
There were some of our mothers in Israel dressed
in a very unbecoming manner—flowers, ribbons,
fringes and bugles, and I thought, oh! my soul,
what a pattern for the young, fair daughters of
Zion. "Mothers filled with so much pride and lofti-
ness, do you, or can you expect anything else
but that your daughters will go astray, seeking
after the vanities of the world? Oh, mothers! are
you followers of the meek and lowly Savior?
Then why so much pride! Have you read the
Scriptures, where it tells how the daughters of
Zion shall bow their heads with shame, and mourn
because of their pride?"
	Ah! here comes an old lady; she has grown old
and grey in the Church, suffered persecutions and
hardships, and should be respected and loved for
her faithfulness, left a widow early, with a family
of small children, which she has toiled day and
night with her own hands to raise respectably.
She has come to conference to renew her spiritual
strength, to associate with her brethren and sisters,
and hear the good instruction that is given to all.
She is neatly, though plainly dressed—as well as
her circumstances would allow—yet she does not
wear a bonnet covered with ribbons, flowers and
feathers; no, she has come a long distance to hear
the word of God from His servants, and her head
is covered in a neat sun bonnet.
	But what has she done that she should be
slighted by those proud ones? She has taken
them into her house and fed them, and given them
beds to sleep in, in times past. Then why not re-
turn such kindness? "Oh! she is not well dressed
and wears a sun bonnet." Indeed, but that is
awful. Well, would it take anything away from
your greatness to be kind to her, and make her
feel that she was welcome? When you receive
your crown as one of the queens in the celestial
city, do you think the shock would be too much
for you if you should see her there with her plain
clothes and sun bonnet.
	Out upon such pride; I have no patience with it.
Go humble yourselves, and repent of your sins in
sack cloth and ashes, ye Latterday Saints, or you
will never be among that choice number. Put by
your haughtiness, be meek and prayerful, kind
and loving to each other, for is not ours one com-
mon cause? Are not our fathers, husbands and
brothers in exile? Yes; and even some of them
groan in prison. Are not many calls made upon
our brethren and sisters for aid and assistance?
Then why should we, who are so small in the eyes
of the Lord, scorn our poor, but equally worthy
sisters?
	It would be more pleasing in the sight of the
Lord if we would all dress more plain, and not
run after the fashions of Babylon, lest we partake
of her sins and receive of her plagues. It would
be quite as becoming to Latter-day Saints, in this
day of trouble, to be more plain, and not spend
so much for foolishness, when with that means we
might do more good.
	A great deal is said about the word of wisdom.
There is no wisdom in following fashion and all
the nonsense that it brings. It will not exalt us
one whit in the estimation of good people. No,
but by our pride we lower ourselves. We also

[Column 3]

hurt the feelings of our poor brethren and sisters,
and this will be held against us.
	Now, my dear EXPONENT, will you be so kind
as to tell the sisters that there are many, very
many poor sisters who would gladly attend con-
ference if they could afford a better dress, so that
they would not be scorned by their well dressed
sisters. Oh! try and get rid of this unholy feeling,
my dear sisters, so that at the next conference the
poor can say they enjoyed themselves, and were
treated as they should be, on an equality, with
love, and respected as Latter-day Saints.
		K.


SCENES AND INCIDENTS AT
WINTER QUARTERS.

BY HELEN MAR WHITNEY.

(Continued.)

	"Father arose and, said:" 'Bro. Kimball
has mentioned the idea of writing things down,
in order that you may ask questions. It is a
good idea.	*	*	I love intelli-
gence, and it is a perfect torment for me to
sit down and talk to some people. Many a
time I have sat down and talked with folks
upon trivial matters, and have actually got
some new ideas by asking a little question.
Some, from diffidence, are backward in asking
intelligence; but no gentleman would laugh or
sneer at another when a question was asked,
even if it was an ignorant one.'
	"Brother Kimball asked the brethren and
sisters if they had enjoyed this party as well
as the one at the Council House. They all
answered Yes.' He observed that he would
like to have his children learn to calm them-
selves, and he hoped that they would mind and
keep the watch wound up, and when they lost
time refer to the chronometer.
	"A hymn being sung the meeting was dis-
missed with a benediction by Bishop Whitney.
Bro. Kimball dismissed them also with his
blessing."
	One thing, which I have neglected to men-
tion, was the kind and brotherly feeling mani-
fested by the brethren towards Bishop Miller,
whenever he came down on his way to Missouri,
where he went to trade for provisions. It
seems that they prevailed on him to return to
Winter Quarters, and at a meeting held at
the Council House, January 26th, he being
present, "it was moved by President Young,
and stated by President Kimball, that inas-
much as Bishop Miller, being here, is about to
return to this place with his family, we build
him a house. Carried unanimously." Wm.
Pitt moved that John Kay also return.
Seconded and carried.
	Monday. February 15th, my husband men-
tions the Bishop's arrival from Punkaw, bring-
ing with him part of his family, his house
being nearly finished.
	A party was given on the 16th by President
Brigham Young to his adopted children.
Horace says, "I went, by invitation, as a musi-
cian, with some others of the band; had a first
rate dinner, and spent the time very agreeably.
Toward evening a couple of brethren from the
army arrived, viz., Bros. Thippetts and Woolsey.
They had journeyed thirty days without eating
anything, except wild game, on the road. 'They
were invited in to supper by Bro. Brigham.
They had been robbed on the road by the In-
dians, and divested of most of their apparel.
They said that most of the brethren were lo-
cated about three hundred miles from Santa
Fe, and this side of the mountains, where they
intend to remain this winter. A number of
them had died by various diseases—the ague
and fever, etc. The names of those who had
died were read over by Brigham, and among
them was the name of Joseph Richards, the
son of Phinehas Richards."


[Column 1]
	On the 18th mention is made of Bro. Luke
Johnson and his sister (Marinda Hyde) being
with us. The heaviest snow storm of the sea-
son visited us that night, which prevented
them from returning home—on the other side
of the river—the snow having completely
blocked up all the roads. On the Sunday fol-
fowing another family meeting was held at
father's, who was still quite unwell. Brother
Brown and Bishop Whitney preached.
	"Tuesday, the 23rd. The bishops, with the
poor, and the soldiers' wives of the several
wards, met at the Council House to enjoy
themselves. I attended, with some others of
the band."
	"Wednesday, the 24th. Snowed consider-
ably to-day, as well as yesterday. My wife's
health is improving slowly. She has lately
been afflicted with canker. She has now been
sick five weeks last Sunday. I am also troubled
considerably with the canker in my mouth.
The company of pioneers will probably start
for the mountains in about four weeks, in or-
der to get to our place of location in time to
put in a spring crop."
	On the 1st day of March he wrote: "Beauti-
ful weather. Ellis Eames, a player on the
violin, came up from the Point—a number of
the band, including him and myself, went
around in a sleigh this evening (Porter driv-
ing) and serenaded several places in the town.
Bro. Kimball was along with us. We stopped
at Bishop Hunter's and played a tune, then, by
invitation of Bro. Kimball, went to his house,
where we spent some time in dancing.
	"Tuesday, the 2nd.	*	*	This
evening Bro. Kay arrived from the Punkaw
nation, having left his family and some others
fifty miles from here, not being able to get
here, in consequence of their feed for cattle
having failed."
	"Wednesday,	*	*	a number of
the band went around and raised a contribu-
tion for Bro. Kay, in corn and meal, etc.—We
got nine bushels of the former. Played with
some others of the band for the quorum of
High Priests, whose parties commenced yester-
day."
	The next day he says, "This morning Bro.
Kay and James Clayton started with their pro-
visions to go back; also this morning Ellis
Eames, J. Redding, Merit Rockwell, two
ladies and myself went down to the point in
Bro: Kimball's carriage, and attended a party.
I took my flute to assist Bro. Eames in playing.
Had a very good time, good supper, etc."
	On the evening of Saturday, the 5th, Bro.
John Kay arrived with his family.
	There was a meeting of father's company of
pioneers, held at the Council House, Monday
morning, it being the intention for some to
start soon, though the majority were not to
start under a week or so. Here Horace and
his brother Orson, concluded to go with the
pioneers in place of their father, and assist in
putting in crops, till the ground, etc., that the
families, when they came on, might have some-
thing to move to. All were busy now prepar-
ing, and laying in their supplies for the jour-
ney.
	Sunday, the 14th, my husband wrote: "By
father's request I went and copied an import-
ant document, which took me the greater part
of the day and into the night."
	The revelation on plural marriage was the
"document" referred to, the Bishop having the
only one in existence, which he afterwards
gave to President Young, retaining a copy.
	He mentions the black scurvy, which had
begun its work, and already many cases
had proved fatal. It would commence with dark
streaks and pains in the ends of the fingers
or toes, which increased and spread till the
limbs were inflamed and became almost black,
causing such intense agony that death would
be welcomed as a release from their suffering.

[Column 2]
It was caused by the want of vegetable food
and living so long on salt meat without it.
	It was now a year or more since the majority
had left their homes and civilizatian behind,
and our trail was marked by the lonely
mounds of the dead, who had made a happy
escape from the sufferings and want to which
we were so many years subjected, through the
wickedness and injustice of man.


WOMAN'S EXPNENT.


[Column 1]

R. S., Y. L. M. I. A. & P. A. REPORTS

ONEIDA	STAKE.
	The Relief Society of Oneida Stake was or-
ganized in October, 1884, Apostles F. M. Ly-
man and John W. Taylor being present and
assisting the Presidency of the Stake in the
organization. Mrs. Elizabeth Fox of Franklin
was elected President; Mrs. Ann Hales of
Gentile Valley, First Counselor; Mrs. Mary
Hawkes of Franklin, Second Counselor; Mrs.
Annie Hatch, Secretary, and Mrs. Josephine
Mayberry, Assistant Secretary.
	The Relief Society of Oxford, Oneida Stake
was reorganized on the 28th of June, 1885,
Mrs. Polly Boyes, the former president, having
resigned her position. Mrs. Mary Jane Hend-
ricks was chosen President, with Mrs. Lewis
and Mrs. Crocker as Counselors.

TAYLOR, ARIZONA.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	We held our annual review of the Primary
Association May 21st. 1885. There were pres-
ent upwards of seventy children, and quite a
number of their parents. We had a very en-
joyable time. Meeting opened with singing
by the Primary, under the direction of Bro.
Harrison Pearce, who is ever willing to assist
us. Prayer by Bro. Wm. Solomon. Singing
by the Primary. Opening speech by President
Magdalena Pearce. The programme consisted
of songs, recitations, select reading, dia-
logues and Bible Questions and Answers. We
must especially mention a recitation by four
little boys—"Give the little boys a chance,"
and also an exercise song by six very little
girls, and a song, "Look on the bright side," by
Misses Maria and Rebecca Standford and
Mable Pearce.
	Then our Stake President, Elizabeth Haw-
kins, read to the parents of the children an
eloquent address made by our beloved sister,
Eliza R. S. Smith, at Weber Stake Conference,
March 12th, 1885, after which Brother Wm.
Solomon and Merrill Willis made some en-
couraging remarks to all. Singing. Benedic-
tion by Bro. Joseph McCleve.
	In the afternoon we had a dancing party for
the children, and they enjoyed themselves, and
we feel to bless all the children of Zion, and
all who are engaged in building up our Father's
kingdom.
	Wishing for the success of the EXPONENT
and all those who are trying to help forward
the great work, we remain,
	Your sisters in the Gospel,
		MAGDALENA PEARCE, Prest.,
			ANN DATTON, Cor. Secretary.

SALT LAKE STAKE.
	The Relief Society Conference of Salt Lake
Stake was held in the Fourteenth Ward As-
sembly Rooms, June 26th, commencing at 10
a.m., Mrs. M. I. Horne presiding. After sing-
ing and prayer by Coun. E. S. Taylor and sing-
ing, the minutes of the March Conference were
read and accepted and roll of Presidents called.
Twenty-nine war is were represented, all of
them as being in good working order, the
general feeling being to improve in wisdom
and knowledge, and to increase in faith and
works of charity and usefulness.

[Column 2]

	Prest. Horne made a very able address to
the officers, giving specific and timely instruc-
tion, and exhorting all to prepare for the great
events that are about to transpire. Conference
then adjourned till 2 p.m. Singing. Benedic-
tion by Mrs. B. W.Smith. 
	Afternoon session:	Singing. Prayer by
Secretary E. Howard. Singing. Remarks 
were made by Mrs. Minerva W. Snow, Prest.
of the Relief Society in St. George Stake.
She spoke upon the spiritual progress of the
daughters of Zion who are in the path of duty
and who testify of the goodness of God; said
such were bound together by the Holy Spirit.
She was followed by her Counselor, Mrs. Annie
Ivins, who referred to the labors of the sisters
of St. George, and the blessings that attended
their labors, and the peace, quiet and union
that existed there.
	Coun. E. S. Taylor, Secretary E. Howard,
Mrs. Sarah M. Kimball, Secretary of Central
Board of Relief Society, Dr. Ellis R. Shipp,
Dr. Maggie C. Shipp, Dr. E. S. Barney, each
made a brief, but interesting and edifying ad-
dress, and Prest. Horne made closing remarks,
reviewing what had been spoken of by the sis-
ters, and exhorting all to diligence and to
practice economy. Among the subjects treated
upon by the sisters was the storing of grain
and donating to the Deseret Hospital.
	Conference adjourned for three months with
singing and benediction.

SALT LAKE PRIMARY.
	The Quarterly Conference of the P. A. of
Salt Lake Stake was held in the Assembly
Hall, June 27th, at 10 a.m., Mrs. Ellen C.
Clawson presiding. Opened by singing, "Did
you think to pray," etc. Prayer by Sister
Smith. Singing, "In the chambeas of the
mountains." Minutes of previous conference
read and accepted. Verbal reports were given
from several wards, and two little girls from
the 16th Ward sang, "What is fairer than the
rose." Some other wards reported and Miss
Dora Boreman of the 19th Ward recited a 
piece entitled "The Martyrs." After some
more reports, Miss Irene Daynes sang a song
entitled "Grandpa's Darling," accompanied on
the organ by Master Josie Daynes, who after-
wards gave an organ solo. The 17th Ward
was then reported, and a very little girl from
the 7th Ward gave a recitation. Three other
wards reported, and a recitation was given by a
boy from the 10th Ward. Other wards were
reported, and a recitation given by a little girl
from South Cottonwood Ward, and after some
other wards had reported, Fanny Day of the
12th Ward sang a song. Mrs. Aurelia S.
Rogers, President of the Primary of Davis
Stake, made a few very interesting remarks to
the children.
	Conference adjourned for three months.
Singing, "The Lord's Prayer," by the children
Prayer by Coun. Lydia Ann Wells.


NOTES AND NEWS.

	"Mrs. Elizabeth Eaglesford is the first
woman admitted to the bar in Indiana."

	London, July 13.—A mass meeting of
women was held at Princess Hall this afternoon
to consider ways and means to protect young
girls from the horrible pitfalls of London, as
revealed by the Pall Malt Gazette. Mrs. Booth
and other ladies made eloquent addresses, and
the meeting resolved that the laws on the sub-
ject should forthwith be made more efficient:
denounced the state regulation of vice demand-
ed a total repeal of the contagious diseases act,
and that the age of consent be raised from 13
to 18. The meeting also voted to send a re-
cord of its proceedings to the House of Com-
mons.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


Gray Memorial at Cambridge.—A bust of
the poet Gray was recently unveiled in the
hall of Pembroke college, Cambridge, by Lord
Houghton, in the presence of a distinguished
company, and the occasion was rendered more
noteworthy from the fact that the Hon. James
Russell Lowell, the late American Minister,
made his farewell public speech prior to leav-
ing England. Amongst the ladies in the
gallery was Miss Mary Anderson. The bust
of Gray is in white marble, and is mounted
upon a pedestal of Pavonezza marble, on the
front of which is a beautiful relief represent-
ing the first stanza of “The Elegy.” Mr.
Lowell said he came to speak as the represen-
tative of certain of his countrymen and coun-
trywomen who liked to renew that affirmation
of the unity of the British race by giving
something better than words in memory of the
poet they loved. Gray had been called com-
mon-place, but that, he thought, was a compli-
ment. He was inclined to judge poets by their
lasting power. Gray not only pleased his
youth and age, but other people's youth and
age. He touched our human nature at a great
many periods and a great many levels. There
was commonplace in the “Elegy,” and also in
some phrases which had been in use for 3,000
years. As long as there were young men, as long
as there were middle-aged men, Gray would hold
his place, in the human mind. They would,
perhaps, before he sat down allow him to say
something about himself. This would be the
last occasion on which he would have the op-
portunity of addressing Englishmen in public.
He wished to express his most heartfelt grati-
tude for the kindness which had surrounded
him in his official and private life, and had
made it delightful. While he had come there
as a distant cousin they were sending him away
as something like a brother.

IN MEMORIAM.

	It is a sweet reward to merit an honorable remembrance
among the Saints of God, and it is with sadness and regret
that we record the demise of another beloved sister, faith-
ful Latter-day Saint, devoted mother and true help-meet to
man.
	Harriet Davis Hughes, wife of Francis D. Hughes, was
born in Glamorganshire, South Wales, January 24th, 1839,
and after having "fought the good fight and kept the
faith," passed peacefully to her rest, in the Eleventh Ward
Salt Lake City, June 1st, 1885, where she was a teacher in
the Relief Society, and a member of the Y. L. M. I. A.
	Sister Hughes embraced the Gospel in her youth, and
was ever a sincere defender of its principles, in private and
public, especially that of celestial marriage, to the truth and
divinity of which she bore a powerful testimony on her
death bed. She always expressed great pride and satisfac-
tion in having accepted in practice that order of marriage,
and the effect of her influence upon her daughters is that
they also honor that holy principle, which was revealed to
their mother in a heavenly vision, which so impressed her
that she became one of its strongest advocates, a synopsis
of which may interest others.
	After seeing many things, she found herself in the mids
of a large forest, and all the trees, as far as her vision ex-
tended, appeared to be withered and dry; the withered
remains of flowers and grasses also filled up the desolate
landscape; not a leaf, or anything indicating life, could be
seen While contemplating the probable cause of such a
calamity, a messenger of exceeding pleasing countenance
stood by her and pointed in another direction, when the
most lovely scene opened to her view—a beautiful land


filled with verdure and the perfume of flowers, vast forests
with their rich foliage extended on every hand, while the
murmur of gently rippling brooklets, and the sweet singing
birds, with their variegated plumage,awakened in her heart
the most heavenly feeling she had ever experienced.
	While gazing enraptured on this beautiful picture the
messenger touched her on the shoulder, and pointing to
the desolation of the first view, said: "This is a true repre-
sentation of the momogamic system of marriage, here is
the end of their increase, and the other represents the holy
order, the new and everlasting covenant of celestial marri-
age. Here is eternal increase, here is peace, eternal life
and joy."
	Just previous to her death she had another vision, the
substance of which I will give as related by her daughter:
	"One night while I was watching by my mother, being
overcome with sleep, I laid down upon her bed—I heard
the most lovely singing. Many voices joined in the chorus
and as the voices gradually died away, I awoke and mother
was still singing. Presently she opened her eyes and told
me she had been in the spirit world, where she was in a
large hall filled with women, who greeted her with singing
as she entered, and she joined in with them, and strange to
say I heard the singing, too." She said all the women
were plural wives, and that there was no distinction between
first, second, or third wives there, all were equal, all was
peace and purity.
	Sister Hughes was a woman of great faith, determined
energy, and of a most cheerful disposition. Her life was
not a "flowery bed of ease" but while surrounded with
many cares and trials, her cheerful presence was an inspira-
tion of hope and courage to those less happily gifted. Of
her it can be truly said that "they who knew her best loved
her most." She was a firm pillar in her own household,
who most keenly feel the loss of her companionship. "Her
children rise up and call her blessed," and the record of her
noble character and useful life is worthy a place in the his-
tory of the women of Israel.		L. T. F.
	July 6th, 1885.


OBITUARIES.

	DIED, at Milk Creek, Oneida Co., Idaho, Sister Martha
E. Wilde, wife of Thomas H. Wilde, formerly of Brigham
City, Box Elder Co., Utah. After four days sickness,
died June 15th, aged thirty eight years and seven months.
Faithful to the last.
	Deceased held many responsible positions among the
young and rising generation, loved and respected by all
who knew her—a respected member in the Relief Society.
She leaves a husband and six children to mourn the loss of
her society.
	P. S. This makes the second deceased adult female
since the organization of the ward. Away off north in the
mountains we enjoy fine, warm weather. Crops are look
ing better than ever. Comparatively we enjoy peace and
happiness, living our religion more or less, undisturbed—
courts and judges and deputy marshals—notwithstanding.
	Our Relief Society is in a flourishing condition, the law
of God, regarding C. L. of M., pretty well understood,
etc. Have commenced to store up wheat for a time of
need.
	On account of Danish, or other foreign births, not many
are able to read the EXPONENT; to me, however, the teach-
ings are greatly relished.
	Always trusting in the Lord, and praying for the deliver-
ance of Zion, I am,
		Ever your sister in the Gaspel,
			INGRID GRAHAM, Prest. R. S.

	DIED at Paragoonah Iron County, July 4th, 1885, after
a lingering illness of several years, Sister Lavenia H. Allen,
daughter of David and Mary Ann Yearsley, born Septem-
ber 1st, 1833, in Chester County, Pennsylvania. Joined
the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints, in the
City of Nauvoo; shared with the Saints in their expulsion
rom that place. Was at one time near death's door with
scarlet fever, and was healed through the administration of
the Prophet Joseph.
	Arrived in Salt Lake Valley in the fall of 1850. Mar
ried to Rufus C. Allen, March, 1853. They were called
to Dixie—she shared with her husband the trials and hard-
ships of opening up that country, and in the year of 1849
removed to Paragoonah where she lived and served as
Secretary of the Relief Society for three years and a half
previous to her death.
	We feel it our duty to express our appreciation of her
labors, and ever hold her memory dear to us. She was
	liant in the cause of truth, and her life was worthy of


imitation. She was most patient, never complaining in all
her years of suffering, willing to pass through all her Heav-
enly Father placed upon her, with the hope of gaining
eternal happiness in the presence of her Redeemer with the
sanctified.
	We sincerely sympathize with the bereaved husband and
relatives of the deceased, and pray that the Spirit of the
Lord may rest upon them and enable them to say, "Not
our will, O Lord, but Thine be done."
					ELIZA BARTON, President.
					SARAH HORSLEY, Ass't Sec.
