SALT LAKE CITY,	SEPTEMBER 15, 1885.


[Column 1]
	THE DAYS IN WHICH WE LIVE.

	These are remarkable days indeed. The times
which “try men’s souls,” and women’s too.
Verily the prophecies are being fulfilled, and one
cannot but see it if his eyes are open to that which
is transpiring around him. In the midst of such
occurrences as are daily taking place, how careful
should each individual be to keep on safe ground;
to fulfill every duty, that the adversary may not
gain any advantage over him; to cultivate
humility and patience, for the Bible says: “Pride
goeth before a fall," and it might be dangerous
even to stumble in a time like this, for there are
many pitfalls which have a fair covering, and the
foolish and unwary may be deceived if they have
not the spirit of discernment, for there are many
false spirits that seek to destroy and lead into bye
and forbidden paths the children of the covenant.
Therefore it behooves all Latter-day Saints to set
a stronger watch upon the children of Zion, and
parents to be doubly diligent and mindful, lest in
some unforeseen way, the enemy who is most
artful and cunning allure them from the straight
and narrow path which the Gospel has marked
out, into the broad highway of sin and folly, where
so many are drifting, carried thither by pride,
vanity, love of gold, and desire for popularity.
But remember all these are only temporary, and
please for a time, and very often are like “Dead
Sea apples,” fair without, but within are full of
bitterness, and how many have found after follow-
ing in pursuit of some bewildering fancy, only
alas too late, that they have lost beyond recall the
Jewel dearer to them than life itself, and what has
been gained? Nothing lasting; only a bitter ex-
perience, a harvest of sorrow after the sowing of
the seeds of selfishness. There are many “who
lay in wait to deceive,” to tempt with false pre-
tences the virtuous and pure from the path of
rectitude, and their arts and subterfuges are more
entangling than the web the spider weaves to catch
the silly flies. We smile at the simple flies that
are so easily caught, but the filmy webs that are
not perceptible to the young and unsophisticated,
often blinded as they are by the bewildering
glamour of fancy, are wrought by the skill and
cunning of those who are adepts at deception,
“whose steps lead down to hell;” in these the
innocent and thoughtless are often entangled, and
price ensnared it is seldom indeed they can en-
tirely free themselves.
	This is, in the strictest sense of the word, an
age of licentiousness; let him who can close his
eyes to the fact, and when the youth of Zion make
pleasure-seeking the aim of life, what can be ex-
pected?  The Lord has gven to His people in
this dispensation certain principles to be observed,
in order that He may have a remnant upon the
earth that will honor Him, a covenant people who
will be pure, and it behooves those who have ac-
cepted the Gospel to be humble, prayerful; and
obedient, that they may be able to stand in the
midst of trials and temptations, and stem the cur-

[Column 2]
rent of wickedness that is flooding the earth, and
so fortify Zion against the encroachments of sin
and crime, that the Saints may not be contamin-
ated by the evils thal are threatening the nations
of the earth with final overthrow and destruction.
	The youth of Zion should choose good company,
shun evil associates as they would a loathsome
disease, that can only kill the body, but the other
might eventually lead to the loss of all that is
worth living for.  The young cannot be too well
instructed in those things which tend to purity of
life and character, nor too closely watched over
and guarded in all their walk and conversation.
The strictest honesty and uprightness should be
observed, remembering the sacred covenants un-
der which they were born, the great mission they
ar. expected to perform, and the high and holy
destiny that awaits those who maintain their in-
tegrity to the Kingdom of God.
	It seems that every variety of wickedness is
sought to be introduced into the midst of this
people, and assuredly are the words of the Pro-
phet coming to pass who said, “Let us look upon
Zion that she may be defiled!” But to those who
observe the signs of the times, and who keep the
commandments given, God will give a portion of
His Holy Spirit, that they may foresee the evil,
and hide themselves from it.


EDITORIAL NOTES.

	SISTERS Sarah M. Kimball and Elizabeth 
Howard and Dr. Romania B. Pratt attended the 
Sisters' Conference in Tooele, on the 12th and 
13th insts. 

	THE Primary Fair of this Stake will open in the 
Social hall on Thursday, Sept. 17th, under the 
supervision of Mrs. Ellen C. Clawson and her 
counselors, Mrs. Lydia Ann Wells and Mrs. 
Camilla C. Cobb. 

	SISTER Eliza R. Snow Smith returned to Logan 
on the 11th inst., to resume her labors in the 
Temple, where she had previously been laboring 
for some time.  Though so far advanced in years, 
she is enjoying good health, and her mind is  as 
bright and active in years gone by.

	THE Relief Society Conference of this Stake 
will be held in the Fourteenth Ward Assembly 
Rooms of Friday, Sept. 18th; and on Saturday, 
19th, the Primary Conference will be held in the 
Salt Lake Assembly Hall, commencing at 10 
a. .m.; and the Young Ladies Conference will 
convene at 2 p. m. in the Fourteenth Ward. 

	ON Thursday, the 3rd, we attended a Primary
meeting in the 21st Ward, Mrs. Mary Davis pre-
siding.  The children were orderly, and rendered 
their exercises very creditably, and speeches 
suited to the occasion were made by the visitors 
present, among whom were Mrs. Hannah T. King,  
Dr. Ellen B. Ferguson, Mrs. Emma Toone, Mrs. 
Ellen C. Clawson, and others.  The singing exer-
cises were specially fine.  Sister Davis and coun-
selors deserve great credit for their energy and 
diligence in advancing the children, and promoting 
their spiritual improvement.


[Column 1]	
	VISIT TO SANPETE, AND MEETINGS.

	On Thursday morning, September 10th, we
started on a short visit to the sisters in Sanpete
Stake, expecting to attend the Conference of
Relief Society at Manti the following day.  At
Nephi we were joined by Mrs. Mary Pitchforth,
President of the Relief Society in Juab Stake, and 
together we journeyed onward to Moroni by train.
Arriving at Moroni, we dined at the Bishop's, and
a carriage was in waiting to convey the party to
Manti.  Our party consisted of four ladies and a 
little boy to drive, Eddie Christianson, a fine little
fellow and a very good charioteer.  Mrs. Deborah

[Column 2]
Irons joined us at Moroni, and Mrs. Ella Empey,
who, by the way, had been a traveling companion
all the way from Salt Lake, made up a very plea-
sant part for the ride to Manti.  We halted at
President Peterson's at Ephraim, and staid long
enough to rest the horses and take supper with
them,  and afterwards drove on to Manti.  The
next morning, Friday, September 11th, the Quar-
terly Conference of the Relief Society of Sanpete
Stake convened in the Council House at Manti,
Mrs. M. A. Hyde presiding.  There were present
of the brethren President Maiben, Patriarch J.
G. Bigler, of Nephi, Bishops and others.
	As the secretary did not arrive with the minutes
and reports, the morning was occupied in speak-
ing.  President Maiben offered up a very eloquent
prayer, and the singing was excellent.  The 
President, Mrs. Hyde, made a brief address, and
was followed by Mrs. Jane Casto, President of
South Ward Relief Society, and Sister Deborah
Irons, counselor in the Relief Society at Moroni.
Each of these gave a good report of the respective
branch to which she belonged.
	The meeting was then addressed by Mrs. Amy
L. Bigler and Mrs. Mary Pichforth, of Nephi,
and Mrs. E. B. Wells, of this city.
	President Maiben made some closing remarks,
expressing his appreciation of the labors of the
Relief Society, and encouraging the sisters to per-
severe in doing good.  Choir sang in a very
impressive manner "How beauteous are their feet,"
etc.  Benediction by Mrs. Rebecca Wanham.
	At the afternoon meeting two or three wagon
loads of peopel had come over from Ephraim—
President Peterson, Bishops Anderson and 
Dorius, among others.  Elder Frank Taylor was
also present to take charge of the choir.  Meeting
opened with spirited singing, and Bishop Ander-
son offered prayer.  After singing again, the
young secretary, Miss Mary Hyde, read the 
minutes of the previous Conference, which were
approved, and the statistical and financial reports
from various parts of the Stake.  Counselor Sarah
Peterson was the first speaker.  Her remarks were
principally upon the financial condition of the
Society, and said though they had over 5,000
bushels of wheat in the granaries, that was very
little if a famine was impending.  She also spoke 
of the factory that the sisters had anticipated
building, and spoke with much earnestness on
home manufacture and other similar subjects.
	President Hyde made some remarks on the
situation in which the people are placed at the
present time, and gave good advice and instruc-
tion to those associated with her in the Relief
Society, etc.
	Mrs. Hansen and Mrs. Ellen Dorius, of
Ephraim, and Mrs. Polly Ann Johnson, of Foun-
tain Green, represented the Society over which
each presided as in a favorable condition, and
added a testimony of the truth of the Gospel.  The
choir sang "Beautiful Home."
	Mrs. Rebecca Wareham, of Manti North Ward,
gave a report of the Society, and spoke upon the
appreciation of blessings, and alluded to the
storing of wheat, the exertions that had been
made in times past to glean, referred to the culti-
vation and manufacture of silk, and at the close
of her remarks, to the signs of the times.  Said
when we were driven from Nauvoo we were 
driven to a better land, etc.
	Mrs. E. B. Wells spoke upon the special mis-
sion of the sisters, of the labors in which they
were called to assist, of home manufacture; on
charity and integrity to all the principles of the
Gospel.
	President Canute Petersen referred to the exer-
tions that had been made to establish home in-
dustries, especially a tannery, and suggested the
appointing of a committee to ascertain the proba-
bilities of having a factory.  Nominated his
counselors, John B. Maiben and—Beal, as two
of said committee, and that they should have the


[Column 1]﻿ 
privilege of naming five, others to assist them.
Spoke mainly upon the persecutions the people
are enduring and said many good things, and
encouraged all to persevere, for those who would
keep a celestial law might expect to inherit a
celestial glory. Said what need the Saints care if
even a heavy storm does come and rock them
about, they have no fear as to the landing on safe
ground.		
	President Hyde put the motion of President
Petersen in reference to a committee of seven to
vote, and it was unanimously carried.
	Bishop Anderson of Ephraim then spoke a
short time upon the duties and labors of the
sisters and stated some of his views on the sub-
jects that had been introduced during the meeting.
	President Hyde adjourned the Conference until
the 19th of December, to be held at Ephraim.
Singing, “For the strength of the hills we bless
thee.” Benediction by Patriarch Bigler.
	After meeting we had the privilege of going
through the Temple and environs, and were much
interested in all that we saw and heard. Presi-
dent Maiben and wife invited us to drive out with
them to the “warm springs,” a short distance out
on the route to Mayfield. We accepted the invi-
tation with pleasure, and thoroughly enjoyed the
romantic scenery, the hills and vales, the fields of
grain, and the new mown hay with its ever grate-
ful fragrance, all added to the consciousness of
pleasure, and for the time we gloried in the
beauties of nature. In the evening we had a pleasant
call from Mr. Harrington, editor of the Manti
Sentinel, a new enterprise in that locality.
	The Young Ladies' Conference of Sanpete
Stake convened on Saturday morning, September
12th, Miss Helen Armstrong presiding. Minutes
of the previous Conference were read by the
secretary, Mrs. Parley Petersen, and general re-
marks were made by the young President calcu-
lated to give encouragement.
	Most excellent remarks were made by Mrs.
Mary Pitchforth to the young ladies upon im-
proving and educating their minds.
	One of the counselors of the Central Board
being present, spoke upon true womanhood, gave
excellent advice, and counseled the girls to
qualify themselves to be good and true wives and
mothers, and spoke of the motto of the Associa-
tion being improvement, and perfection the aim.
	The secretary read an essay on fashions, etc.,
which was very creditable.
	Mrs. E. B. Wells spoke of the method of con-
ducting the meetings, urged the secretaries to
make their minutes brief and as perfect as pos-
sible, entreated the young girls to be prompt in
every duty, and be careful what company they
kept, avoid all excesses and the keeping of late
hours, which  often led to evil. Spoke of the
influence young ladies might exercise upon so-
ciety, and advised them to set up a high standard
of excellence to attain to.
	Miss Olive Lowry recited in a very expressive
manner a poem entitled "One Stalk of Wheat."
	President Maiben gave a brief address, exhort-
ing the young girls to improve the opportunities
now afforded them of laying up wisdom and
knowledge, and alluded to the subjects which had
been treated upon.
	Remarks were made suitable to the occasion by
Patriarch Bigler.
	Elder Frank Taylor made a few spirited re-
marks at the close of the meeting, giving the
young girls some excellent advice. Singing and
benediction.
	Primary Conference at 2 p.m., Mrs. Helena
Madsen presiding. The exercises of the children
were well rendered, and the singing was heavenly.
We expect hereafter to publish in detail the
exercises.
	The same evening we attended a general meet-
ing at Ephraim, in their large and commodious

[Column 2]
meeting house; a very full attendance of brethren
and sisters.
	Sunday morning.—Visited the Sunday Schools
of the North and South Wards, Ephraim, and in
the evening met with the Young Ladies' Associa-
tion and Relief Society at Moroni.
	Monday.—We met with the Relief Society at
Nephi at 12 a.m., and dined at the residence of
Mrs. Rebecca Udall, with quite a pleasant party
of ladies. Altogether the visit to Sanpete was a
very pleasant affair, and we shall ever remember
the kind people we have met and associated with
in that Stake of Zion.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]	
	﻿FOREIGN CORRESPONDENCE.

MRS. EMMELINE B. WELLS:
	Dear Friend:—It would give me great pleasure
to send you a few lines from this far-off land that
would be interesting to the readers of the
EXPONENT,but as I am not gifted in that direction
you will have to look for that merit from some
other source. I can, however, tell you that the
EXPONENT comes to us as a most welcome visitor
in a strange and foreign land. We prize it not
only as a messenger coming fresh from our moun-
tain home, where so many of our dear ones are,
but as a valued and interesting companion and
friend, and an instructor of great worth. Our
foreign society here is very limited, and more so
at present than usual, as nearly all the Elders are
out among the people on this and the other islands
laboring in their callings as missionaries, while
the President and two or three brethren in charge
of important interests on the plantation, and three
foreign sisters with our little children, are all that
remain on the place, in company with the little
colony of native Saints, most of whom have
gathered here from other parts of the islands.
	Brother Farr has the general oversight and
management of this mission, and of the business
of this plantation.
	Brother Jos. S. Hyde, one of the sons of the late
Apostle Orson Hyde, is teaching an English
school here at the gathering place, and has about
49 pupils; all except one of these are Hawaiian
children from six to eighteen years of age, and
many of them are bright and intelligent, and are
making commendable progress in their study of
the English tongue.	
	Brother S. Allred has charge of the live stock
of the plantation, and furnishes the colony, es-
pecially those employed on the plantation, with
the principal part of their beef.
	Brother A. Westly is the “master mechanic,”
and finds ample employment in keeping the
machinery, fanning implements, etc., etc., in run-
ning order.		
	The Saints here have the finest looking meeting
house that I have seen on this island, outside of
Honolulu. It is certainly a credit to the place
and the builders.
	Their old meeting house is now being used
principally as a school-house, but still serves for
Priesthood meetings, testimony meetings, meet-
ings of the various associations, and for ordinary
purposes. Meetings are held in the large new
house from 10 to 12 every Sabbath morning and
from 8 to 5 p. m. The Saints meet in the old
meeting house for prayer and testimony. These
services are always conducted in the Hawaiian
language, the congregation being almost exclu-
sively natives. I notice that the native people,
both men and women, seem to be very fluent and
ready speakers. I have never yet seen either man
or woman falter for a word or hesitate in giving
expression to their thoughts. They are evidently
great talkers, and are very earnest and emphatic
in their gestures, but I will not now try to describe
the people. Besides the general meetings on the
Sabbath, Sunday School is held from 8 to 9.30
a. m., Mutual Improvement Associations meet

[Column 2]
between 1 and 3 p. m., and in the evening there is
a meeting for the foreign Elders and their families,
generally held in the mission house, occupied by
reading the Scriptures and commenting ther eon.
The reading of the Scriptures is joined in by both
the brethren and sisters, and is in English, but
the singing and prayer is generally done in the
native language, so as to aid the Elders in their
study thereof. There is a very efficient female
Relief Society at Laie, of which Sister Kapo
Keaakolo is the President, and Sister Kaahahui
and Napapale are her counselors. Sister Hana
Naihe is the secretary and Sister Kekuewa Moses-
the treasurer.
	There are three other societies on this island,
at Kahana, about eight miles from here, and at
Honolulu and Waikiki. There are also one or
more societies each on the island of Hawaii,
Maui, Molokai, and  Kauai, all in more or less
efficient working condition.
	At the last April Conference the General Presi-
dency of the Relief Society for the mission were
chosen as follows: E. Farr, President; Kaheaana
Kealakaihonua and Esther Kalua Coles, coun-
selors. The two counselors, who are very faithful
and intelligent native sisters, recently returned
from visiting the Relief Society of north-eastern
Hawaii, a conference over which Brother R. V.
Miller is presiding, consisting of the district of
Kohala Hamakua and Hilo, where they organized
and reorganized several branches, some of which
had become a little dilapidated for the need of
proper care and pruning.
	They contemplated making a trip on the same
business during this month to the island of
Kauai. Their expenses are borne by the Relief
Society, and as these sisters are excellent
speakers, and possessed of more than ordinary
intelligence, and of an excellent spirit they are
very efficient missionaries, as well as faithful
laborers in the society.
	At this place (Laie) there is a very commodious
and convenient frame building, erected by the
organizations of the islands, designed for the
assistance and convenience of such as might need
a temporary dwelling place on coming to the
gathering place, until they could make permanent
arrangements for themselves, and for the general
purposes of the Society at headquarters.
	I am happy to say we are all in the enjoyment
of good health, and peace and the good spirit seems,
to prevail.
	God bless you and the EXPONENT, and all the
faithful and true hearted in Zion. May the dark
clouds of persecution which seem to be gathering
blackness over our fair land, threatening devasta-
tion and sorrow, soon be dispersed by the over-
ruling power of God, in whom we trust, and yet
shower blessings upon the heads of the faithful,
instead of the destruction which they now threaten,
is my earnest prayer.  	
	Very Respectfully, Your Sister in the Gospel,
	J.
Laie, Oahu, S. I., Aug. 12d, 1885.


YOUNG JOSEPH. 

	Young Joseph as he is called, is at the present,
time in Utah, and because he is ths son, of the
Prophet there may be those who would naturally
enough wish to see and hear him mainly because
he is the son of the Prophet Joseph. But al-
though he is the son of the Prophet, he is at the
present time at the head of a Church composed-
mostly of apostates from the true Church of
Christ and our avowed enemies seeking in our
midst whom they may destroy.
	They are, or the most of them, are apostates of
the darkest hue. They have not only apostatized
from the true Church but they seek to destroy it
and agitate a warfare against the people of God,
who have retained their faith in the Gospel.
	The Josephites claim to be guided by


the "Doctrine and Covenants" and are carrying out
the work commenced by the Prophet Joseph. I
once listened to one of their elders for a few mo-
ments, who was talking to a few persons who had
been enticed together to hear what he had to say
when there was a pause in the conversation. I
asked him the following questions which he
answered.
	Do you believe in sending missionaries to the
world to preach?
	Only a few at the present time.
	Do you believe in the gathering of the Saints?
	Not now, but will by and by.
	No, and do not expect to do so untill we get to
Jackson County.
	Do your people pay any tithing?
	No we do not.
	Do you believe in celestial marriage?
	No.
	Do you believe in plural marriage?
	No we surely do not.
	No, said I, nor in any of the fundamental princi-
ples of the work of God, as taught by the Prophet,
Joseph, and I left him.
	They frequently quote the saying of Paul:
"Prove all things and hold fast to that which is
good." When we know that they are apostates
and are full of animosity toward the Church of
Christ and would destroy it if they could, and that
they are full of darkness (for they cannot take the
Holy Ghost with them) and, as Jesus said, "how
great is that darkness;" when we know that they
want to draw down with themselves as Satan does
all they can to destruction and nothing to give to
us but the filth of apostacy, why should we wish
to prove them or hear them preach.
	Now, I say, let us not go after them, for if we
do we therby bid them God speed, and His
Spirit will not go with us, and we may not be en-
abled to find it again for (there are principalities
and powers above over which we have no con-
trol) or if we should have made a blotch upon
the great white sheet spread out before us on
which to write our character for eternity. If we
go upon Satan's ground he will claim his own as
he did with Moses because he sinned. Then as
Paul admonishes us, when we think we stand let
us take heed lest we fall and shun the very appear-
ance of evil.
	S. F. A.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]	
	R. S. REPORT

	EPHRAIM, COLORADO.
	The first annual meeting of the Relief So-
ciety of the Ephraim Ward of the San Luis
Stake of Zion, held in the meeting house, Aug.
11, 1885.
	The house was very handsomely decorated
with evergreens and  flowers from Sister Bodelia
Poulson's flower garden.  There were many
beautiful quilts of all kinds of patterns hang-
ing up—stockings, linseys, laces, embroideries,
tidies, and pillow-slips, and one lovely knitted
bedspread knit by Sister L. M. Thoms, and
other nice things too numerous to mention.
Prest. A. M. Rasmussen presided in the meet-
ing.  Singing by choir.  Prayer by A. H. 
Rasmussen. Singing. Rollcalled, 25 mem-
bers present, 32 enrolled.  Minutes of the pre-
vious meeting read and approved.  Statistical
and financial report was then read.
	Prest. A. M. Rasmussen then gave thanks
for the decorating of the house, and gave very
good instructive advice and counsel, exhorting
us to press forward in future as in times past.
Several of the other sisters bore their testi-
monies and gave very good instructions.
	This Bishop and Counselors, Home Mis-
sionaries N. S. Whitney and Joseph Hunsaker,

[Column 2]
our school teacher, and Brother H. C. Heiselt
made excellent remarks.  Each bore a testi-
mony to the good spirit which was in our
midst.  Gave other good instructions.
	The following named officers were then sus-
tained in their positions.  A. M. Rasmussen,
president; Mary Coray and Mena Cornum,
counselors; Annie Otteson, secretary; Annie
Cornum, assistant secretary; Sorsena Heiselt,
treasurer.  Singing by choir.  Benediction by
the President; after which all, numbering
about 90, went to Sister Bodelia Poulson's 
where the tables were spread with abundance
of good things and dainties of all kinds, and
in the evening went forth in the dance, enjoy
ing ourselves very much, until 12 o'clock, when
all retired. We have a very peaceable com-
munity, living at peace with our enemies more
than is generally the case at present.  Crops
generally look well, and if frost keeps off we
shall be blessed with a bounteous harvest better 
than heretofore.
	Your Sisters in the Gospel,
	A. M. RASMUSSEN, Prest.
	ANNIE OTTESON, Sec.

	REORGANIZATION
	A meeting of the First Ward Relief Society 
was held in the First Ward meeting house on
Thursday, Sept. 3rd 1885.  There were pre-
senr, besides the officers and members of the
Relief Society, Bishop Joseph Warburton and
his counselors, Joseph Booth and Alexander
Steel.  The President, Sister Miriam G. Chase,
resigned her office as President on account of
old age, and the treasurer, Sarah J. Sement
also.  It was found necessary to reorganize the
Society, whereupon the following officers were
elected, and were set apart by the Bishop and
his counselors.  Margaret F. Steel, President;
Annie Young and Jane E. Van Tassel; coun-
selors; Annie S. Musser, secretary; Mary W.
Musser, treasurer; Caroline Grouch, President
of the Teachers. 

	UTAH STAKE.
	Minutes of Conference of Relief Society of
Utah County, held August 28th, 1885—Presi-
dent Mary John presiding with her counselors.
Meeting opened by singing and prayer. The
minutes of former Conference were read by
secretary; also reports from the different
branches throughout the county etc.
	President John in her remarks said we are,
according to our report, great helps to our
brethren in many ways—in helping to build
Temples, Tabernacles, and in caring for the
sick and the afflicted, the poor and the needy;
and although we have no visitors with us
to-day, I feel that we can edify one another.
We will hear verbal reports from the Presi-
dents of the several branches of the Relief
Society of the Stake, and this afternoon we
expect to be favored with the company of some
of the brethren who will speak to us. Since
our last Conference we have sustained another
loss in the deat of Sister Beebe, who was our
treasurer, as also President of the Silk Associ-
ation of this Stake.
	The Relief Society of the county was repre-
sented, with but few exceptions, by the respec-
tive Presidents, the reports being favorable as
to an increase of faith and good works, and a
determinaton to press forward to the end and
gain salvation and eternal lives.
	Sister Emily G. Cluff spoke of the loss we
had sustained since our last Conference in the 
death of Sister Hester Ann Beebe; and said in
the last two years we have been called to
mourn the loss of three of our dear sisters who
were wont to meet with us here at this stand
—Sister Margaret A. Gluff, counselor, and our
beloved Ma Smoot, our President, and now
Sister Beebe, our treasurer, who was also Stake
President of the Silk Association of Utah

[Column 3]
County. Sister Cluff exhorted the sisters to be
faithful in attending these meetings, and also
the fast meetings, and said the good these
organizations are doing will never be esti-
mated until the great day of reckoning; ex-
horted mothers to guard their children, pray
with them, and teach them to pray, etc.
	Conference adjourned till 2 p. m.
	Afternoon session was opened by the usual
exercises.  After a few remarks from Counselor
Marilla Daniels, the time was occupied by the 
brethren of the Presidency of the Stake.
	President H. H. Gluff being the first
speaker, spoke of our present persecution as
one of the greatest evidences of the truth of
this work.  God's purposes are peculiar, and
in direct opposition to the plans of man.  We
must maintain and promote the interests of 
this people, etc.
	President David John followed with good
counsels to all.
	President A. O. Smoot heartily endorsed the 
teachings we had received, and gave more
good counsel in regard to keeping out of
debt, and in regard to teaching the children
to be neat, industrious, humble and prayerful,
and not to follow after the fashions of
Babylon, etc.
	After a few more remarks from President
Mary John, Conference adjourned for three
months.  Benediction by Prest. A. O. Smoot.
	CAROLINE DANIELS, Sec.


Y. L. M. I. A. ﻿REUNION.

EDITOR EXPONENT:
	The Y. L. M. I. A. of the Fourth Ward,
Ogden, held a grand reunion on Wednesday,
August 26th, 1885.  It was in honor of the
parents of the members of the Society, and was
held in the meeting-house and in the pavilion
on Lester Park. At the meeting in the fore-
noon some excellent exercises were given in
the meeting-house. Sister Ruthinda E. Monch,
who has for six years been the President of the
association and given the utmost satisfaction,
resigned her position, which was accepted. At
the close of the forenoon meeting one of the
finest feasts ever given in Ogden awaited the
people in the pavilion. It was prepared by the
members. Three hundred persons feasted from
the good things, and there was much left which
was given to the poor. Among the prominent
persons present were: President L. W. Shurt-
liff and counsel; Sister Jane S. Richards, Stake
President, of the Relief Society; Sister Sarah
A. Herrick, Prest. Y. L. M. I. A.; Hon. Joseph
Stanford and wife, Hon. Jos. A. West and
wife, and presidents of the city societies. After
dinner a fine programme was carried out in the
pavilion, which had been beautifully decorated
for the occasion. The exercises were of the
highest order. Speeches were made by Presi-
dent Jane S. Richards and several of the
brethren. Sister Monch received deserved
credit for her diligent labors in behalf of the
association. In the evening nearly one hun-
dred couples enjoyed themselves greatly in a
pleasant party in the pavilion. The kindliest
feeling prevailed throughout. We give below
a well written essay on “The Object of the
Reunion,” written and read in the afternoon
by Mrs E. H. Anderson.

	OBJECT OF OUR REUNION.
	It seems hardly necessary to state the object
of a meeting of this class. It gives its own, on
the face of it. To write of the object of our
gathering to-day is like writing an introduc-
tion to a preface. But since it has been alloted
to me, there can be no harm in an expression
that may explain more fully, perhaps, than
the pleasant spirit that animates us all, why
we are assembled here on this occasion.


A reunion is a meeting of associates, and
may be for specified purposes.  Reunions are
held to meet old friends, to celebrate events,
to show respect for classes, people, or societies.
Our meeting to-day is a reunion to show re-
spect, in every sense of the word, to our fathers
and mothers whom we have invited to spend a
few hours with us.  While we trust we have so
conducted ourselves that our parents are well
aware that we venerate, love, and respect them,
yet we have invited them here to show that the
same regard which we cherish for them pri-
vately, we delight to show them in the society 
of our friends and the public.
	There is sweet melody in the sound of father
and mother.  We love to pronounce these
words.  The sound delights us as the merry
ripple of the brook; and we love them as the 
return of spring with the music of birds. When
we think of their labors for us, none among us 
can fail to feel the importance of their mission,
nor see the magnitude of their works.  They
have done many things for our good.  In re-
gard to parents, we are somewhat differently
situated from many of the children of the
earth.  Our parents were reformers.  They
marked out for themselves, early in life, a path
which varied from that in which the majority
of mankind trod, and for this innovation re-
ceived the jeers of the cold world.  They kept
steadfast, however, for their guide was the
Lord, and their trust the power of Omnipotence.
There is no necessity here to more than re-
mind the audience of the cares, labors, trials,
temptations and obstacles that have silvered
the hairs of the noble men and women whose
labors have brought us peace and plenty. 
They are too well known to need recounting.
The lesson we may learn, however, from their
experience should not be passed over so easily
and so lightly.  In all their tribulation they
were yet prosperous and contented.  This
prosperity and contentment sprang from their
obedience to God and to the whisperings of the
Holy Spirit,  so that in all their adversity they
could yet rejoice and praise God.  Through
His blessings they have made the desert waste
bloom with the prosperity of civilization. Their
homes have been blessed with children who
have grown up in the fear of the Lord, while
all around the Giver of all good has smiled
upon them with prosperity.  In common with
them, we enjoy all these mercies to-day, and
while so doing, it is highly proper to remember
the circumstances that brought these about, 
the vicissitudes that createdthem under the
power and guidance of God.  It is proper to
meet together, and, reflecting upon the past,
remember that it is our parents' adversity
which has led to our prosperity; their inspira-
tion which has led to our knowledge; their 
faith and works which inspire us to diligence
in the great work which lies before us.  It is
proper and well that we meet together in re-
union in honor of our parents, to show that we
appreciate what they have done for us, that
the love of their labors, toils, trials, is not for-
gotten by their children.  We cannot do
enough to show our appreciation, but they may
rest assured that we love them.  As sons and
daughters of such noble sires—men and 
women who battled and overcame, who planted
the feet of prosperity in the desert—we meet,
in a land which you have made delightful, to
welcome you to our little reunion, to extend
our love to you, to ask the Father of all to
continue his blessings upon you.
	We can offer you no great things, but as
your children we meet, and with one accord,
in songs and voices of praise, gratitude, love,
make the proud heavens of our free land ring
from mountain to mountain with your chil-
dren's "God bless you."
	REPORTER.
Ogden, Utah, Aug. 27, 1885.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
DIALOGUE.

BY L. K. YOUNG.

[Characters: Mary, Minnie, Rose, Ethel, Elsie,
Katie.]

	Mary.—Now, girls, as we have met in ac-
cordance with our appointment, we will pro-
ceed to give an account of  the different
organizations of the sisters, and the object of
their organization, or what items of informa-
tion we have been able to get.
	Minnie.—The Relief Society comes first,
does it not?
	Rose.—Yes; that was the first one organized,
	Ethel.—That was instituted by Joseph
Smith; but ma has forgotten the date of its 
organization.
	Elsie.—It was organized March 17th, 1842.
	Katie.—Has it been continued from that
time until the present?
	Ma.—No; it was inoperative from 1846, the
time of the exodus of the Saints from Nauvoo,
until 1855, when it was reorganized it Salt
Lake City.
	Eth.—And now there are many branches
of the Relief Society.
	Min.—Yes; there are many branches, but
they are all working in unison, striving to
assist in the building up of the Kingdom of
God upon the earth.
	Ma.—I have an extract from the history of
Joseph Smith, which defines the duties of the
Relief Society, and gives the names of the first
officers.
	Rose.—I, too, have an extract.
	Eth.—And I.
	El.—Mary, please give us your extract, and
then the rest of us will follow with our infor-
mation.
	Ma.—I can repeat it.  He says:  "March 24,
—I attended, by request, the Female Relief
Society, whose object is the relief of the poor,
the destitute, the widow, and the orphan, and
for the exercise of all benevolent purposes.
Its organization was completed this day.  Mrs.
Emma Smith takes the presidential chair;
Mrs. Elizabeth Ann Whitney and Mrs. Sarah
Ann Cleveland are her counselors; Miss El-
vira Cole is treasurer; and our well-known
and talented poetess, Miss Eliza R. Snow,
secretary." 
	Eth.—In speaking of the sisters of our
Church, he says:  "Our ladies have always
been signalized for their acts of benevolence
and kindness; but the cruel usage they have 
received from the barbarians of Missouri has
hitherto prevented their extending the hand of
charity in a conspicuous manner."
	Min.—On another occasion he said:  "I met 
the members of the Female Relief Society, and
after presiding at the admission of many new
members, gave a lecture on the Priesthood,
showing how the sisters would come in pos-
session of the privileges, blessings, and gifts of 
the Priesthood, and that signs should follow 
them—such as healing the sick, casting out
devils, etc., and that they might attain unto
these blessings by a virtuous life, and conver-
sation, and diligence in keeping all the com-
mandments."
	Ka.—On page 49 "Women of Mormondom"
I found an item which I will repeat:  "Because
of these organizations Utah has no poor-
houses.  Under the kind and sisterly policy of 
this society the worthy poor feel much less
humiliated, and are better supplied, than any
alms-house system extant.  By an admirable
arrangement, under the form of visiting com-
mittees with well defined duties, the deserving
subjects of charity are seldom, if ever, ne-
glected or overlooked."
	Rose.—Where does the Relief Society get
their means to work with?
	El.—They had but little to begin with, but
by working they add to their capital, and the

[Column 2]
Lord is sure to bless everybody who works for
the building up of his kingdom, whether 
individually or collectively.
	Eth.—Some  branches of the Society have
built houses to meet in.
	Rose.—I wonder where the first Relief
Society house was built?
	Ma.—In the Fifteenth Ward, Salt Lake
City.
	Min.—The laying of the corner-stone of that 
building was performed by women.
	El.—On page 191 "Women of Mormondom"
I found an item which I will repeat:  "This
ceremony—the laying of the corner-stone—
being performed, was followed by appropriate
speech-making on the part of the presiding
officers of the society, Mrs. S. M. Kimball,
Eliza R. Snow, and others, each in turn
mounting the corner-stone for a rostrum, and
each winning deserved applause from the as-
sembled thousands."
	Ka.—You will find in vol. iv., page 287,
"Times and Seasons," the first annual report
of the Relief Society.  They received during
that year in money, clothing, and provisions
507 dollars.  Of that, 306 dollars and 48 cents
was expended for the relief of the poor.
	Ma.—The Relief Society Central Board was
organized June 19th, 1880, with the following
named officers:  Mrs. E. R. S. Smith, President;
Mrs. Zina D. H. Young, Mrs. Elizabeth Ann
Whitney, counselors; Mrs. Sarah M. Kimball,
secretary; and Mrs. M. Isabella Horne, trea-
surer.
	Min.—Since then Sister Whitney died.
	Ma.—Yes; but the vacancy caused by her
death has not yet been officially filled.
	Eth.—Was the Young Ladies' Association
the next one organized?
	Ma.—Yes; that was the second organization
of the women, but there was an organization
in Nauvoo called the Young Gentlemen's and
Ladies Relief Society.  They held their first
meeting one evening in the latter part of
January, 1843.  But their organization was
not completed until the 21st of March the
same year, with William Walker, President;
William Cutler, Vice-President; Lorin Walker,
treasurer; and James Monroe, secretary.
	Rose.—I have never heard of that before.
	Ka.—Mary read the account of their or-
ganization to several of us a few days ago.
	Rose.—What book is it in; I would like to
read it?
	Ma.—The "Times and Season," vol. iv.,
page 154.
	Min.—I was there and heard her read it.
They had such good meetings.  Joseph Smith
and others of the leading men attended their
meetings, and gave them very good instruc-
tions.  But Brother Heber C. Kimball took 
the lead in organizing them.
	Ma.—I will try to repeat an extract, which
may be found on page 155, vol. iv., "Times
and Seasons."  President Smith was present
at one of their meetings, when "he expressed
his gratitude to Elder Kimball in the strongest
terms for having commenced and carried on
in so masterly a manner the good and glorious
work he had undertaken.  He said it would be
the means of doing much good, and of benefit-
ing his young friends more than they were
aware of; that the gratitude of all good men,
and of the young people whom he had so much
benefited, would follow him through life, and
when grey hairs should his temples adorn he
could look back with pleasure upon the winter
of 1843 when he was engaged in promoting
the cause of benevolence, and preparing his
young friends for the glorious career which
awaited them."
	Ka.—I will repeat another extract from the
same article, on page 157:  "If the youth
throughout the land would follow this good
example, and form themselves into such so-
cieties, there would be much less sin, iniquity,


misery and degredation among the young
people than there is at the present day. There
would not be as many suffering poor, neither
would there be as much immorality among
the people; but, on the contrary, peace, good
order, happiness, cheerfulness and plenty
would reign in the land, the Lord would look
down from his holy habitation, and smile upon
us and bless us all."
	El.—The Young Ladies' Mutual Improve-
ment Association was first organized in May,
1867, in the Lion House, by Eliza R. S.
Smith, under the supervision of President
Young, with the instruction that his daughters
should compose the first organization. It be-
ing entirely a new move, so far as acting in
organized capacity was concerned, she thought
it advisable to appoint more than two coun-
selors, but afterward they were reduced to
two.
	Rose.—Can any of you tell who the first
officers were?
	Eth.—I can, all but the treasurer; I could
not learn who held that position at first. And
they had no treasurer, as they had no use for
one at that time. Mrs. Ella Young Empy
was President; Mrs. Mary E. Young Croxall,
Miss Maria Young, now Mrs. Dougall, Miss
Phebe Young, now Mrs. Beattie. Miss Julia
Young, now Mrs. Burton, Miss Zina Young,
now Mrs. Williams, were counselors.
	Min.—The Central Board presiding over all
the Y. L. M. I. A. in all the world was or-
ganized June 19th, 1880, with Mrs. Elmina S.
Taylor, President; Mrs. Maggie Y. Taylor,
Miss Mattie Horne, now Mrs. Tingey, coun-
selors; Miss Louie M. Wells, secretary; and Mrs.
Fanny Young Thatcher, treasurer.
	Rose.—What is the object of this as-
sociation?
	Ma.—The name of the Association signifies
its object—mutual improvement—in behavior,
order, punctuality, refinement; in short, we
should as children of the Latter-day Saints
improve in all things.
	Min.—Yes, we should improve all the time.
Every day we can make a little advancement
towards perfection, and then we will not any
more than be prepared for the trials that we
may be called to pass through.
	Ka.—I think we had better take up the
Primary Association. They are a very essen-
tial organization. There we take our first
lessons in a public capacity, in bearing testi-
mony, giving recitations, etc.
	Rose.—And we are taught many good and
useful lessons, which if we give heed to will
prepare us for future usefulness.
	El.—The first Primary Association was
organized in the autumn of 1878, in Farming-
ton, Davis Co., with Mrs. Aurelia S. Rogers
President; and in Salt Lake City, with Mrs.
Louie Felt President. The one in Farmington
was partially organized August 11th, but was
not completed until after the one in Salt Lake
City, which was in the Eleventh Ward.
	Ma.—The Central Board was organized the
same day the others were, with Mrs. Louie
Felt, President; Mrs. Matilda M. Barratt and
Mrs. Clara C. Cannon, counselors; Mrs. Lillie
Freze, secretary; and Miss Minnie Felt, trea-
surer.
	All.—And now the Relief Society has on
hand over 40,000 bushels of grain laid up
against a time of famine.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


﻿IN MEMORY.

	Of our dear friend and sister Mary, daughter of John
F. and Elizabeth Snedaker, died June 21st 1885, of
diptheria. She was bom April 17th 1867, in Mill Creek,
where she lived and died, truly a Latter-day Saint, being
18 years, 2 months and 4 days old.
	Her nature was such that made her highly respected by all
who knew her. And we may say, in the best sense of the
word, that Mary was a superior young lady; possessing


that innate refinement, which gives sublimity to womanly
character; added to this were gentleness and genuine
kindness, which won for her a great circle of friends.
Her whole soul seemed to be wrapped up in the study of
music, thus bringing sunshine in all our gatherings. She
was an active member in our Sabbath School, and was
ever striving to improve those talents with which her
Maker had most generously endowed her. And as
such she will ever live as a bright spark in the memories of
all, both old and young.
	It seemed that our dear sister was too good for this earth.
That mighty and noble spirit was too pure to be long en-
cased in that mortal casket. A greater and more glorious
sphere awaited her.
	She was permitted to see angels once or twice before she
died, and also had the privilege of seeing some of her
friends who had gone before. She retained consciousness un-
til the last, giving instructions as to her burial etc., besides
giving a great deal of good and beneficial advice.
	Though hard indeed to part with one so good and
kind, yet one great comfort to the parent is to know that
God has blessed them with such a beautiful and noble
daughter; and to know that by faithfulness to the Gospel,
they will meet her again, filled with all the light and in-
telligence possible.
	We extend our sympathies to the bereaved ones; for we,
too, have sorrowed, and know how to appreciate the tears
which flow, when we are called to part with those whose
affections are entwined around our hearts. But, in all
our trials and afflictions we should remember this: "God
knows best."
	"Sweet be her sleep, while her loss we deplore, 
	Thoughts of her virtues, increase more and more,
	'Twill be long e'er the sting of her absence is o'er.
	God grant we may meet on heaven’s bright shore, 
	Where sorrow and parting shall grieve us no more."
		N. S.

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.﻿

The Rights of the Women of Zion, and the Rights of the Women of all Nations.

VOL. 14	SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, OCTOBER 1, 1885.	No. 9.


"AT REST!"

Through mossy tombstones, old and grey,
That near a ruined abbey lay, 
	Pensive I strayed;
So dim th' inscriptions on the stones,
Unknown the names of those whose bones
	Beneath them laid.
Unnoticed, came the ev'ning sky;
Unminded rose the unconscious sigh
	Up from the breast,
(Dreaming, perhaps, of brighter days,)
When, full beneath my wond'ring gaze
	I saw "At Rest!"
Two words, there, full upon the sod,
As if, with faces turned to God
	In calm repose;
No tombstone near, no fresh-made monnd,
No tear-dimmed eye, no ling'ring sound
	That spoke of woes.
But, like a daisy in the grass,
Unseen of thoughtless as they pass,
	(Half hid in green,)
Mayhap by loving eyes were traced,
By loving hands on tomb were placed
	These words, I ween.
Nor was it dreamed these words, by chance,
Might stay the careless, slow advance
	Of wayward feet;
Nor thought they from that tomb should fall
And, to a thoughtless mind recall
	That life is fleet,
That days are few; that hours speed fast;
That, though we strive from first to last
	With steadfast strain,
Life's worthy battle ne'er is won--
Something that's needful to be done
	Will yet remain.
Some wayward habit to abjure;
Some ling'ring thought, not wholly pure,
	A joyless hate
That from the heart must needs be torn;
From some friend's path to pluck a thorn--
	'Tis ne'er too late.
Thus striving on, life must be spent,
Still something to forgive, repent,
	Ere yet we're blest;
Ere yet the soul speeds unto God;
Ere yet beneath the freshen'd sod
	We're laid "At Rest!"
"At Rest!" At Rest!" O solemn thought!
With deep, with soulful quest'nings fraught,
	Thou fill'st the mind;
And joy too bring'st. What can express
The rapture spirits feel that press
	On, unconfined!
To think the madden'd, fever'd brain
Shall rest--life's never ending strain
	At length must cease,
To feel the bleeding heart no more
Shall bleed, to know the test is o'er!
	Ah! This is peace!
Ere yet the spirit leaves this coil,
But once to gaze back on the toil
	Of weary life,
And, seeing, know (while weak and frail,
While much remains o'er which to wail--
	Unconquer'd strife,)
Thou still hast won th' approving smile
Of Him o'erwatching all the while,
	Then art thou blest!
Bound, Spirit! at thy ransomed birth!
Sleep, Body, in the waiting earth!
	And sigh "At Rest!" --Millenial Star. 
	R. W. Sloan.


[Column 1]

﻿GIRLS, DON'T MISTAKE BRASS FOR
GOLD.

	The bestowal of the affections of a woman
is a strange subject, perhaps, to dilate upon,
but one which, in my estimation, should be
dwelt upon and discussed far more than it has
hitherto been, for by the bestowal may come
joy and happiness, or woe and heartache.
	Who would wish for shadow when they could
have sunshine? Who would wish for hell
when they could gain heaven? Methinks I
can answer for all—not one. Then why should
we not be more wise and discreet in the choice
of a husband, when, by the selection, may
come life-long sorrow, tears, and wishes for an
early grave.
	How often some of us are fascinated by the
bland, wicked smile of one of the opposite
sex, and fall a victim to the snarer. Such is
the case many times with a young, thoughtless
girl, and often those more mature are thus in-
fluenced. How many rush headlong into
wedded life,never pausing to think,or to realize
the importance of the step they are about to
take; some, perhaps,with a very short acquaint-
ance. But alas! they awaken to a realization
of the extent of their folly all too late; they
know nothing of one another’s tastes and
habits, therefore they are not very likely to
agree; one quarrel brings on another, until a
final separation ensues, or they drag out a life
of misery and despair together; this is most in-
variably the case with too hasty courtships and
marriages.
	Oft times one fancies herself in love with a
gentleman whose main forte, in making him-
self agreeable, are his expressive looks, wit and
nonchalant manner. They wed; he, of course,
soon tires of always showing this part of his
character; he then becomes surly and quiet,
and she finds to her sorrow that all that attract-
ed her, or caused her to imagine herself in love,
has flown. She has found out the truth of the
maxim, "All that glitters is not gold," too
late.
	Then we should be careful not to mistake
brass for gold, dross for silver—to be sure that
we have the real, genuine metal; then we are
more confident of happiness.
	Sitting by my open window, this afternoon,
busily employed with writing material, two
pictures are visible to my mind's eye, which I
shall try to describe.

FIRST PICTURE.
	I see a beautiful girl sitting alone in her
boudoir. She is about to write, for she has
two letters before her; two proposals of mar-
riage. She seems in a dilemma which to ac-
cept. Hush! These words escape her lips:
"Which shall it be? Both rich and handsome,
and one so witty and gay, that I am always so
pleased when in his society; the other, while
wealthy, is so quiet and dignified, I am quite
sure I would be miserable with him, or I
should be afraid of him, and I think I love the
other best."
	The beautiful head bends lower over her
writing; she pens the word that silently seals
her fate. In one brief moment she has marred
the happiness of her whole life-time. She has
unconsciously mistaken brass for gold.
	I look a little ahead, perhaps two or three
years; I see that same beautiful being, but
alas! not that same bright, happy face; there is
a hapless look in those dark, mournful eyes, as

[Column 2]

she stands on the open verandah, awaiting her
husband's return home. Ah! he comes; does
she spring forward to meet him as of yore?
Oh, no! If she had, think you those arms
would have opened to receive her in a warm
embrace? To all appearance they would not.
A cool "Good evening," is his greeting. They
pass into the sitting room, where all betokens
luxury. A servant enters and announces tea.
They enter the dining room. While seated
there only now and then an occasional word
passed from either of them. After their even-
ing meal, they pass back to the sitting room;
she to while away her time with some fancy
work, which I see in her listless fingers, he to
don slippers, and to throw himself down on a
low sofa with a novel in his hand, and soon
reads himself to sleep; while his wife—the
work has fallen from her hands, and her eyes
are closed in thought; not in slumber, but in
thought; nor do I think they can be very pleas-
ant reflections, by the painful shadow that now
and then creeps over her face, and by the way
those small white hands are clenched, as they
are interlocked in one another. A tear steals
silently down her cheek. She hastily wipes
all traces of it away, and softly walks to where
her sleeping husband lies. She bends over
him with a woe-begone look her face, and
words escape her lips. I plead guilty of
eaves-dropping, and bend nearer to listen: "So
unlike what he was before we were wed; then
he was always so witty, gay and kind; then I
was happy; but it did not, could not last." A
sob escapes her lips, as she turns and silently
leaves theroom.

SECOND PICTURE.
	Listen, while I disclose to you this beautiful
picture. 
	In the parlor of an aristrocatic mansion, a
slim, graceful figure, elegantly attired, pacing
to and fro as if in thought; finally she com-
munes to herself aloud. "Alas! the time has
come when I must make a decision between
these two gentlemen. One rich, witty, dashing
in manners, and, I believe, warm-hearted; but
the question is, would this last always? If not,
what would there be to attract or draw my at-
tention afterward? I do not think he is often
serious. I must choose wisely, for the happi-
ness of my life is at stake; I must not imagine
I love unless I do."
	"This other gentleman, although not wealthy,
when in his society, he seems towering to
heights far above me, in intellect, and—(and a
delicate blush mantles her cheek)—I think I
could easily learn to love him, and I should be
sure of happiness, as he is so intelligent and
refined."
	There is a determination in those soft blue
eyes, as this part of the vision slowly fades
away.
	Again I see her. She is on a lawn in front
of her residence, racing and romping with
three beautiful children, who are laughing and
crowing in childish glee; but who comes silent-
ly and unperceived on the background? It is
a man of noble, commanding presence. His
arms are folded across his chest, a smile hovers
around that stern mouth, as, unseen, he watches
the happy group, and a fond look steals into
those keen, dark eyes. Finally he advances
toward them; they perceive him and rush for-
ward, and each in turn receives a fond caress.
Surely she has not mistaken brass for gold.
No. I will draw back unpreceived and leave
them to their happiness.


These two scenes have convinced me that in-
telligence and nobility of character will surely
beget true love. Our religion teaches us to
keep ourselves pure, holy and truthful; to re-
gard the marriage vow as something too sacred
to be trifled with. And then again, when we
think of the great privileges we have of marry-
ing for time and eternity, it behooves each
party to think long and well before taking that
all-important step. We should make the
choice of a husband a matter of prayer,
		IDA.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]

SCENES AND INCIDENTS AT
WINTER QUARTERS.

BY HELEN MAR WHITNEY.

(Continued.)

	When hearing the rehearsal of trials, priva-
tions and hardships endured by the camp of
Israel, while journeying from the Mississippi
River to the Missouri, my mind inadvertently
turns to that portion between Nauvoo and
Garden Grove, as the darkest in my memory.
We were poorly prepared for such an exodus;
and many joining us who were anxious to come,
but had little or nothing of the necessaries for
their own sustenance, ours had to be divided
among them. We had many weeks of cold,
stormy weather, and our teams being insuffic-
ient to draw the loads, rendered traveling over
those soft praries next thing to an impossibility;
expecting, as we did, to go over the mountains
that year we were put on rations, and had to
lengthen out our flour and provisions in the
most economical manner. During that time
our sea biscuit, crackers, parched corn meal,
etc., which were among the luxuries, molded,
until finally they were fed out to our horses
and cattle. At the beginning of the journey
the crackers went very well to eat dry, but
I'll never forget the first time I saw a meal
made of sea biscuit broken into milk. I had
called at Uncle Brigham's tent—I had always
addressed him by that title—where he was
just taking some for dinner, and he invited me
to have a bowl; but I declined, with thanks,
and a feeling of wonder how he could relish it.
When it came to sitting down daily to milk
and water porridge and crackers in it, it be-
came so nauseous that hunger could not tempt
me to eat it.
	At this period the young, and even aged
people were forced to walk, if it were possible,
a goodly portion of the way to save the teams,
no matter what the weather might be, storms,
or excessive heat, or how weary, faint and foot-
sore we became, there was no alternative but
to endure till we had reached the spot called
Garden Grove. Here they unpacked and
sent out many valuables into the Missouri set-
tlements and exchanged them for provisions
and the most needful articles. They also
traded horses for oxen and milch cows, and
from that time our living was more comfort-
able. Father's and Bishop Whitney's families
were divided up into messes, each tent having
one or two women to cook for the teamsters,
and wagons were provided for all the women
and their little children to ride and to sleep in
at night. Although we were not rid of hard-
ships and vexations, consequent upon the
moving of a great camp, and the intense heat
of the weather, we certainly had little cause
for complaint compared with what we had ex-
perienced previously, and the rest of our jour-
ney was made with comparative ease.
	The whooping cough, which had so sorely
afflicted my mother's babe, Brigham Willard,
previous to starting from Nauvoo, grew lighter
from the time we commenced camping out at
Sugar Creek, and Mother Whitney's babe,
Mary, which had been sick unto death, im-
proved rapidly from that date. There were

[Column 2]

others, and even some of the most feeble and
delicate women, that grew stronger for a time,
until after the hot weather set in. Some of
the strongest, who were more exposed, became
prostrated with fever and ague, etc., before
arriving at Chariton River, where we stopped
a time for the weather to settle. My mother's
little one had commenced to run down with
teething, etc., and her own health, which was
never good, failed to that degree that Mary
Ann Shefflin, one of father's wives, who had
lived with her in Nauvoo, being anxious to re-
lieve her, took the child, and she loved and
cared for him as tenderly as though he had
been her own, till after we arrived at the end
of our journey.
	Lately, in looking over some old papers, I
found a letter of exhortation and counsel to
father's family, written by my husband, at his
dictation, as he was about leaving Cutler's
Park to go with President Young to attend to
some business over the river. It is dated
August 22nd, 1846, my 18th birthday, and
had been preserved among father's papers. The
words being as appropriate to day as then, I
reproduce a portion for the benefit of those
who regarded him in the light of a friend and
wise counselor.
	"I wish to give a few words of counsel to
my family and to all that belong thereto—both
male and female, old and young, and what I
say unto one I say unto all, and shall expect
it to be heeded in the full sense of the word.
	Of late there has been a great deal of care-
lessness, imprudence and slothfulness with
many of the members of my family as regards
to taking care of themselves—going without
suitable clothing and stockings in cold, chilly
weather, sitting out of doors under the bower
after dark, when it is cold, wet weather, expos-
ing themselves to sickness and death, which we
have suffered much of. These things have
caused me much sorrow and regret, and have
caused my family much toil and hardship and
fatigue, which has been brought upon us by a
total neglect of counsel, which would have
saved us these things.	*	*	*	*
I am now about leaving you for a short time,
and I improve this opportunity to address you
in this manner. I love my family, and I have
never faltered for the first time in seeking their
welfare, to provide everything that lay in my
power for their happiness, and still intend to,
inasmuch as my counsel can be respected. Now,
my feelings are this: That all domestic affairs,
as far as women are concerned, are out of the
way 'while it is called day,' for when the night
cometh no man can work, for the Lord says,
in a certain revelation, 'Retire to thy bed
early, that thy body may be invigorated, that
thy days may be many.'
	"Now, I shall require these things at your
hands, except circumstances shall otherwise
direct. Cease from vanity, seek humility and
meekness, bow before the Lord in the morning
and at eve, and in so doing your days may be
many, peace and tranquility shall soothe your
bosoms, health and happiness shall dwell in
thy wagons, tents, wigwams and cottages,
while these things are observed. Cease from
murmuring or complaining, finding fault with
your friends or with each other, but manifest
to all men and women, by your works, and the
closeness of your tongues, and the integrity of
your hearts, that you are right, and that God
is with us of a truth. I always do, and shall
expect that those which constitute my house,
will remember me before the Lord, that I may
have wisdom, health, patience and endurance
to endure all things, that I may always give
suitable counsel to my family, even that that
shall proceed from the Holy Ghost, for I never
wish to counsel by any other spirit.
	I now feel to bless you all in the name of
the Lord God of Israel, with peace, prosperity
and salvation, that we all may be pure in

[Column 3]

heart. These are the words and blessings I
feel to communicate and leave with my dear
family.
	As ever, I remain, your most affectionate
husband, father and friend in the new and ever-
lasting covenant. Amen."
	HEBER C. KIMBALL.


REQUISITE FOR GOOD HOUSE-
KEEPING.

	A great deal is said now-a-days in favor of 
good housekeeping. Much of it is well said.
But one essential to good housekeeping is never
mentioned. It is never said that the "wives of
well-to-do men should not be made paupers."
Yet this is really the word that most needs to
be said.
	The great majority of wives who devote
themselves to their families are as destitute of
money as the poorest pauper. This is not al-
ways so because the husband is niggardly.
More often, perhaps, it is from thoughtlessness.
For instance, one wife, before her first child
was born, was offered no money for its little
wardrobe. She could not ask for any. She
cut up her own fine garments to make a supply.
She did the same for the second. Before the
birth of the third child, she said to her hus-
band, "I have no money. I have cut up all
my clothes that were suitable, for the other
children. How am I to dress this one?" The
surprised husband said: "Why did you not
tell me you needed money?" It had never
occurred to him to offer any. He was a farmer,
whose wife, without help, cooked for the family
and for the hired men. She made butter and
cheese, and did the family sewing. All her
time was consumed in her housework and the
care of her children. Before her marriage
she had a remunerative business. She had the
means of gratifying her tastes, her wishes and
her feelings. How it must have taken out of
her, courage and heart, to find toil, day after
day, neither lightened nor brightened by the
hope of even small means with which to do
as she pleased! This man and woman repre-
sent a large class.
	But, whatever the class, the custom prevails
of leaving the wife without money, and this is
as common among the rich and well-to-do as
anywhere.
	Now, it is easy to see that a pauperized wife
is not in the best position for good home-mak-
ing or good housekeeping, to say nothing of
anything else. The first and last word of
those who really desire to improve the home
should be one to improve the condition of the
home-maker, in the special direction of reliev-
ing her from a penniless state. Much has
been said and written in favor of good house-
keeping. Now let us hear something of the
way to help wives to such a share of the family
income, be it little or much, as shall save them
from the sense of being either beggars or pau-
pers. This is one essential requisite to secure
good housekeeping.	L. S.


"Lady Tennyson is in a state of ill health
that causes her friends great anxiety."
	Mrs. Helen Jackson left several unpublished
manuscripts. Her last work was a story of
humble life in the West, entitled "Zeph." It
was finished during her fatal illness, and is
now, with other manuscripts, in the hands of
her publishers, Messrs. Roberts Brothers. A
new edition of the "Century of Dishonor,"
carefully revised, and with additional matter
is soon to be published by Roberts Brothers.
Mrs. Jackson's latest poem, "A Rose Leaf,"
will be published, with illustrations, in Outing
for October. Of this poem, Mrs. Jackson
wrote to the editor that she actually dreamed
it, and awoke with it on her lips.—Ex.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


RESPONSE.

[TO ONE WHO WROTE IN THE EXPONENT "FAREWELL
TO LOVE.]

Love on!
	Love on! let none freeze up the heart,
	For love will ever youth and life impart;
	A precious gift, our Father gave to man,
	To aid him, God's eternal truth to scan.

Love on!
	Love on! when in the prime of youth
	Our spirit clings to beauty and to truth;
	Nor dream a foe may lurk in smiling guise,
	And drive the beauteous phantom from our eyes.

Love on!
	Love on! in life's maturer age,
	When time, with iron pen fills up the page,
	O'er which we sigh and often drop the tear,
	As scenes are shifting by us year by year.

Love on!
	Love on! in life's declining age
	The youthful and mature become the sage;
	Three score and ten may number out our day,
	But still love on until we pass away.

		Love on! Love on!
			HANNAH T. KING.

Salt Lake City, Sep. 26th, 1885.


ALL WILL BE WELL.

BY ELLA WHEELER WHEELER WILCOX,

All will be well. I heard this blest assurance
	Flung o'er the borders of the unseen spheres,
It gave me faith and courage and endurance
	To walk serenely on and meet the years.
Like the sweet voice of some consoling spirit,
	Down through the silence of the night it fell.
My soul's fine ear was rightly tuned to hear it:
		"All will be well,"

All will be weli. Why should we ever doubt it?
	There were no blunders in creation's plan.
When God's vast mind conceived, and went about it,
	He was not aided or controlled by man.
The stars that move in such immortal beauty
	Through their appointed pathways seem to tell 
Our questioning souls, if we but do our duty,
		"All will be well,"

All will be well. Let not our hearts be troubled
	By passing clouds or shadows that may fall,
We must press bravely on with faith redoubled;
	The glorious end will justify it all.
I will believe that voice from heaven's portal
	Clear as the utterance of a silver bell—
It spoke to me a truth that is immortal:
		"All will be well,"—Advance.


[Column 1]

"MORMON" CREED.

"MIND YOUR OWN BUSINESS."

	Yes, Mr. Hendricks spoke as follows: "Fel-
low citizens!" We, the Latter-day Saints,
away out in Utah, are fellow citizens, and
claim every right pertaining thereto. Our
rights are assailed and we are meddled with by
United States officials, who are sent here to
mind our affairs, when we know how to govern
them ourselves much better, and have not cost
Government half so much as uninterested
officials, sent here to feather their own nests
and deprive the people of every vestige of
right guaranteed to American citizens. We
recognize the right and power of the general
government, but when it affects you and me
and the people of Utah, then it is that Utah
makes her own laws and votes in her own
officers by the voice of her own subjects, born
on her own soil, or are permanent residents;
those who are honorable, self-sustaining men,
who have built up Utah, by cultivating the
soil, who have taken an interest for the people,
made good and wholesome laws for the estab-

[Column 2]

lishment of virtue, peace and good, moral con-
duct, and order and thrift of her people.
	We claim the right to make our own laws,
simply because we can regulate our own affairs
better than any one else can regulate them for
us; and so we, on our own soil, for that simple
reason, must be legislatures for Utah. "That
was the great argument first asserted in this
country. One hundred years has established
the fact that self-government, with respect to
local affiars, is the true system of government
in this world."
	No trouble can arise but what our own legis-
lators can regulate, in all matters of home
affairs. So, we must have local self-govern-
ment. What citizen, among any other people,
of a free mind, would like to be dragged from
a home fireside into a distant state and be im-
mured in a prison for months, to await what?
An inquisition. In our boasted land of liberty
—"America—not Ireland,not England,no; but
the home of the brave and the land of the
free." You may hunt the wide world over, and
I defy any one to find better men and women
than have been taken from their homes and
friends, deprived of their usefulness in the
public good, and their wives and children de-
prived of their support, to gratify lawless in-
truders, forced on a happy and thriving com-
munity to fill the idler's coffers out of the
hard earned real estates of its own native born
citizens. Is not America indebted to the
Latter-day Saints for Utah's resources? Was
it not settled up by them under the most try-
ing circumstances? We need not go to Irish
history to find a persecuted people. No, not
beyond our own country's limits. Here has
been enacted the most unjust and stringent
measures against an innocent and unoffending
people. In plain contradiction to our coun-
try's laws and Constitution, which gives liberty
of religious belief and conscience, can an ac-
tive mind be eternally ladened with supersti-
tious fogyism? Why is it that America's
rulers are so far seeing as to sympathize with
Ireland and sending men away out to Utah
with a legislator's lash to thrash her own sub-
jects? "Well," say they, "Utah is a Territory,
and they have no rights in common with us."
Indiana is a state. Yes, and cannot Utah be-
come one? Are not her numbers sufficient? Are
fifteen thousand people not to be trusted with
the reins of government? Who, in Indiana,
would trust to any other state in legislation for
her schools, in the building up of her indus-
tries? So according to the majority in Utah.
When persecution ceases with us to exist, not
only the agricultural classes, but the the me-
chanics, the people of the cities and towns
must live, and Utah becomes clothed with the
right and power of self-government, these
matters will be cared for. That is a doctrine
so plainly expressed and so powerful in its ap-
plication to human interests, that it will never
stop; it will go on.
	For many years it has not been a contro-
verted question that the Latter-day Saints
have been dealt hardly by. It is widely known
that they have not had fair play. How is it
that American citizens were driven by a law-
less mob into these wild, unknown regions,
uninhabited by the white race? Thought unfer-
tile and barren—made by this people to blos-
som as the rose, redeemed by them from its
sterile condition by hard industry in the midst
of poverty, being deprived of their mother
land, the homes of their birth, and thrust out
beyond their land of freedom into a desert
waste. Have the Irish people been served
thus? No; they could stay at home, but we
could not. Missiles of war were used—pointed
at men, women and children, and they were
driven over the Mississippi River in the month
of February, to freeze and starve.
	And now our good, kind followers say,
'Give yourselves up; oh, do! that we may

[Column 3]

have no further trouble on your account, and
our deputy marshals can be paid and so can
we, and live on your exit." No, never! we are
not all fools.
	"Let us come back to the great question
which lies at the foundation of government,
the question of the rights of the people to
make their own laws, and that no other power
has a right to make laws for them. You re-
member, in the Declaration of Independence,
we asserted the right of men to govern them-
selves. That is the great fundamental idea of
America, and is now the one being applied, to
which we give our sympathy and support,
The right of man to govern himself and to
abolish laws that are inimical to his welfare is
the principle that was asserted at Bunker Hill,
and in glorious triumph it was proclaimed at
Yorktown." (Great applause.)
		MARY ANN MERRILL PRATT.
	Salt Lake City, Sep. 17th, 1885.


IT DON'T PAY.

	It don't pay to have one citizen in the coun-
ty jail because another citizen sells him
liquor.
	It don't pay to have one citizen in the luna-
tic asylum because another citizen sold him
liquor.
	It don't pay to have fifty workmen ragged,
to have one saloon keeper dressed in broad-
cloth and flush with money.
	It don't pay to have ten smart, active, intelli-
gent boys transformed into thieves to enable
one man to lead an easy life by selling them
liquor.
	It don't pay to give one man, for $15 a
quarter, license to sell liquor, and then spend
$5,000 on a trial on another man for buying
that liquor and committing murder under its
influences.—Christian Secretary.

	"Mrs. Marietta M. Bones is deputy clerk of
the district court of Webster, Dakota."
	"Kate Greenaway will offer the holiday sea-
son a new book, called 'The Marigold Gar-
den.'"
	"Miss Cleveland is a successful author. Her
book has already run through ten editions, and
has been translated into several languages."
	"Lady Dufferin has formed in India a Na-
tional Association for supplying 'female medi-
cal aid,' i.e., women doctors to attend the
wives of the natives in sickness."
	"Mlle. Sinay has been awarded by the jury
of the Paris Conservatoire a first prize for
violin-playing; Mlles. Gauthier, Dautin, and
Boutin a premier accessit, and Mlle. Magnier a
second accessit."
	"Mrs. Kingsley Harrison, a daughter of
Charles Kingsley, is the author of the novels
'Mrs. Lorimer' and 'Colonel Enderby's Wife,'
under the pen-name Lucas Malet. For a long
time her first book was supposed to be the
work of a man."
	Without a doubt, the mother's superior or
inferior intelligence is the most deeply im-
printed upon the child.—Horatio Yates, M. D.,
Professor Queen's University.
	"Colonel Higginson's 'History of the United
States,' most of which has appeared in Harp-
er's Magazine, is soon to be printed in book
form."
