HOUSEHOLD HINTS.

	Stain on cups and saucers may be removed by rubbing
	with ashes.

	If the oven is too hot when baking, place a small dish of
cold water in it.


<lry 'they="" ;are="" excellent="" -terpolish="" stoves="" tiiathiiye="" not="" been="" blatkendd="" for'some="" length="" of="" tlmp'.="" '="" -:="" :i="" ‘="" -ii.v="" i="" r.="" ti="" i.="" -="" a="" turqi#="" deltiatr—clan="" dnd.pare="" some,="" turnips',="" and.="" pt,="" them="" in.="" two-,="" ipngtjiwise;="" melt'godrsirni="" piece.="" ;of-="" butjerj="" andput="" it="" intar="" saucepan,="" with,="" tlturnips.jdnd="" a.-="" spopnful,="" ,11,0nr..="" cook.="" qn.="" a,="" brisk="" fire,;="" yflthoqt="" ieting="" the="" turnips="" turn,="" yellow;="" moisten="" with.milk="" or="" good="" stock,.="" i'iha="" p!p?h,of.powerugarjand,="" ty.tfipwhole="" simmei="" tj="" hq="" sauce="" nearly.bsorbed.ythen;dishnp,="" -a<="" div="">	When sponge cake becomes dry it is nice to cut it in
thin slices and toast.

	To remove mildew, soak in butter-milk and spread on
the grass in the sun.

	To brighten the inside of a coffee or tea pot, fill with
water, add a small piece of soap, and let it boil about forty
five minutes.

	Old newspapers will put the finishing touch to newly
cleansed silver, knives and forks and tinware better than
anything else. Rub them well and make perfectly dry.
They are excellent to polish stoves that have not been
blackened for some length of time.

	A Turnip Delicacy.—Clean and pare some turnips, and
cut them in two lengthwise; melt a good-sized piece of
butter, and put it into a saucepan with the turnips, and a
spoonful of flour. Cook on a brisk fire, without letting
the turnips turn yellow; moisten with milk or good stock,
add a pinch of powdered sugar, and let the whole simmer
till the sauce is nearly absorbed; then dish up.


OBITUARY.

	DIED, in the First Ward, Salt Lake City, Nov. 28th,
1885. Sister Elizabeth Lovett, born Oct. 21st, 1824. She
was a loving and indulgent mother, and a most affection-
ate wife to an invalid husband, whom she nursed with the
tenderest care for five long years, and survived him just
ten years. Of our departed sister it can truthfully be said,
she has come up through much tribulation and washed her
robes white in the blood of the Lamb. She was a teacher
in the Relief Society a consistent Latter-day Saint, and
died as she had lived, in full faith of the Gospel, and in
the hope of a glorious resurrection.
						M. F. STEEL, Prest.,
						ANNIE S. MUSSE, Sec


RESOLUTIONS OF RESPECT.

EDITOR EXPONENT.
	The following Resolutions of Respect for our beloved
sister, Esther H. Davis, who departed this life December
21st, 1885, in her thirty-eighth year, were unanimously
adopted and sustained by the Primary Association of Big
Cottonwood Ward:
	Whereas, Our esteemed sister was formerly President of
the Primary Association of Big Cottonwood Ward, where
she was greatly loved and respected by every member of
the association, and,
	Whereas, In the loss of this our much esteemed sister,
we have parted with a willing worker, at all times, and one
who was ever devoted to the cause of truth and righteous-
ness,
	Resolved, That we condole with the bereaved family and
relatives in their great loss, and while we mingle our tears
with theirs, we look forward with the hope of meeting
her again, in the morning of the first resurrection of the
just.
	Resolved, That a copy of these resolutions be sent to the
bereaved family, that a copy be placed upon the minutes
of the association, and that one be sent to the WOMAN'S
EXPONENT for publication.
				ELLEN SUTHERLAND, President,
				MARY QUIST,
				EMILY E. STEVENSON, Counselors,
				MARY E. SUTHERLAND, Secretary

The Rights of the Women of Zion, and the Rights of the Women of all Nations.
VOL. 14.		SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, FEBRUARY 1, 1886.	No. 17.


MIDNIGHT MUSINGS.

		WRITTEN FOR P.S.

The fire burns low upon the hearth,
	The household sleeps, no cares intrude,
Without hath darkness wrap'd the earth,
	Within I sit in solitude.

But not alone: dear voices come,
	Breathing soft cadence in my ear,
Which through the day, all wearisome.
	With rush of work, I could not hear.

Their whisperings soothe my tired soul,
	And all its worldly longings lull;
Then o'er my passive senses roll,
	Visions most fair and beautiful.

On scrolls of purest, matchless white,
	Earth's best endeavors far above,
Engraved, as in eternal light,
	I see dear names I know and love.

The poor, the meek, the faithful ones,
	Those bowed by sorrow and by care,
These are God's daughters and His sons—
	I see their names recorded there.

And names I have not known on earth,
	So humble are their stations here,
I see enshrined with gems of worth.
	And in bright glory they appear.

Brave men, who labor day by day,
	To fling oppression boldly down;
Who nobly dare God's laws t' obey.
	Though all the world in anger frown.

Brave women, too, and chaste and pure,
	Who vaunt not here their cherished claims.
But, for the truth's sake, all endure—
	Their names are there, bright, hallowed names.

First, though I saw it not before.
	Shines the blest name of Christ our King,
Who all our pangs and sorrows bore,
	And perfect grew through suffering.

And must I close my eyes, and lose
	This gleam of heav'n, this blessed ray?
Oh! sacred is the "Midnight Muse,"
	Fairer and sweeter oft than day.


[Column 1]

WILL THE LIGHT GO OUT?

	Probably no class of persons are watching
and waiting more anxiously the final result of
the present crusade against "Mormonism,"
than those who, for reasons best known to
themselves, have apostatized and left the
Church, and are now fighting against it. No
other class have the same interest in seeing
"Mormonism" swept away, or would feel more
truly thankful if it could be proved, what they
sincerely hope it to be, a fraud, and obliterated
from the face of the earth. The reason for
this "the wise will understand," but much of it
might be explained, even to the casual thinker,
by that feeling of hatred, almost fiendish in its
bitterness, so characteristic of the "Mormon"
apostate.
	True, there are some seceders who do not
exhibit it, but I do not regard them, except in
a limited sense of the term, as apostates, for
they either belong to a class who never had
any "Mormonism" to secede from, or to an-
other, more noble but unfortunate, who have
not in their hearts entirely forsaken the faith,
though they are counted outside the Gospel
fold.
	I am speaking more particularly of those

[Column 2]

"perturbed spirits" who, not content with fall-
ing away themselves, would fain drag everyone
else along with them, or failing in that, whelm
in ruin and disaster all who prefer to cling to
the old ship Zion, despite the example of the de-
sertion and treachery which they have set them.
	Strange, is it not, that such should be the
case? These persons are, for the most part,
men and women who have been heard to say,
perhaps a thousand times, that they knew this
religion to be the Gospel of Jesus Christ, and
have given evidence in many ways, most of
all by that infallible indication of faith and
sincerity, good works, that what they said they
meant, and were then in possession of a testi-
mony of its truth which they could not deny
and would not hear other gainsay. With re-
gard to the causes which have produced a sub-
sequent "change of heart," we have nothing
to say, nor of the peculiar predicament in
which such people find themselves, in repudiat-
ing with scorn what they before solemnly
avowed as a fact; attempting to trample in the
mire the jewel they once wore proudly
upon the breast, and "knowing" to be "all
wrong" what they are already on record for
as knowing to be right and proper in every
respect.
	Such are the ones, I repeat, who are the
most anxious to have it shown that the claims
of "Mormonism" to a divine origin are false;
and the proof of it is that they are straining
every nerve and effort in that direction. And
why this anxiety and unrest on their part? If
they know that "Mormonism" is not true, and
that they simply lied when they solemnly
testified that they knew it was true, what need
of all this nervous activity? Why this haggard
impatience, this feverish haste to have the so-
called "problem" solved by fire and sword,
and the fact of its falsity demonstrated in its
downfall, beyond the shadow of a doubt? Is it
a pure, unselfish, disinterested regard for the
welfare of humanity that inspires such inhu-
man propositions for the cure of an alleged
evil against which the battering rams of mob
violence, special legislation and polemical de-
bate have been thundering in vain for fifty
years? Or, is there another reason, supposed to
be carefully concealed, but nevertheless "gross
as a mountain, open, palpable," to all who see
aright, why the apostate from "Mormonism"
should desire, more than anyone else, to see the
system extirpated and all who cleave to it
swallowed up in the fierce maelstrom of popu-
lar fury?
	I take the ground, and am not afraid of its
being honey-combed, that no man who fights
against "Mormonism" after testifying in all
sincerity that he knows it to be the work of
God, feels positively certain that it is not. And
the bitterer his hatred, and the more active
his hostility to the pure and sacred principles
which he once practiced and advocated, the
more evident is it that in his heart of hearts
he fears and dreads, as only such souls can,
the possibility of its final triumph and his own
utter condemnation. It is the light which
dazzles the eyes and reveals our personal de-
fects, that we wish taken away. For the hon-
est man daylight has no terrors, but the evil-
doer, pursued by the torments of a guilty
conscience, would fain have the bright sun ex-
tinguished, the moon and stars blotted out and
the universe one eternal night, that he might
escape in the darkness and revel in his wrong
doing undisturbed

[Column 3]

	Those who are looking Zionward as the
winds and waves begin to rise and the storm-
clouds to lower overhead, and are asking
themselves the question, "Will her light go
out?" may take home to their souls a negative
response. The light will not go out. It was
the hand of God that kindled it, and not all
the floods of earth or the infernal regions can
drown it, or prevent it from "growing brighter
and brighter unto the perfect day." There is
no such thing in the programme of history as
the extinguishment of "Mormonism." That
programme was prepared in the heavens be-
fore the foundations of the earth were laid, and
though earth and heaven pass away, taking
with them to perdition all who forsake and
fight against God's truth, not one jot or tittle
of what He has forespoken and preordained
will fall to the ground unfulfilled.
	O. F. WHITNEY.


﻿THE SIGNS OF THE TIMES.

	The world appear blind to the signs of the
times, which to the Latter-day Saints, however,
are proving beyond a doubt that the great day
approaches when, as was long ago foretold, a
government will be set up which will never
end.
	The little stone that Daniel saw cut out of
the mountain is rolling swiftly, and will soon
fill the whole earth. The wicked rule at the
present time, and thousands mourn because of
these things, and the fearful judgments that
have commenced to be poured out upon the
nations. The desolation and pestilence that
will follow the terrible deeds that have been
and are being committed against the weak and
oppressed, they are illy prepared to meet. They
cannot see the wonderful things which are be-
ing accomplished before their eyes, and which
cannot be staid. Though they may scourge
the Latter-day Saints for a little season, it is
only a purifying process which is actually
necessary to separate the gold from the dross,
and we hope will leave none but the faithful
and true, who will render more strict obedience
to the requirements of the Gospel of Christ.
We have the consolation of knowing that the
community called "Mormons" have ever been
a strictly law-abiding people, yielding obedi-
ence to every law which promotes the interest
of morality and religious liberty. They have
been instructed to befriend and support every
constitutional law of the land from the time
that this Church was organized—fifty-six
years ago; also that those who will not abide in
the covenant, even unto death, are not worthy
to be called Saints, as the Lord has declared;
and we know that He has decreed in His heart
that He will prove His Saints in all things,
that they may be found worthy.
	To polygamy and the priesthood have been
attributed all the evils that have befallen the
"Mormon" Church. How fallacious this state-
ment is!  The early Saints know that the
principle of plural marriage was never intro-
duced, much less practiced in the Church, till
after we had passed through the most cruel
persecutions. We have repeatedly testified to
this fact, and we knew when they said that
polygamy was the only thing objectionable in
"Mormonism," that were we willing to lay
aside this doctrine there would soon be other
similar requirements made at our hands. Al-
ready are we witnessing the fulfilment, and the
war is now openly declared to be against our


[Column 1]
religion. The last infamous bill introduced
by Senator Edmunds, and now pending in
Congress, is not only to take away the Church
property, but to rob the women of the privilege
of voting—the poor, sorrowing, down-trodden
slaves of the priesthood (?) of a vested right,
conferred upon them for the purpose of placing
them in a position to free themselves from the
"Mormon" hierarchy, with the full ex-
pectation of our emimies that they
would take advantage of it and lend
their assistance to break up the "Mormon"
Church. But the Latter-day Saints are blessed
with a knowledge for themselves that God
reigns supreme. Long ago we were warned of
the events which are now transpiring in
America, our beloved land of liberty, a land
which the Lord declared to be choice above all
others, where the fulness of the Gospel of
Jesus Christ has come forth, His Church been
established, and the gathering of His people
commenced. This no power will be able to
stay; and those who are striving to rouse this
people to violence, hoping to uproot and crush
out "Mormonism" will be as sadly disappointed
as have been all their predecessors. Numerous
proposals have been made for our removal as a
body to some island of the sea—to Mexico or
South America. But having received no such
teachings, nor revelation to that effect, but to
the very opposite, and believing that when we
emigrate from these mountain regions, it will
be to the spot from whence we were driven, and
knowing that the set time is near for the ful-
filment of that prediction, we look to the Lord
to accomplish it in. His own way, the same as
He did the great move of Israel across the
barren deserts to these rocky fastnesses, which
He had reserved for them and the great multi-
tude who desired to come out of Babylon.
Here we expect to remain until the set time
comes for our return to take possession of
those homes of which we were robbed and then
driven out to perish in the wilderness. The
over-ruling hand is apparent in this, too—that
Fremont and his men had explored one or two
portions of this great basin, but finding the
soil dry,and parched, they considered it alto-
gether unfit for the habitations of men. We
have cause to rejoice when we reflect upon the
mighty work that has been accomplished in
this valley. "A little one shall become a
thousand and a small one a strong nation,"
said the Prophet Isaiah. Have not this and
many more of his predictions been fulfilled
since the early days of our pilgrimage, when
we could look forward only with the eye of
faith? Can such a people feel themselves slaves
to the priesthood, as has been charged, when
every faithful Saint in Zion may himself enjoy
a portion of the priesthood? They are bound
together by no heirarchy, but by a love which
surpasseth the understanding of man. Neither
are they low and degraded, for such are always
the quickest to rebel, while the truly wise will
yield obedience to proper authority, to those
whom they have willingly accepted as their
leaders. Those who have embraced this Gos-
pel are the freest people living. We are not
trusting to priests and to their prayer like the
sectarians who suppose they have nothing more
to do than hold on to what they have, nor to
that false authority which has been received
through the mother of harlots. Such things
may do for those who know not God, and have
not obeyed His Gospel, but this ignorance can
not exist among the Saints. Each one must
receive a testimony from on high, independent
of man's. No matter how well educated he may
be, or how high his authority, he can know
only for himself and not for another. With-
out this knowledge none will be able to stand,
for the time is here when all that can be shaken
will be shaken. It is not for only a few to the
exclusion of the many, but all may know it
for themselves, and by this means all will be

[Column 2]
intelligent and may become one in all things,
even as God and His Son Jesus are one. It is
a glorious theme to dwell upon—this fulfil-
ment of the prophetic words that were spoken
concerning the work that was to be accom-
plished in the latter days,and which is a marvel
and a wonder even in the eyes of those who
have taken part in bringing it to pass.
	While looking over the Evening and Morn-
ing Star, published in Independence, Mo., my
eyes fell upon the following, written June, 1833.
It is headed, "Israel will be Gathered," and
reads: "There is something beyond expression
that cheers the heart of the Saint while con-
templating such a happy day to come, when
everything will be turned to its proper use:
the Spirit of God upon all flesh, will cause all
to fill the place of its creation, as in the day
when all was named by Adam in the Garden
of Eden. The people of the Lord may re-
joice, for the time will soon come when they
shall plant vineyards and eat: for as the days
of a tree are the days of my people, and mine
elect shall long enjoy the work of their hands.
They shall not labor in vain, nor bring forth
in trouble, for they are the seed of the blessed
of the Lord, and their offspring with them."
	Should not such a people rejoice in the
midst of their tribulations, witnessing the ful-
filment of the words of the prophets? and
should not we and our descendants hail the
days which were foretold, and the many privi-
leges enjoyed through being connected with a
people whom the Gospel has made free?
Should we doubt the final triumph of "Mor-
monism?" No; I think not. Having been
forewarned of these troublous times, we ought
in this day to be able to meet the issue.
	While the emissaries of evil are working for
our destruction we can live our religion in
spite of them or any other power this side of
heaven. And Zion's watchmen should awake
and put on the whole armor of faith and stand
in defense of the precious truths which the Al-
mighty has committed unto us, and also pro-
claim them to the honest of every nation,
kindred, tongue and people. This is the mis-
sion of Latter-day Saints, and the Lord will
sustain those who stand in the defense of its
principles. We have proven that we desire
only the welfare of our fellow beings—offering
eternal life and salvation to those who are still
walking in the paths of darkness, desiring with
all our hearts to show them the better way.
We know how the evil one is blinding the eyes
of the majority of the world to their own inter-
ests. We honor those who are actuated by
principle and are possessed of the amount of
moral courage necessary to defend the rights of
their fellow creatures against their oppressors,
who are moved by the same spirit that pos-
sessed Cain when he rose up and slew his
brother Abel, whose offering the Lord had ac-
cepted, and gloried in the thing he had done,
thinking surely that every obstacle was now
removed, and that the flocks of his brother
would fall into his hands. But the doom of
disappointment which awaits those who are
seeking the downfall of "Mormonism" will be
equal to the murderer's, when, in the bitterness
of his soul, he cried,"My punishment is greater
than I can bear!"
					H. M. WHITNEY.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


EVOLUTION OF THE PEACH.

	The great ancestor of the peach was purely
a tropical plant that could not endure the
least touch of frost. The almond, from which
by man's aiding nature, the peach has been
produced, is so fragile that it can scarcely be
made to grow even in the warmest portions of
the United States; yet after many ages of
coaxing and gradual acclimatization, it was


transplanted from the hot suns and the warm
fields of Arabia to the frost-touched highlands
of Persia. But instead of the rich kernel's
being the tempting food which the tree offered
to the cultivator, as was the case with the
parent almond in Arabia, it was the husk
which man ate in Persia; but not the hard,
green, indigestible, woody rind of the almond
but the rich luscious peach.


NOTES AND NEWS.

	Mrs. Louise Bethune a practicing architect
of Chicago, was lately elected a member of
the American Institute of Architects.

	Sadie Bay, although only 16 years of age,
is the proprietor and editor of the Buena Vista
(Col.) Wasp; does the editorial work upon her
paper, sets all the type, conducts all of the
business of the office, and runs the press her-
self. it is a hand-press, and she has the re-
quisite muscle to perform the labor. Besides
doing all the work enumerated, Miss Bay at-
tends school every day.

	Miss Kate Kane was one of the two attor-
neys who defended the three Italians sen-
tenced in Chicago a few days ago to be hung
for strangling one of their countrymen.
Judge Hawes in passing sentence referred to
their counsel as having done everything in
their behalf that could be properly and legal-
ly done, and as serving their case as faithfully
as any attorney could.

	Miss Mamie Dickens, the eldest daughter
of Charles Dickens, has written a short bio-
graphy of her father, which will soon be pub-
lished by Cassell & Co. Miss Dickens has
condensed the story of her father's public life,
and has also shown him as he was at home,
with his family and at his work. She has giv-
en what will be regarded as the pleasantest
pen picture of the great English novelist.

	The following sums are said to have been
received by the respective authors for the
works named: "Romola," George Eliot, $50,-
000; "Waverly," Scott, $3,500; "Woodstock,"
Scott, $700; "Life of Napoleon," Scott, $90.-
000; "Armadale," Wilkie Collins, $25,000;
"Lallao Rookh," Thomas Moore, $14,000; "His-
tory of Rome," Goldsmith, $1,500; "History
of Greece," Goldsmith, $1,250; "History of Eng-
land," Goldsmith, $3,000; "Vicar of Wake-
field," Goldsmith, $300; "Decline and Fall of
the Roman Empire," Gibbon, $50,000; "Lives
of the Poets," Johnson,$1,500; "Rasselas," John
son, $500.


EXPERIENCE.—To some the experiences
they pass through are like beads upon a string,
and when the thread of life is cut, they simply
roll away out of sight and out of mind. A
new business here a new friendship there; here
a lucky accident, there a disastrous failure; here
a birth and rejoicing, there a death and mourn-
ing; and, as the mind recalls them, it finds no
special meaning attached to them and no con-
nection between them, except that of time or 
place. Another person comes out of each one
fuller, stronger, wiser than before. A serious
illness has led him to study the laws of his
physical mature, and taught him how to secure
health for himself, his family, and those whom
he may influence. A disaster in business is
sifted to the bottom, and new light is thrown on
future plans. Or a great happiness comes to
him, and he hastens to share it with others, or
learns through it how he may bless others. He
is accumulating experience, not simply to
count it over and recall its pleasure or its pain,
but to develop out of it all power and wisdom
by which his own life and the lives of all who
approach him may be improved.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


OH, THOU! WHO DRY'ST THE MOURN-
			ER'S TEARS.

Oh Thou! who dry'st the mourner's tear,
	How dark this world would be,
If, when deceived and wounded here,
	We could not fly to Thee!
The friends who in the sunshine live,
	When winter comes are flown;
And he who has but tears to give,
	Must weep those tears alone,
But thou wilt heal the broken heart,
	Which, like the plants that show
Their fragrance from the wounded part,
	Breathes sweetness out of woe.

When joy no longer soothes or cheers,
	And even the hope that th[-]ew
A moment's sparkle o'er our tears,
	Ay, dimm'd and vanish'd too,
Oh, who would bear life's stormy doom,
	Did not thy Wing of Love
Come brightly wafting through the gloom
	Our Peace-branch from above?
Then sorrow touch'd by Thee grows bright
	With more than rapture's ray;
As darkness shows us worlds of light
	We never saw by day
							MOORE.


A DEAD ROSE.

O Rose! who dares to name thee?
	No longer roseate now, nor soft, nor sweet;
	But pale, and hard, and dry, as stubble-wheat.—
Kept seven years in a drawer—thy titles shame thee.

The breeze that used to blow thee
	Between the hedge-row thorns, and take away
	An odor up the lane to last all day,—
If breathing now,—unsweetened would forego thee.

The sun that used to smite thee,
	And mix her glory in thy gorgeous urn,
	Till beam appeared to bloom, and flower to burn,—
If shining now, with not a hue would light thee.

The dew that used to wet thee,
	And, white first, grow incarmadined because
	It lay upon thee where the crimson was,
If dropping now, would darken where it met thee.

The fly that lit upon thee,
	To stretch the tendrils of its tiny feet
	Along the leaf's pure hedges after heat,—
If lighting now, would coldly over-run thee.

The bee that once did suck thee—
	And build they perfumed ambers up his hive,
	And swoon in thee for joy, till scarce alive,—
If passing now,—would blindly over look thee.

The heart doth recognize thee,
	Alone, alone! The heart doth smell thee sweet,
	Doth view thee fair, doth judge thee most complete—
Perceiving all these changes that disguise thee.

Yes, and the heart doth owe thee
	More love, dead rose, than to such roses bold
	As Julia wears at dances, smiling cold!—
Lie still upon this heart which breaks below thee.
			ELIZABETH BARRETT BROWNING.


[Column 1]

THOUGHTS ON THE TIMES.

	We are living in very eventful days. Inci-
dents are continually occurring that test people
and show that the "Gospel net" has indeed
gathered of every kind. We find that there
are good, bad and indifferent individuals in
our midst. All that is required is something
to bring them out to show their true character.
You can judge pretty correctly how much
faith people have by their expressions, for "out
of the abundance of the heart the mouth
speaketh." A person who has faith and confi-
dence in God that He will fulfill His promises
to His people, does not speak in a doubtful,
fearful manner about the outcome of this cru-

[Column 2]

sade that is being waged against the Latter-day
Saints, but is confident that all will be over-
ruled for the good of those who trust in Him
and remain faithful.
	Those who enjoy the spirit of the Gospel do
not ask such questions as these. "Why do not
the authorities of the Church come forth and
give themselves up and stand the consequences
like men? If plural marriage is of God why
does He not protect His people from their ene-
mies, and not suffer them to be cast into prison?"
These questions are sometimes asked by those
who profess to be Saints, but I say they are
only Saints in name, or they would understand
the reason, and would feel as all true Saints
feel, anxious that our leading men should re-
main concealed, and their prayers would as-
cend daily to God that He would keep them
from their enemies.
	In the days of the Prophet Joseph Smith, he
made arrangements to escape from his enemies,
but some of the Saints of little faith accused
him of cowardice, and he returned, knowing
what the end would be—that his life would be
taken. Do we wish to see a similar tragedy?
There are those who are thirsting for the blood
of our prophets to-day, who would repeat the
crime committed at Carthage Jail, if they had
the chance. The blood of our beloved Presi-
dent was shed on that dreadful occasion, but
he has been preserved to bless this people and
fill the place he now occupies. He is a living
monument of God's power and mercy, and
may God grant that he may continue to be for
many years.
	Who thinks it reasonable to ask, "Why was
Jesus Christ allowed to be crucified, if He was
the Son of God?" Yet some people ask why the
Lord does not prevent His servants from going
to prison, if He commanded them to practice
plural marriage? It is just as necessary for
God's chosen people to-day to be tried, perse-
cuted and imprisoned for the Gospel's sake, as
it was for Christ to be crucified, and all
Christains acknowledge that that was essential
for our salvation. Why? To test us, to see if
we are willing to suffer for the Gospel's sake.
If we are not willing, we are not worthy to be
called Saints. Jesus said, "He that is not
willing to lay down his life for my sake is not
worthy of me." And again He said, "Blessed
are ye when men shall revile and persecute
you, and say all manner of evil of you
falsely for my name' sake; rejoice and be
exceeding glad, for so persecuted they the
prophets who were before you."
	The persecution that we receive is an evi-
dence and testimony to us that we are the peo-
ple of God whom He has chosen out of the
world, therefore we are hated and persecuted
for our religion as no other people are perse-
cuted; but if we would "be like the rest of us"
they would love us and let us alone. We do
not want their love nor anything they offer us.
—no, not even the liberty they offer us. The
liberty that the courts offer has to be bought
with too great a price for Latter-day Saints to
think of purchasing. They cannot afford to
barter away their exaltation and endless lives,
and become slaves by accepting the liberty
that the courts offer; and a man who does so to
escape a few months' imprisonment forfeits his
wives and his children and his exaltation. Bet-
ter accept imprisonment for life with honor,
and gain endless lives, that accept liberty and
dishonor. When I read about the brethren
who have made promises to the court, I wonder
how their wives feel to be cast off and deserted
by those they loved and trusted, and how their
children feel. I brought it home to myself,
and asked myself how I would feel under the
circumstances. It made me feel sick at heart;
it was terrible to think of, and I was glad to 
think of something more pleasant. I say,
"God bless and comfort my sisters who have to
endure this trial," and let me encourage you

[Column 3]

to do your duty bravely, keep your covenants
and live a pure life, and our Father will re-
ward you by giving you a noble husband, who
will not be afraid to serve Him, and will be
worthy of the affection of pure faithful women.
The man who puts away his plural wives is
more to be pitied than the wives (if they re-
main faithful), for by his cowardly act he will
bring upon himself the condemnation of a just
God, who hates and has forbidden the putting
away of wives.
	If a man breaks his covenants and forfeits
his plural wives, what about his first wife?
Has he not also forfeited her, and is she justi-
fied in the sight of God in living with him?
He made the same covenants with his other
wives as he did with her, and is under the
same obligations to them as he is to her. I feel
proud of my sisters who went to prison rather
than betray their trust, and think there are
many more who would do the same, if they
were tried the same. Many of the brethren
have shown their integrity by going to prison,
and have gained the admiration and love of all
true Saints, who remember them daily in their
prayers. I should think the wives of those
noble men would love and honor them more
than ever, and would strive to be faithful and
true to them, that they might be worthy to be
their companions for time and eternity. But
if some of their wives are unfaithful, the Lord
will give them others who are worthy; and will
reward them for all they have suffered.
	Let us, my sisters, be patient; the redemption
of Zion draweth night. Let us not murmur or
complain at the providences of God, but be
thankful that He has counted us worthy to
live upon the earth at this time, and given us
the privilege of assisting to build up His king-
dom and establish righteousness upon the earth,
thereby showing our faith by our works. Let
us try to be humble, and say, "Father, not my
will, but Thine be done," and then strive to do
it with all our might, and all will be well with
us. When Jehovah has accomplished His pur-
poses in this crusade it will cease; the prison
doors will be opened; the exiles will return;
families will be reunited; righteous men will
sit in the judgment seat, and Zion will arise
and put on her beautiful garment, and the
glory of the Lord will rest upon her. That
this day may speedily come, is the daily prayer
of	A PLURAL WIFE


MISCELLANEOUS.

	A HAPPY HOME.—"Six things," says Hamil-
ton, "are requisite to create a happy home.
Integrity must be the architect, and tenderness
the upholsterer. It must be warmed by affec-
tion, and lighted with cheerfulness, and indus-
try must be the ventilator, renewing the
atmosphere, and bringing in fresh salubrity
day by day; while over all, as a protecting
glory and canopy, nothing will suffice except
the blessing of God."

	SIMPLICITY OF EXPENDITURE.—Simplicity
of expenditure is important to peace and
happiness. By this is not meant mere retrench-
ment, but that wise and independent manner
of spending that chooses comfort, health, edu-
cation, and real refinement, and refuses vanity,
self-indulgence, and display. The spending of
money always involves choice and usually some
sacrifice. One thing must be given up for the
sake of another, and the art of selection is one
on which much of life's happiness depends.
Emerson well says, "Spend for your expense,
and retrench the expense that is not yours.
We are sympathetic, and, like children, we
want everything we see. But it is a large
stride to independence when a man, in the dis-
covery of his proper talent, has sunk the neces-
sity for false expenses."
