SALT LAKE CITY,	FEBRUARY 15, 1886.


[Column 1]

ITEMS FROM WASHINGTON.

	The National Capital is not so gay this winter,
at least so far on, as it has generally been of late
years; it is said that the Senators and Representa-
tives are not men of so much means and wealth as
those of the last Congress. But still there is
plenty of display in dress, jewelry and all that
goes to make up the lady of fashion, and balls,
parties, clubs and calls are numerous as ever, and
it would seem even more so. The extravagance
in dress is something from which one whose
thoughts tend in a higher direction must turn
away with a sort of disgust. It almost absorbs
the life of women of society, and there is no doubt
but that many deaths have resulted from the ridicu-
lous fashions and customs of this public society.
It is quite time women turned their attention to
something loftier and better than this dress-parade,
this constant drain upon the purse, and the foolish
and exacting demands of Madame Grundy.
There is, one would suppose, and indeed there
must be, something very fascinating about this
continuous whirl of excitement, that makes one
forget the duty of self-preservation and carries too
many of its votaries to an early and untimely
grave, making them victim's to its cruel demands.
One cannot help exclaiming, "To what will all
this tend in the history of the race of men and
women, and what will the offspring of the next
generation be, if indeed there be a generation
born at all of these lovers of pleasure, these devo-
tees at the shrine of fashion?" The habits of men
are perhaps more deteriorating in their tendencies
than those of women, though differing materially
in the main; tobacco smoking seems to be the
great evil that is most apparent, though there are
many vices that are said to be increasing to an
alarming extent, and those who look at these mat-
ters seriously are greatly exercised over the fearful
statistics of crime, and the secret evils that creep
into modern society and sap the very foundations
of life, as it were, unawares. But from sins such
as these may Zion long be free, and her sons and
daughters grow up simple in their habits and
moderate in their tastes, and if unsophisticated,
pure and chaste in their lives, that the race may
grow strong morally and physically, as well as
spiritually, and that the cultivation of the heart
and the higher attributes of one's nature may sur-
pass the love of dress and amusements, and that
they may never despise the mission of motherhood,
and prefer dogs and pets of various kinds to the
children given them of God, possessing immortal
souls.
	We had no intention of moralizing, but it is a
sickening sight to see women calling themselves
ma and grandma to puppies, and saying, "Your
papa" so and so, to dogs, and see the homes
desolate of the voices of little children.
	Washington is a grand old city, and one never
tires of admiring its grandeur and magnificence,
its elegant public buildings, its beautiful parks and
avenues and squares, its handsome and stupen-


[Column 2]

dous monuments, and its many statues to great
and illustrious men—all are interesting to the
general observer, and specially so to the native
born American, who delights in the glory of
his country and her many noted places of in-
terest.
	The Capitol Building itself is really the centre
of attraction to the majority; thither in crowds day
after day, the people congregate to see and hear
the great men of the nation, who assemble daily
to perform the duties of the Congress to which
they have been elected by their constituents in the
several States and Territories of the Union.
	The Capitol is acknowledged to be the finest
building in this country, and some people affirm
in all the world, but as we have not been all over
the world, we cannot answer for that assertion,
but can say truthfully it is the finest we have
seen.
	It stands on Capitol Hill, as though nature her-
self had prepared the very spot purposely for this
imposing national edifice. To give a full descrip-
tion of the building would take up too much space,
and there are other things more important to tell,
but we will say it consists of a main building, with
two extentions, one at either side, in one of which
the Senate meets, in the other the House of Re-
presentatives. In the main building is the hall
used by the Supreme Court of the United States,
what was formerly the Senate Chamber, also the
National Gallery of Statues. There are many
objects of interest, about the Capitol, and one
might go there day after day and be agreeably
entertained with pictures, statuary and the like,
but it is not always convenient or, for many rea-
sons, expedient. One sees a great many fine
looking people in and around the rooms of the
Capitol, and occasionally one hears fine oratory,
but that does not usually come every day.
	The weather has been very cold in Washington
this winter, and there has been some good sleigh-
ing and fine coasting, and the people have enjoyed
it, judging from appearances.
	No doubt our readers would like to hear about
the new President and the Lady of the White
House—his sister. With the most of the people
they do not seem to be very great favorites,neither
with Democrats, his own party, much more than
with Republicans. In New York and Boston, and
indeed in country towns in New England, he is
very freely discussed, and his administration most
severely, and, one might almost say, savagely
critciized. At a concert given at All Soul's Church,
by blind children who had been trained in Eng-
land, President Cleveland was present, and as he
sat on the next seat to where the writer was sit-
ting, on a chair in the aisle, there was a very good
opportunity to see him. He looks exactly like his
picture, except he seems to be more fleshy, and is
bald at the back of his head. One would never
judge he was the President from looking
at him, though he looks quite as Presidential as
many of his predecessors in office. It is quite
usual to imagine that people must really look like
the ideal one has in mind, but they very rarely do.
However, he does look as if he could fill the
President's chair, even if it is so very large indeed.
His sister is small compared to her brother, she is
rather thin, looks very intellectual, and is sweet
mannered. She gets the credit of being the
power behind the throne, and is spoken of as
strong-minded, and having great influence with
her brother. A very funny story goes the rounds
here, which will give the idea of what is imagined
concerning her, by a large class who, no doubt,
envy her the position she holds here, and are not
half so amiable or approachable as she is. A
Frenchman said, in a satirical and humorous way,
"There was Catharine of Russia, and Elizabeth
of England, and now we have Elizabeth of
America."
	Senator Edmunds is still hammering away at
the Mormons, and public sentiment is at the

[Column 3]

present time pretty well educated in its opposition
to Mormonism. People advise us to take mea-
sures to educate public sentiment on the Mormon
side of the question, but how is that to be done,
when they will only hear one side of the story?
There are some fair-minded people who would
listen, would read, if they had the time, but they
are mostly the busy people, who have so much
business that they have not time for religion. A
few men here and there, and one or two women
see through the schemes of the Federal officials in
Utah, and declare that it is not polygamy they are
fighting, it is a combined effort to rob the people
of their rights; others think Edmunds means well,
but is too zealous, but he does it for the best, and
is an upright man, and would not encroach upon
the rights of others, "but he has got off wrong"
on this "Mormon question." There are those
who fear very much the provision of the bill in re-
gard to the confiscation of Church property—and
those are Church people. And again, the advo-
cates of suffrage for women are indignant that
Senator Edmunds should, want to take the suffrage
away from women and not from men, and
they look upon it as another great injustice to
women, which is, of course, plain to be seen, and
these strong suffragists have the names of the
Senators who voted for that Bill "down on their
lists," and as sure as the world, Edmunds is
down, the ringleader of this gross injustice;
"they've got him on the list," rest assured. But,
then, its only Mormon women, and a few others,
he thinks, no doubt, and that is all right, Mor-
mons have no rights any way that ought to be
respected; that’s exactly what he must think, or
he could never have framed such a bill as this last
one, for his former bill, passed in 1882, covers all
the grouud that the United States at large, the
sixty milllions of people, they talk so much about,
are hankering for, the breaking up of homes that
have been made in plural marriage. This is what
the press and pulpit have howled over, "we must
put down polygamy, and make these Mormons
conform to our views," and then they are just like
other people, but when they've got a law passed
that is so severe that every man against whom
any proof can be obtained is convicted, no matter
how flimsy the testimony, then they are not satis-
fied, but cry out for more, until they may be like
the boy who wanted an apple, and when he got it
he wanted another, and still another, until he
could no longer hold them, and they all rolled
away, and he lost even the first. Senator Ed-
munds is too greedy on the Mormon question, and
has plainly shown to all fair-minded people that
polygamy was only a drop in the bucket, instead
of the great big fish he intends to catch; but if the
great big fish he is after don't swallow him, as the
whale did Jonah, he may think himself pretty
lucky. It is thought, generally, that he is working
for the next Presidental election. "God save the
mark!" The country is badly enough off now,
without such a narrow-minded bigot as he is to
occupy the chair of State. If the sixty millons
of people think the "Mormon" question the one
most necessary to have settled, and the Edmunds
bills, one after another, don't succeed in getting
rid of the Mormons for them, why then, perhaps,
they will think if he was President he could do it;
but Mormons are not so easily bilked, and Con-
gress and the President have some other matters
of a serious nature to engross their attention, and
although Edmunds may be the ablest man in the
Senate, there is a very poor prospect of his being
elected, or even nominated, and some other great
big greedy fish, who is looking after big fish, in-
stead of such small fry as "Mormons," will prob-
ably run away with the coveted prize, and leave
him and his "Mormon" bills to go down stream.
He has, however, probably with other influences
at work, succeeded in arousing a great deal of
animosity against the "Mormons," and prejudice
is very much stronger now than seven years ago,


but it is always the darkest just before day, and to
the true Latter-day Saint there is courage that the
day dawn is near, and though storm clouds may
threaten, and even break over their heads, they
know who can at any moment say to the winds
and waves, "Be still!" and they will obey, and
that there is One above who controls the destinies
of nations and holds them in His hand, and in the
darkest time of deepest trial, the Saints have seen
light spring suddenly forth from the midst of dark-
ness, and help has come when most needed, even
at the last moment, as it were, and while the
Saints are true and faithful and keep the coven-
ants they have made with God and with each
other there is no need of fear and trembling.
	There are so many things to write about, one
scarcely knows where to begin, and, indeed, it is
difficult to decide which matter would be most in-
teresting, but some day we must tell of our visit to
Plymouth and Pilgrim Hall, to Haverhill, where
we met a dear old schoolmate, and felt as though
we had seen a vision—about our Boston cousins,
and so on, the visit to Oak Knoll, one of the
homes of Whittier, the poet, to the grand old
theological town, Andover, where we met Eliza-
beth Stuart Phelps, and to the most interesting
visit of all these—in Whittier's study, at his other
home, in Amesbury, where so many of his beauti-
ful poems were written, of the dinner there, and
the hucleberry pie—not "cold huckleberry pud-
ding;" then you must hear, too, of the very queer,
not to say weird old houses we wandered through,
where, when a child, our feet have strayed, and
lastly, the White House and Capitol, where, by
the by, we ascended to the top of the Dome; and
very soon we expect to leave Washington for
other interesting points, among which may be
mentioned Kirtland and Nauvoo.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]

OUR CHILDREN.

	This theme, so exhaustless and so important,
although so well worn, is always interesting to
the Latter-day Saints; therefore, no apology is
deemed necessary for its introduction here; in
fact, every issue of the WOMAN'S EXPONENT
should contain at least one page devoted to this
one subject.
	Many mothers, who have had long experience
in the rearing of children, are capable of giving
valuable hints to others who have not been in the
work so long, and to whom such hints would be of
great worth. But, alas! the hard-working, careful
mother is rarely the one to find time for sitting
down and writing bits of her experience for the
benefit of others. The result is, we have too
little of such matter in our papers, and too much
that is of far less value.
	From a private letter, written by a lady whose
residence is at Ryde, on the Isle of Wight, the
following extracts are taken by permission:
	"I think those persons are much to be con-
gratulated who are prompt in their habits, and to
whom it is neither effort nor denial to do their
work, and carry out their good purposes with
alacrity. I have heard it said, 'If you want a
thing done, give it to the man who has most to do,'
and I firmly believe it admits of sufficiently sim-
ple explanation, from the force of this most de-
sirable habit. And partly, perhaps, from natural
temperament, it is actually less trouble to such,
even though pressed for time, than to the individu-
al with ample leisure who must needs wait for the
breeze to fill the sails, or, in other words, to be in
the right humor, before making these intentions
take form and become accomplished facts. No
doubt many a good purpose is hopelessly lost in
the horribe quicksand of procrastination, which
would never have been sacrificed deliberately.
	If I had the management of the young, such
points as these are what I should consider of im-
portance, leaving mere book learning to a later

[Column 2]

date, as I firmly believe, often to the gain of the
young learner:
	Kindness and consideration to brothers and
sisters and companions, and to their inferiors in
position, of course, not omitting ready obedience
to parents, teachers and those above them; truth-
fulness, care in making a promise, and great care
in keeping the same, assuming, of course, that
the promise is a right one, and the avoidance of
the procrastination to which I have been just al-
luding; these, and kindred virtues, are what I
should lay every stress on, as laying a worthy and
attractive foundation for development in later
years, nor should I fear for the result.
	That all children, as a rule, are selfwilled, goes
without saying; also, that there is a wide differ-
ence in their dispositions, some seeming to be
born with much better dispositions than others
(Lock's theory, notwithstanding), but when we
see a child given to selfishness, and delighting in
tyranny, or teasing, as the case may be, if un-
checked, what can be expected but that these
faults will become vices in mature age? And it is
a poor set-off against it in such children, that
they are, perhaps, very talented, and in after life
may become great ornaments in society, and that
they can be very agreeable when they like, and
might be of much use if they only chose.
	Talent can be turned to evil as well as good ac-
count, as probably most persons could find sad
examples of, where, it is to be feared, this leading
aim in education, the developing of what is good,
with the repressing of evil tendencies, has been
overlooked or left in abeyance.
	Happily, we are none of us wholly dependent
upon oral tuition or human instructors. We have
all the teaching and training of experience, a safe
teacher in all cases."
	Will not the points tonched upon in the fore-
going be worthy of consideration by many parents
to whose notice they may be brought through their
insertion in the EXPONENT? And will not some
of our gifted and truely wise mothers in Israel,
exert themselves to assist in filling a column, such
as has been mentioned, to aid in the training and
teaching of our children?
	LULA.


[Column 1]

THOUGHTS ON THE TIMES.

	These times of persecution and trouble for
the Latter-day Saints, though aggravating in
their form and hard to bear are neverthe-
less fraught with blessing for them that
are manifest in many ways. There is at the
present a spirit of unity and independence
existing among them to a degree not witnessed
before for many years.
	The injustice and abuse heaped upon the
people are having a good effect upon the minds
of the youths of our Church; many who have
hitherto apparently taken very little interest
in the teachings of the gospel are beginning to
arouse and "buckle on the armor," a spirit
of action begins to burn within them which
without the stimulus that persecution gives
would have lain dormant. And this feeling of
despotism and cruelty which is steadily increas-
ing toward us is not to be regretted when we
realize the purifying effect it has upon us and
especially upon our children who have been
conceived, born and reared under a spirit of
perfect love and confidence. Then, inheriting
as they do this sweet spirit of confiding love
and trust, how essential to their welfare it is
that the emissaries of Satan who have sought
us out and established themselves among us
should be made to throw off the mask and
show themselves in their true character.
	It is pleasing to note how prompt the Y. M.
M. I. A. members, true sons of Abraham, were,
to fold the cloak of independence and pride
about them as soon as our enemies, the wor-

[Column 2]

shippers of Baal, began to uncover their
faces before them.
	How pleasant the situation when all the Lat-
ter-day Saints exhibit in their lives the same
spirit that did that ancient patriarch when
the idolatrous king bent humbly before him
offering presents, and, although the king was
much indebted to him for favors received, yet
he answered that he would not receive from
his hands even the value of a shoe lace. If
we are the children of Abraham we will do
the works of Abraham and pattern after his
ways, and we will not regret any position we
are placed in that will bring us to his stand-
ard of righteousness and nearer the Kingdom
of heaven. No Latter-day Saint who has the
fire of the Gospel burning in his veins can
fail to see that the power of God is in all these
things, and that His arm is stretched out to-
wards us and they cannot help rejoicing under
all the trials we endure. That spirit of sociabili-
ty and freedom that sprung up a few years
ago between our people and the outsiders
was not savoring of good for the Latter-day
Saints, and much care and anxiety
was imposed upon the faithful parents and the
authorities of the Church in consequence of
the tendency of many of our young people to
intermingle and marry with those not of our
faith, and it is well that there is beginning to
be a line drawn and that a separation be made
between the righteous and the ungodly. At
every period of time when God has had a peo-
ple of His choice on the earth the command has
been to not mix with those of an alien creed.
This subject has been frequently and very
plainly spoken upon by the editor of the
Juvenile in editorials and "Topics of the Times"
in that magazine and which no mother or
daughter in Israel should fail to read. He
makes mention of several instances in which
sorrow and distress has been the result when
this law given to Latter-day Israel has been
broken, and to those given by him another
might also be added, that of a young girl of
Salt Lake City, sixteen years old, and a mem-
ber of a highly respectable family in the
Church who married an outsider, who had
lived for many years in the Territory a good
enough man she thought. About a year ago
he came south on his way to Arizona, carrying
with him warrants for the arrest of some of
the leaders of the church whom he expected
to find there, he having been deputized
by Marshal Ireland to hunt these men
down; he took with him his wife, the young
girl mentioned, and a private letter written
by her to a friend a few months ago states that
she was then living in Arizona a few miles
from Phoenix alone and deserted. The noble
defender (?) of woman's rights and persecutor
of the "grey haired abusers of the law" (as he
termed our venerated exiled leaders) had left
her and gone, she knew not where; her watch,
jewelry and all her surplus clothing she had
sold, she said, to buy food for herself and child,
and, she adds, "I know no one in this whole coun-
try and there is nothing left for me to do but
starve." In reference to the death of one of
her friends, she says: "The news of her death
was a shock to me, but she is better off than
I am; I would willingly change places with
her, my greatest sorrow is that I cannot die."
This is a deplorable picture, but the fruits of
the bitter lessons we learn are sweet. And the
sooner the women of Zion learn to follow
strictly the voice of Him who has called them,
the sooner they will be saved from the dread-
ful and damning sins which the men of the
world would heap upon them. They are
learning from the cruelty and injustice im-
posed upon them by the leaders of the nation
and by those who have been foremost in the out-
cry for some law to be enacted to free the Mor-
mon women from their serfdom, that outside of
the fold into which God has called them they
have no friends. They have learned that they


cannot depend upon the governors and judges
of this nation to insure to them their personal
rights. They have seen the liberty, happiness
and all the rights of a wife laid at the feet of
the judge and instead of their being cherished
and protected by him, saw them spurned, cast
aside and trampled upon, while he empowers
the man to crush her, cast her and her lawful-
ly begotten children off and brand them with
shame. I say it is well for the women of Zion
to learn this, that aside from the noble few who
are brave enough to stand forth in the face of
the opposing nation and die if necessary for the
principles of salvation, there are none who
will defend their rights nor protect their virtue.
This purifying flame by the hand of our Father
cannot fail to scathe with grief the hearts of
some, those whose misfortune it has been to be-
come united with those who are wanting in
faith in the gospel and in integrity of heart
and purpose, and are unable to stand before
their oppressors without quailing. With such
wives and mothers our sympathy is deep.
Those are the real sufferers. To see husbands
and sons immured in prisons or seeking refuge
in the mountains like wild beasts is a grievous
thing, but it is much more grievous to see them
crouch with trembling knees, and supplicating
mercy before an infamous judge whose mis-
sion it is to apply the test to prove them. But
if we have husbands and sons who cannot bear
this test and are not willing to bear all for the
truth's sake let us remember that " 'Tis better
that one of the members should perish than
that the whole body should be cast into
hell," and so still kiss the rod that chastens.
'Tis a wise Father who is giving us these se-
vere but soul strengthening lessons; and that
He is on the side of the women of Zion, and
that he is pleased with the work they have
done before Him cannot be doubted by any
except those of little or no faith. He has
proven to them that He has given them true
friends and protectors among the men of the
earth, and that His Priesthood dwells with
them, a fact that does not exist among any
other class of women on the earth. The
prisons of Utah, Arizona, Idaho, and that at
Detroit can answer the question as to what
some of these are. This is a dark hour for
this nation, and the names of those men,
"beacon lights" they have justly been called,
will shine forth in the future history of woman
with a brilliant lustre, and they will live in
the hearts of womankind and be honored
for the noble manner in which they have given
up liberty in defense of that principle which
the women of all nations will yet know was
instituted by God for the salvation of their
race. So I say there is nothing in the present
crusade against us to make even the most
faint hearted woman falter. It is for her to
stand still and see the salvation of Zion, in the
work of which she bears a most honorable
part.		MAC.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


R. S., Y. L. M. I. A. & P. A. REPORTS.

	MOAB.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	Thinking that a brief report from the Relief
Society and Primary Association of this ward
would be of interest to your many readers, I
submit the following:
	On the 7th of November last a meeting was
called by our esteemed Bishop, R. H. Stewart,
for the purpose of re-organizing the Relief So-
ciety and Primary Association of this ward,
which was made necessary by the death of the
President of the former, Sister China Lutz, and
the removal from the ward of the President of
the latter. The following organization was
effected for the Relief Society: Sarah M.
Burnham, President; Priscilla Warner and
Sarah J. Stewart, Counselors; Amanda E.


Taylor, Secretary; Julia A. Pierce, Treasurer.
For the Primary Association, Anna McBride,
President; Clara M. Burnham and Rose Lutz,
Counselors; Lizzie Roberson, Secretary; Ros-
anna Stewart, Treasurer. The officers were
then set apart by the Bishopric.
	It seems the determination of the officers
and members to press on in the cause of truth
and assist in bearing off the kingdom of God.
Although bemeaned and persecuted for the
Gospel's sake, we know in whom we trust, and
that He will wreak vengeance on those who
fight against Zion, and that we will get our re-
ward by yielding obedience to the requirements
of the Gospel.
	Praying for the success of the EXPONENT,
we subscribe ourselves,
		Your sisters,
	SARAH M. BURNHAM, Prest.,
	AMANDA E. TAYLOR, Sec.


[Column 1]

CORRESPONDENCE.

	BLOOMINGTON, Feb. 8th, 1886.
EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:
	Thinking you would like to hear a word
from this part of the country, I thought I
would write a few lines.
	On the 3rd of February Bishop Hulme and
the President of the Relief Society got up our
customary old folk's party, when all who had
attained the age of fifty and upwards, with a
few invited guests, were present at a dinner
given in the schoolhouse. There were between
ninety-five and a hundred persons seated at a
bounteous repast. After the the tables were
cleared, and singing and prayer, a programme
was gone through, consisting of songs, speeches,
recitations and dancing, all of which was much
enjoyed by all present. The two oldest gentle-
men and the two oldest ladies were each the
recipients of a small present. Prest. James
H. Hart and Prest. Julia P. Lindsay of R. S.
gave us interesting and valuable instructions,
as did a number of brethren and sisters. Since
our last gathering of this kind, we have lost
two of our aged sisters by death, but they were
faithful to the end.
	Our winter has been unusually mild for this
northern country. We occasionally receive a
visit from the marshal or his deputies, but not-
withstanding this we enjoy a good spirit, and
feel that all is well with us so long as we are
true to our covenants and our God.
	Wishing success to your paper, I remain,
		Your sister in the Gospel,
			E. C. PIGGOTT, Sec.

	WEST JORDAN, Jan. 17th 1886.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	This is my first attempt to write to your
valuable paper, so I hope you will please ex-
cuse my feeble efforts, as I feel I would like
to pen a few items of my experience.
	A few years ago I knew a noble young
woman, who had been taught from her infancy
to love truth and virtue and the principles of
the Gospel of Jesus Christ. Her affections were
placed upon an honest, good and intelligent
man. She entered into the marriage relation
by making solemn vows and covenants that
were for all time and eternity. In the course
of time she was blessed with offspring and
among them a pair of twin baby girls. They
were very delicate; but by the careful and
affectionate care of the loving mother and
the faith and earnest prayers of a fond father
the Lord stayed the hand of the destroyer and
they were snatched, as it were, repeatedly
from the jaws of death.
	As time rolled on they could run around the
mother's knee with their prattling sweet voices
raised in musical tones singing "Sweet Birds,
Sweet Birds". On the fourteenth of January

[Column 2]

they would be two years old, so the fond mother
thought she would invite a few of her rela-
tives and friends and celebrate the day by
returning thanks to God our Heavenly Father
that He had spared their lives up to the pres-
ent time, hoping to have a pleasant time and a
sociable visit. On the morning of the thir-
teenth, just as they had risen from their bended
knees in offering up their thanks to God for
sparing their lives through another night, and
asking for His blessings through another day,
there came a knock at the door, and who
should it be but a deputy marshal, who soon
spread confusion and sorrow in that happy
family. The next morning that sorrow-
stricken mother was compelled to leave her
twin babes and two other dear children and
take her infant a month old and travel twelve
miles to Salt Lake City exposed to the cold
inclement weather, there to appear before
judge and jury. Her feelings may be imagined
when she reflected that her truthful testimony
was likely to tear that dear husband and af-
fectionate father from his dear wives and little
innocent children and place him inside prison
walls, there to mingle with murderers, thieves
and other wicked men. Next morning, feeble
and weak after the exposure and excitement
of the day before, she was seated in her easy
chair with her baby in her arms, when there
again came two deputy marshals wanting some
person they supposed to be there, telling her
their business and saying that they were
sorry, but if she did not tell the truth and the
whereabouts of the person they wanted they
would be compelled to take her to the city and
perhaps it would cause her imprisonment.
She said she did not know where the person
was any more than they did. After searching
the house and not finding the object of their
inquiry and asking her many questions which
she answered to the best of her ability, they
finally said they believed she told the truth.
They could not look that saintly woman in
the face and say otherwise. Well, such trials
the Saints of God are subjected to to-day.
What will come next? Well may the cry go
up, "oh Lord how long, how long, must thy
people endure these things for conscience sake."
	Praying the Lord to give His children
strength sufficient for their day, I am,
		Yours etc.,
			M. B.


THE WOMAN OF INFLUENCE.

	Mr. Worth, the eminent Paris dressmaker,
telegraphs to the Boston Sunday Herald that
the great and pressing need of the age is a
Woman of Influence, somewhere or other, to
set the fashions. In default of this, he has,
after exhausting his genius upon a new dress,
to use various indirect devices to bring it into
vogue. When one thinks what a beautiful
work of art a lady's dress may be, when wealth
and Worth have done their best for it, and
what an apalling product mere wealth, without
taste, can develop under that name, one may
well give a sigh of sympathy to this man of
genius who can find no woman quite worthy of
his scissors. Yet the truth is that the Woman
of Influence is demanded not alone to wear
clothes, but to modify and control all the
habits of society. A person of power, of in-
dividuality, of resouoces, of charm, is needed
in every place where a woman stands, and is
not to be had in answer to an advertisement.
"What we want," said a certain school com-
mittee-man, after a long debate in committee
about the best way to secure a competent fe-
male assistant in the high school—"what we
want, gentlemen, is a splendid woman." This
was at once accepted by all as the complete
formula for the situation; it was the later task
of actually hunting up this priceless creature
and securing for her eight hundred dollars a
year that proved formidable.


[Column 1]

	In these days one is certainly impressed with
the prominence of literature as a sphere for the
Woman of Influence. When we think of the
thousands of high schools and academies
throughout the land in which, next gradua-
tion-day, some maiden in white will read an
essay on "The Genius of George Eliot," we
may well say with Rufus Choate, "After all, a
book is the only immortality." And surely
the reader is impressed with the way in
which a woman's genius, even if not of
the very highest order, may retain its hold
after her death, on seeing the late statements
of Mr. Routledge, the great publisher of
cheap books in England, as to the continued
demand for Mrs. Heman's poetry. In the last
generation the pure and melodious muse of
this lady had great reputation; her American
editor was Professor Andrews Norton, father
of the present Professor Charles Eliot Norton,
and one of the most cultivated critics of his
day; and it appears from the late memoirs of
Garrison that her verses were long the favorite
food of that strong and heroic mind. Yet it
has been the custom to speak of her popularity
as a thing of the past. Now comes Mr. Rout-
ledge and gives the figures as to his sales of
the different poets in the year ending June 12,
1885. First comes Longfellow, with the ex-
traordinary sale of 6,000 copies; then we drop
to Scott, with 3,170; Shakespeare, 2,700; Byron,
2,380; Moore, 2,276; Burns, 2,250. To these
succeeds Mrs. Hemans, with a sale of 1,900
copies; Milton falling short of her by 50, and
no one else showing much more than half that
demand. Hood had 980 purchasers, Cowper
800, and all others less; Shelley had 500, and
Keats but 40. Of course this is hardly an ap-
proximate estimate of the comparative popu-
larity of these poets, since much would depend,
for instance, on the multiplicity or value of
rival editions; but it proves in a general way
that Mrs. Hemans holds her own, in point of
readers, fifty years after her death. What
other form of influence for man or woman
equals this?
	Yet there may be other modes of action.
That of Florence Nightingale for instance,
modestly vindicating a woman's foresight
against the dulness and red tape of a whole
War Department; and returning from the most
superb career of public service that ever
woman had, with ruined health, but with such
universal love and reverence from the Crimean
army that a statue would have been erected to
her by a penny subscription had she not re-
fused it. That of Clara Barton or Dorothea
Dix, or Mary Livermore, or Jean Lander, or
Mother Brickerdyke, in our own civil war.
That of many a worker in the Associated
Charities of our large cities; or of those special
organizations which were almost always carried
on, thirty years ago, under the official leader-
ship and treasurership of men, but which have
been steadily falling, more and more, during
that period, into the hands of women. That
of many a woman of society, so called, who
recognizes in "society" itself a sphere for con-
scientious duty—so that the tone of a whole
town or city may sometimes be said to be kept
up or let down according as the leading "so-
ciety woman" is a person of character or a
doll. That of many a woman in some log
cabin on the frontier, whose "society" consists
in a dozen children of her own and perhaps
two or three more taken in from charity; the
woman who, nameless and noteless, maintains
that average quality among our American peo-
ple that can always be relied upon to send
from obsecurity a Lincoln or a Grant in time
of imminent need. Beyond all these, perhaps,
in total influence, ranks the great army of
women teachers, spreading their unseen and
daily labors through every school district from
Cape Cod to the Golden Gate; smoothing the
waste places, equalizing all our civilization,
doing the most for the poorest; and again, in

[Column 2]

the upper regions of education, rising into the
work of such missionaries of the highest train-
ing as Mary Lyon in the past, or Alice Free-
man in the present. Compared with these, how
petty seem the little struggles for position and
etiquette! In what lingering childishness does
the most exalted womanhood of Europe seem
still to be involved when we read in the tele-
graphic headings, "Great indignation of Queen
Victoria," and find that this excitement re-
lates, not to the tremendous Irish problem and
the threatened dismemberment of her empire,
but to the hesitation of certain courts of
Europe to accord to Prince Henry Something-
or-other, her latest son-in-law, the title of "His
Royal Highness!"—T. W. H., in Harper's
Bazar.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]

THE CARY FAMILY.

THE POET SISTERS.

	No names are better known to the school
children of Cincinnati perhaps than the names
—Alice and Phoebe Cary; yet it may be that
many of the children do not realize the fact
that the two sisters who wrote so much of the
poetry read and recited in the schools were
born and grew up to girlhood right near Cin-
cinnati—within three miles, perhaps, of the
city's present limits. The "brown house low
and small" in which they were born is gone to
decay, and only a sunken place in the ground
marks the spot where it stood. The "fruitful
apple and cherry trees which grew so
close they nearly pushed their thrifty boughs
through the attic window and shook their
fruit upon the mossy roof; the luxriant sweet-
brier which grew beneath the window, 
the small garden with its two straight rows of 
currant bushes and bed of vegetables on either
side, the rose bushes and thriving peach tree, 
shading a row of beehives, and its withered
mate suuporting a fragrant hop vine" described
by Phoebe, are all gone; as is also the "path
of blue flag-stones which led to a well of cool, 
unfailing water." The well is still there, but
unused, and covered with rails and boards;
cedar trees and bushes have grown up all
around the well, as if to protect it.
	The old "ample, weather-beaten barn, the
busy haunt of the restless swallows and quiet, 
comfortable doves, and in all seasons the
never-failing resort for children," has left no
trace of itself. But the oak tree which, Phoebe
said, "had been kindly spared from the forest
when the clearing was made for the house," in
1814 or '15, still "throws its thick, cool shad-
ows over the road."
	Alice Cary was born on the 26th day of
April, 1820. Her sister, Phoebe, was born
September 4, 1824. In early life their parents
were very poor and the sisters had to do their
reading, studying and writing at night, after
the day's work was done. The country was
new, no railroads, telegraphs, telephones, 
libraries, to aid them. But few books and
papers found their way to them, and the
schools were poor, and generally only kept
during the winter, when the road (a mile and
a quarter long) to the school house was rough
and muddy. This school-house—a little one-
roomed brick building—stood until a few
years ago, and the sycamore tree which Alice
planted, near by, still grows.
	The Carys built a new dwelling house in
1832, in which, as Alice said, "the faimily ex-
pected to see the beginning of better times, 
but saw nothing but trouble and sorrow." This
house still stands, and is occupied by Warren
Cary, a brother—a hale, kind man of fifty-
nine years, who ever seems willing and glad
to show to visitors the places of interest made
sacred by the verses of his two gifted sisters. 
	Another brother, Asa, the constant com-
panion and playmate of Phoebe in childhood


[Column 2]

days, lives across the road (Hamilton Pike)
from the old home. Alice and Phoebe left
the childhood home and went to New York in
1850 and began their literery life in earnest
papers all over the country had already con-
tained fragments of poetry from their pens, 
but the first novel written by Alice, entitled, 
"Hager, a Story of To-day," appeared in the
Cincinnati Commercial in 1851-2. After
that the Cary sisters soon became well-known
writers, and their home was the favorite re-
sort of the literati.
	Both sisters died in 1871, and are buried side
by side in Greenwood Cemetery, N. Y.
	A Mr. Alexander Swift, who had in early
life married a sister, Susan Cary, and after
her death wedded yet another sister, Almina
Cary, started, a scheme a few years ago to fur-
nish the old home on College Hill with all the
rich and tasteful furniture which adorned the
New York home, and keep it ever as a memo-
rial place. He bought the "Cary place" for
that purpose, but the furniture belonged to his
daughter Alice, "Little Alice," who had mar-
ried a Mr Clymer. Mr. Clymer and his
father-in-law had a falling out, and he refused
to give up the furniture, and has it with him
in Pensylvania, saving it for his children, he
says.
	Many persons from all over the country,
especially from New York, visit the "Cary
place," and the verses of the two gifted
daughters of Ohio are sung in the churches,
recited in the schools by old and young.
	In the College Hill Cary graveyard, not
far away and also close to the pike lies buriedChristopher Cary, the Grandfather, who was
born in Windsor. Ct., 1763; died in Cincinnati,
February 6, 1837.  There are buried there,
too, several revolutionary soldiers; one, a
Henry Deats, was a soldier of 1776; died
August 20, 1843, in the nineteenth year of his
age.  Another, James Keniston, who served
five years' active service in the war for freedom
the tombstone says.  Many of the Carys sleep
here, but the immediate relatives, except the
grandfather, have been removed to Spring
Grove.—Commercial Gazette,
	E. S. D.


PICKING OUT GOOD ORANGES.

	To very many an orange is an orange, the
only variation distinguishable being in size
and corresponding price, while those who
know the difference between "Florida," "Se-
ville," and "Messina" oranges are considered
experts. The "Florida catechism" tells us
better than that. We learn from it that there
are "over thirty" varieties of sweet oranges,
not to mention the "natural stock," which is
a larger and handsomer fruit than the sweet
orange, and is excellent for orangeade and
marmalade, but, being very sour is seldom
shipped North. The medium sizes are apt to
be the choicest, and "probably the very sweet-
est orange that is marketed is the rusty coated
and rather ill-looking orange, which might be
considered inferior by an amateur." Further-
more, "the way to detect oranges is to 'heft'
them in your hands; pick out the thin skined
heavy fruit, and you are all right." The light-
weight fruit is apt to be juiceless, a condition
caused either by a slight freezing while on the
tree, or, more probably, by the poverty of the
soil in which it grew.
	All this applies to the sweet oranges. The
"kid glove" oranges are grown in Florida
from two stocks brought, respectively, from
China and Tanglers. Hence they are called
Mandarin and Tangerine oranges. Both are
small; the skin is loose and easily removed, and
the sections fall apart so readily that a lady
can eat without soiling her gloves; hence the
name.
	Some other interesting bits of information


may be picked out from this "Catechism."  It
is not generally known, for instance, that "an
orange that is entirely dead ripe in December
will hang on the tree until March, and is
ready at any time to be picked and shipped;" 
while so far from deteriorating, "the longer
they hang on the trees the sweeter they grow,"
and Florida oranges, purchased in February
and March, are therefore apt to be better
than those procured early in the season.  Again
the notion that to know what an orange is
really like, one must go where the oranges
grow appears to be a popular fallacy, as wel
are told that "the orange picked from the tree
is no riper or better than the orange on the
fruit stall in the North."—Philadelphia En-
quirer.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


HOME DECORATIONS.

	Home-Made Hanging Baskets.—Take an old
tin two-quart basin; paint it inside so that it
will not rust; cover the outside with putty. 
Split peach stones and stick the parts into the
putty; after the putty hardens, varnish the
outside.  Fill the basket with earth, and plant
in it such vines or flowers as fancy may dic-
tate. 
	Fans.—Palm leaf fans are covered with silk
or cretonne, and have a loose piece of the same
material attached so as to form a bag.  There
are pompons around the edge at regular dis-
tances.  They are hung against the wall, 
handle upward, and serve as receptacles for
odds and ends.  The outsides are painted;
gilded, or covered with silk or satin, fitting 
tightly. 
	Flowers at Table.—Their beauty and frag-
rance add not a little to the pleasure of the
table hour.  A large, fragrant bouquet takes the
place of an extra dish.  And nothing is so
cheap as flowers.  During a considerable part
of the year they cost absolutely nothing.  It is 
the sweetest pleasure to grow them.  And it is
surprising to find how cheaply a small con-
servatory may be managed.


Mrs. Bancroft, the wife of the historian, is
said to be preparing a volume of her reminis-
cences and experiences.  As she was a belle at
Washington sixty years ago, and has seen
many of the best people and places at home
and abroad, she has the materials for an un-
commonly interesting book. 
	Mlle. Benoit, a young vendean lady who
lately took her medical degree, is now ap-
pointed medical examiner of girls throughout
the municipal schools of Paris.  It is the busi-
ness of Mlle. Benoit to see that girls are not
overworked, and that they get through their
studies under sanitary conditions. 
	The woman suffragists of Iowa have great
reason to hope, that the bill for municipal
suffrage will be carried by the L[-]gislature
this winter.  The governor has recommended
it.  The attorney-general supports: it.  The
prohibitory party has adopted it.  Leading,
papers in that S[-]ate advocate it.  The suffra-
gists petition and work for it.


HOUSEHOLD HINTS.

	For rough hands use lemon juice. 

	Tepid milk and water without soap will clean oil cloth.
	
Mince Meat Without Liquors.—Use three howls of meal,
six of apple, one of suet, one of raisins, one of citron, 
three of molasses, one of appl[-] jelly or any other sour
fruit juice, and spices. 

Rice Pudding Without Eggs.—Take two quarts of milk, 
from one and a half to two teacupfuls of rice, one cupful
of sugar, a cupful of raisins, a piece of butter the size of
an egg, and a little nutmeg.  Bake the pudding two hours.


Cologne Water.—Use oil of lavender one drachm, oil
of bergamot one drachm, oil of lemon two drachms, oil of
rosemary two drachms, fifty drops of tincture of musk,
eight drops of oil of cinnamon, eight drops of oil of cloves,
and one pint of alcohol.
	Fruit Cake. Two cupfuls of butter, three of sugar, and
three and one-half cupfuls flour, eight eggs, one pound of
raisins, one pound of citron, one teaspoonful of cloves, a
teaspoonful of cinnamon, one nutmeg or a little mace, and
one gill of brandy or milk.
	Tapioca Pudding.—Soak two-thirds of a cupful of
tapioca over night, in one quart of milk; then add three
beaten eggs, five tablespoonfuls of sugar, butter the size of
an egg, one whole lemon, grated; after baking for three
quarters of an hour, serve the noodles with milk or
cream.


IN MEMORIAM.

	Amanda M. Matticc, born December 4th, 1869, in
Porterville, Morgan Co., Utah. Died at Pima, Graham
Co., Arizona, November 5th, 1885, of typhoid malaria.
	She was a young lady of refined habits, ever willing to
do her duty in the Y. L. M. I. A., of which she was a
worthy member, also an affectionate and dutiful daughter.
		RHODA E. FOSTER, 
		LOUISA JOHOSON,
		PERMELIA A. RANSOME.

	Now her earthly toils are over,
		She is free from care and pain,
	Though we miss her, sadly miss her,
		Our loss is her eternal gain.

Pima, Jan. 31st, 1886.


OBITUARIES.

	Sister Betsy Crane, of Provo, Utah, departed this life on
the 29th of December, 1885. Deceased was born March
29th, 1830, at Andover, Hampshire, England, and emi-
grated to Utah in 1882. She was a faithful member and a
teacher in the Fourth Ward Relief Society, which, on her
demise, passed oppropriate resolutions.

	DIED, in Centerville Ward, Davis Co., Utah, January
4th, 1886, of consumption, Thirza Jane, the eldest and
dearly beloved daughter of Alwood and Ann Elizabeth
Brown, born at Walton, Derbyshire, England, September
13th, 1870. With her parents, came to Utah in 1872.
Modest and unassuming in her manners, she was truly be-
loved by all who knew her. Though greatly afflicted from
her infancy, she bore all without a murmur. She died in
full faith of the Gospel, and in the hope of a glorious
resurrection. The Primary Association passed resolutions
of respect.
		OLIVE CLEAVELAND, Secretary.

	DIED, at Richfield, Severe Co., on the 5th of December,
Augusta J. Westman, eldest daughter of Peter E. and
Johanna M. Westman, aged fourteen years, eight months
and twenty-four days. She was on extraordinary girl of
her age. Her greatest ambition was to gain knowledge
and do good to others She acted as a leading member in
the Primary Association since its organization, and was
Assistant Secretary. In the Y. L. M. I. A. and Sunday
School she was a most energetic member, as well as Secre-
ary of the Silk Association. Resolutions were adopted,
expressing, in the highest terms, the worth in which de-
ceased was held by her associates.	M. A.
